review:
I installed CS2 thinking I’d play casually and have fun.

Massive mistake.

Now I spend my evenings getting Ferrari peeked by a guy named “中华小当家” while my teammates debate politics, food, and their entire life story instead of playing the game.

The matchmaking feels like a social experiment.

One match:
I’m dropping 30 kills and feeling like prime s1mple.

Next match:
I lose a duel against someone moving like an NPC with 200 ping.

And somehow… the second someone says:
“one more?”

I instantly queue again like I have the memory span of a goldfish.

At this point, I’m convinced CS2 isn’t actually a video game.
It’s a psychological endurance test created by Valve to measure how much suffering the human mind can tolerate before snapping.

⭐ 3/10 — Would still get one-tapped again.

review:
Counter-Strike 2 has become a deeply alienating experience for the average solo player. The matchmaking system feels completely broken, frequently dropping English speakers into lobbies where communication is nonexistent and regional barriers are ignored. It creates a massive disadvantage in a game built entirely on coordination. Beyond the language gap, there is a toxic "gatekeeping" culture where pre-made groups treat solo players like uninvited guests, acting with an aggressive sense of ownership over every match.

The frustration is doubled by a total lack of accountability for trolls and smurfers. Because these players often queue in groups, the kick system is essentially locked, leaving you trapped in a ruined match with no way to fix the situation. At this point, the game has shifted from a tactical shooter to a weekly grind for drops. The only real incentive to keep clicking "Play" is the slim chance of landing an expensive case or skin to fund a different game on Steam. Valve has created a beautiful engine, but the actual social environment is a hostile landscape that only feels rewarding when the weekly loot finally hits your inventory.

review:
CS2. Обзор от того, кто уже смирился

Графон:
Всё красиво, ♥♥♥♥. Дымы теперь как настоящие, тени сочные, скины блестят. Только вот оптимизация — это отдельный вид пытки. На видосе у блогера 500 фпс, у меня на мощном пк — просадки до 30. Особенно на Инферно, карта красивая, но играется так, будто я на пекарне. Зато смотреть на обновлённые модели можно вечно... пока не начнется ♥♥♥♥♥♥.

Геймплей (он же "где мои попадания?"):
Стрельба изменилась, и не в лучшую сторону. Спрей из АК — это теперь лотерея. Вроде целишься в голову, а пули уходят в молоко. Сабтик обещали, что будет чисто, но на деле разница в пинге чувствуется так же, как и в GO. А ещё эти баги: пытаешься запрыгнуть на ящик, а моделька застревает. Или бросаешь дым, а он летит непонятно куда. И как в таком состоянии играть серьёзно?

Премьер-режим (ад для одиночек):
Отдельная песня. Рейтинг работает через жопу: за победу дают +100, за поражение с читерами снимают -400. А поиск игры... Ты сидишь, выбираешь карты, жмёшь старт, и ♥♥♥♥♥. Очередь зависает, потом ошибка перед баном карт, потом тебя выкидывает. А если найдет, то команда из пати против тебя одного и четырех раков, которые не знают, где кнопка "W".

Читеры (главная боль):
Их ♥♥♥♥♥. Серьёзно. В топе лидеров сидят аккаунты со 100% винрейтом. VAC Live, который должен был всех наказывать, работает как сито. Спинботтеры, валлхаки, аимботы — весь джентльменский набор на месте. Иногда кажется, что разрабы Valve сами продают читы, чтобы мы не расслаблялись.

Итог:
CS2 — это как старая любовь. Ты её ненавидишь за то, как с тобой обошлись, но каждую пятницу возвращаешься, потому что альтернативы нет. Вальва выпустили сырой продукт, и вместо того, чтобы допилить античит и нормальные серверы, они фиксят наклейки и ломают игру с каждым патчем всё больше. Оценка: 3/5. Играю, матерясь, и вам не советую. До следующего обновления.

There is a secret that gives players no peace,
When your accurate shot goes into the void.

You aim for the head, the crosshair is like a magnet,
And you see how the enemy trembles from your bullet.
But here he stands, as if nothing happened,
And the blood on the screen is just an illusion.

"Register it, game!" you shout in your heart,
"My shot was accurate, I don't believe in words!"
But the server is silent, and the statistics lie,
And again resentment will grow in your soul.

You see how bullets fly through the enemy,
As if the walls are just your leg.
And it seems as if the world is a dream,
Where the rules of the game are just a strange law.

Easily one of the biggest downfalls in gaming history....

     The only comparison that can be made from the transition from CS:GO (legacy and prior) -------> CS2 (slop)
is like taking the Mona Lisa out of its display, setting it on fire, and then replacing it with some millennial modernist painting, and then trying to convince everyone it's better. 

     The most sad part about it is that, only the people who actually got to experience csgo (legacy) for what it was are fully able to comprehend the tragedy that has happened to this game. The real reason Valve removed csgo (legacy) from steam is because.....
     
     A.) They would have been review bombed and CS2 would have overwhelmingly negative reviews for CS2. By merging the game they avoided this and were able to keep the reviews from legacy which now sits at (very positive).

     B.) They would have lost almost there entire player base to csgo (legacy) because this game has been an absolute nightmare for 2 years straight and very very slightly gotten better. (every single other counter strike game is still listed on steam not hidden in dropdown menus.... however they are 3rd party as well. All of which still have active servers)

----->nobody wants any of this and half the people play in stretched res, 1440x1080 with graphics all the way down for frames. 

     Every server on this game has become a hacker-frenzy. Face-it has become the only option for the true competitive scene. Utter carelessness from valve on this whilst making billions from this game. They have truly given up. Premier is completely in the gutter.

review:
CS2: A Problem-Filled Experience

The game is problematic from top to bottom. AWP flick shots land behind where you're aiming. There's no optimization. I shoot the enemy — they don’t die; I shoot a wall — it registers as a headshot. A teammate who passed in front of me a second ago somehow still seems to be in front of me and takes a bullet to the head.

Cheating is another disaster. VACnet is absolutely insufficient. Deathmatch and other modes are unplayable due to bot accounts farming weekly rewards.

The subtick system is broken. It doesn’t work as we were told — tick intervals still exist and even affect how fast we can peek in-game. Errors that never occurred on 64-tick servers happen frequently on subtick servers.

The smoke bug that was fixed in CS:GO works again in CS2. Even at the Major, bullet registration and hitbox issues occurred on LAN. So it’s not just us — the problems are clearly visible even at the professional level.

And while all this is happening, Valve releasing new skins honestly feels like mockery. None of the issues we complained about for years in CS:GO have been fixed — in fact, they’ve only much worse in CS2.

Anyway, when’s the new skin collection dropping, Valve?

review:
Play anything else. Any other game. This game feels 20 years old like we've figured out how to do this better. The culture around this game is terrible with the slurs and the gambling. Even the sense of superiority of the players who subject themselves to this terrible experience. I feel like the world has been lying to me this game is not fun. The gunplay feels really awkward and there's no feedback as to whether you're hitting anything. There's no visual clarity, enemies blend into walls and are hiding in pixel angles where you can't see them anyway. Then if you have the gall to be a new player your teammate will get on the mic and start yelling at you, meanwhile a man with 2000 hours and an inventory worth more than your house has just headshot you again. Genuinely does anyone like this? Or have we just convinced ourselves this is good? Games are supposed to be fun. Every single thing in this game is frustrating, especially as a new player. I haven't even mentioned util yet. I respect the legacy of this game and understand that the Esport is entertaining, but the experience of loading into Dust 2 and being dinked as you cross mid on CT to hold B and then being flashed 4 times and naded till you're dead sucks. Finally taking a gun duel with a rifle and all your bullets hitting the wall behind the enemy when your crosshair is on them sucks. Being able to damage your teammates is a ludicrous decision for an online game. All the maps are CT sided, especially when you're new, so if you get T side first prepare for your whole team to be tilted and mentally checked out before you even get a chance to rack up some rounds. But don't worry cos some genius at valve decided that you shouldn't be allowed to surrender games, so you're stuck until you properly lose, in case you had any will to live left. Play. Anything. Else.

TLDR: We have new games that feel nice to play, 2/10 stop gambling.

review:
I came back to play Counter-Strike 2 after more than 10 years because I used to love playing Counter-Strike 1.6. I was excited to experience the series again and hoped the competitive spirit and fun memories would still be there. Unfortunately, my experience was extremely disappointing.

The biggest issue is the community. Almost every match was filled with toxic players, constant insults, griefing, and people trying to ruin the experience for others. On top of that, cheating feels far too common. Many matches felt unfair and frustrating, making it hard to enjoy the game or take competitive play seriously.

I tried to stick with it for around two months, hoping things would improve, but instead it became more stressful than enjoyable. In the end, I had to stop playing because the experience was simply more pain than fun.

I cannot recommend this game in its current state, especially to returning players who remember the older Counter-Strike days more fondly.

review:
The game used to be fun a long time ago. 
These days it's ridden with Russians, cheaters, bots, etc.

The Russians are everywhere and 99% of the time, they're extremely toxic and refuse to speak a word of English. Cheaters are near constantly present in any and all ranks, whether it be in premier or competitive. While the casual modes, namely deathmatch, is filled with bots farming the weekly drops. 

It's absurd, and it's as if Valve doesn't even seem to care as long as they're making money. I feel like most of these problems would've normally been solved or at least addressed, if they weren't still making money. But, as long as toxic people and cheaters buy skins, and farm bots drive Steam market sales, why address them? Maybe I'm wrong, and I would love another explanation, but unfortunately Valve pretty much never addresses the community directly, so I guess we'll never know. 

They also haven't produced any meaningful updates in years. There's so much content the community has been begging for for years now, including content that was already present in CS:GO, that still hasn't seen the light of day in CS2. 

Maybe I'm just bitter and nostalgic for the earlier days of Counter-Strike, but I am certain a lot of players feel this way, and I wish Valve would care.

But hey, the release of a barely touched or updated Cache sure did magically fix everything..

review: CS2 has become one of the worst competitive games lately because cheating is completely out of control. Almost every match feels ruined by obvious wallhackers, aim cheaters, or suspicious players who never seem to get banned. Valve keeps talking about anti-cheat improvements, but the experience for normal players just keeps getting worse. It’s frustrating to spend time trying to rank up or enjoy the game when so many matches feel unfair from the start. The matchmaking system also feels broken, and trust factor doesn’t seem to help anymore. A lot of longtime fans are losing interest because the game doesn’t feel competitive or fun when cheating is everywhere. CS2 has potential, but until Valve seriously fixes the cheating problem and improves matchmaking, many players will keep seeing it as a bad game instead of the great Counter-Strike experience it should be.

review:
Before we dive into this game review, here is a key to help -

[🟢] = 6/10+ (or more) ~ Good
[🔴] = 4/10≤ (or less) ~ Not So Good
[⚪] = 5/10  ~ It Is Alright



Price/Value [Is it worth it?] 💰 – 10/10 ~ 🟢
Game is 100% free to play, but you have to buy prime ($21 AUD) to play competetive. Prime is worth it tho, you get free weekly drops from getting xp.

Gameplay [How does it feel?] 🎮 – 6/10 ~ 🟢
Every gun has crazy recoil, you have to get used to it. Learning the way to play this on youtube is also a big help.

Campaign [What is the story like?] 📖 – 1/10 ~ 🔴
No story, but similer gameplay to Rainbow Six Seige.

Graphics [How does it look in game?]  🖥️ – 9/10 ~ 🟢
Plenty nice, considering you want to proritise fps. Has a vast range of options to change your game settings (res, fps, etc).

Multiplayer [Is their Multiplayer? & what is it like?] 🌐 – 9/10 ~ 🟢
Your either playing real people in a public game or bots in a private server if you choose. Server has little - no delay and feels responsive.

Performance [How does it run while playing?] ⚙️ – 8/10 ~ 🟢
Won't use all of your GPU if the graphics are set to moderate. A dedicated GPU is a must.

Downsides [Are there any negatives?] ❌ – 6/10 ~ 🟢
Aiming is hard and you have to stay still when shooting or learn the strats.

Overall [In the end...] ⭐ – 8/10 ~ 🟢
If you enjoy a fast paced fps multiplayer, then CS2 is for you. Also if you can handle the heat that chat puts on you then this is also for you.

~HAZZZA62

review:
After more than 20,000 hours across every era of Counter-Strike, from old LAN café nights in 1.6 to the tactical grind of Global Offensive and now Counter-Strike 2, I can honestly say CS2 feels like the most refined version of Counter-Strike ever made. What stands out immediately is how clean and responsive the game feels. Movement has weight without feeling sluggish, gunplay is razor sharp, and the subtick system makes every duel feel incredibly precise. People who haven’t invested thousands of hours into Counter-Strike may not notice the tiny improvements, but veterans absolutely do. Micro-adjustments, jiggle peeks, spray transfers, and reaction shots feel more consistent than they ever did in CS:GO. The new smoke mechanics alone completely changed the tactical depth of the game — being able to interact with smokes dynamically using HE grenades or gunfire adds a fresh layer of strategy that rewards creativity and high-level teamwork. It still has that unforgiving skill ceiling that made Counter-Strike legendary, but now it feels modernized without sacrificing the soul of the franchise.

What I appreciate most after spending so much time in the series is that CS2 understands what makes Counter-Strike timeless: simplicity mixed with nearly infinite mastery. There are no overloaded hero abilities, no gimmicky mechanics trying to chase trends, and no random chaos deciding rounds. Every win still comes down to mechanics, positioning, communication, utility usage, and mental discipline. Visually, the Source 2 upgrade is stunning without hurting readability. Maps like Dust II, Inferno, and Nuke feel familiar yet refreshed, preserving decades of muscle memory while improving visibility and atmosphere. Even after 20,000 hours, CS2 still creates moments no other competitive game can replicate — impossible clutches, perfectly timed executes, tense eco rounds, and that unmatched adrenaline from a 1v5 situation. It’s one of the few games where raw experience genuinely matters, and every hour invested continues to pay off. For longtime players, CS2 doesn’t just feel like a sequel — it feels like the definitive evolution of one of the greatest competitive games ever made.

review:
It's been a while since I've played this game, but I figured I'd finally leave a review for it since one's been pending for a while.

This game was honestly always a good game. It may have had a rough start due to bugs and performance issues, but the core storyline and gameplay, as well as the more interesting questlines you can follow, were always there. Then with all the QoL changes and the complete overhaul they did to the perk system, it became much more refined and enjoyable to play since you now had a variety of builds and perks you could experiment with. 

I think one of my favorite things about the game was the story and characters. Every character has an interesting and expressive personality, including your own as expressed through the voice acting (I personally prefer Male V's voice direction). Everyone feels fleshed out and like they actually have a growing relationship to you the more you interact with them. The main story itself feels very personal and high stakes, though some of that is admittedly ruined by the open world nature of the game since you can just dally along however long you want. As the meme goes, "Anything but meeting Hanako at Embers" lol. Even so, I think it manages to portray a nice drama about the meaningfulness of life, self-determination, and even hope in a world that seems determined to grind you down.

The environment is effective in portraying just how degenerate and trashy (literally, even) Night City really is, which helps in creating this sense of "contamination" and a desire to separate yourself and rise above it. 

Also, the music. The music in this game kicks ass, and I still listen to some of my favorite tracks on my Spotify every so often.

I'd say my only real complaint with the game is the lack of being able to clearly show yourself as cybernetically enhanced, such as by having clearly mechanical limbs or metallic embellishments like a Kano-style red eye. I was also never too fond of any of the romance options, but it was nice that I never felt forced to pursue any of them from what I can remember.

review:
This game is wide as an ocean, shallow as a puddle. It's good, but people keep overrating this game. The endings fit the settings theme and it's very cinematic. The shooting is mediocre, character creation is not really that engaging, the "choices" you make don't really matter all that much, on both a micro and macro level, and EVERY single mechanic is very surface level. Jack of all traits, master of none.

It's also just a god awful RPG. Like to the point that it being described as that feels like false advertising. There's literally no "roleplaying" in this game beyond picking dialogue options. The backgrounds hardly matter beyond how you meet your character's first friend. Shooting is boring, driving is awful, the cybernetics aren't that interesting of a system. (Deus Ex and even 'Cruelty Squad' do it better! Which is embarrassing for a game that was in development for this long.)

I've tried my best to care for the characters, but I kind of just don't. You hardly get to know each other, and you don't really "work" with another beyond the individual missions. Your "friends" are not plot relevant at all, beyond Panam for Act 1 and the finale. The "ending" of the game is probably what 'saves' this game at all, as it resonates so thematically with the nihilistic world that it almost convinces you the story was actually good, and not boring meaningless filler for 80% of it. 

You are so absurdly powerful in this game - both mechanically and storywise that it kind of makes all mission "planning" a bit boring and annoying. Why even do all of this if I can just kill everything that moves? Phantom Liberty is a little bit better about this but it's still not really enough.

The themes and story is so annoyingly nihilistic, and to make it worse it comes from a pretty decently sized studio with a huge budget behind it. Oh, I'm sure a corporation would be HAPPY to promote a game where capitalism is some magical all consuming force that enslaves the planet and cannot be stopped by literally any means possible. Solutions? To capitalism? Well the writer doesn't want you to think about that, they just want you to bask in how miserable everyone is! It's individualist slop that I can't tell is out of ignorance or out of malice for the world.

The anime just saved this game's ass insanely badly. It's THE "hype moments and aura" game. If you like that, play this game. If you want an actually good story with good gameplay??

Hope that maybe the sequel will be actually good.

review:
This game is a total dumpster fire. I cant believe how many reviews sing it any praises, beyond it looks good. Visuals are the only thing CP2077 has going for it. 
The world is shallow and derivative, with little to no reason to go anywhere or see anything in particular. Not counting the fact that when you are inspired to check something out, there is ZERO interactability. Very little to actually DO in night city, except vibe. 
The cutscenes are all drawn out, basically just Visual Novel style, except the characters are paper thin. Seriously, it's like the writers here don't even understand basic humanity, or how people interact together. 
Characters feel stiff and lifeless, without real human depth, just an edgy facade with a subtle implication of substance. Story pacing is all over the place.
 There's moments of intrigue, ruined by ♥♥♥♥♥♥ dialogue or some ubelievably thin character aspect; cool cars scene ruined by on the rails action, generally inconsistent action will pull you out of the scene over and over in a playthrough. Not to mention the driving feels terrible. Nothing like a cool looking veneer on a slippery brick to get around NC with. When you do get somewhere with 'something to do' you're basically just killing everyone on site for the plot. Guns are weak and clunky, melee feels inorganic and unrealistic, and hacking is just not that fun, not to mention they don't reward stealth play very much, if at all, thanks to enemies being omniscient or totally blind. 

My biggest gripe with the game is one I almost never see mentioned: Almost every single story line, quest story, side quest, some characters and locations are Straight up RIPOFFS, from ACTUAL GOOD media. They ripped the name from a whole genre, confusing newbies into the scene, trying to force themselves into the discussion around the genre, when CDPR haven't even really contributed in that realm; its very disingenuous, and speaks to their philosophy as a company. Go check out any foundational cyberpunk media, and see how many tropes CP2077 straight ripped off from actual good writers and creators. Everything voodoo/AI ghost in the machine/ sentient AI using peons to escape, etc. are total 1:1 rips from William Gibson's Neuromancer trilogy. 
The only reason this game wasn't a MASSIVE flop is because braindead CONSUMERS ate up Witcher 3, and cant be honest with themselves, that this game is no anywhere near that level. 
Honestly hate this game, and I will NOT purchase anything CDPR does in the future.

review:
I would argue that the opening of the game is maybe a little excessively vulgar. Maybe that's the point: desensitize everyone to this new world as quickly as possible so the banality of it at a point isn't shocking whatsoever. Regardless of whether that was fully intentional, I do think the gameplay of the opening, at least, is by far the weakest stretch of the game, even if it isn't particularly bad.

Nevertheless, the game looks beautiful, plays well, and has a solid enough story. Even if its themes and world as a whole are largely derivative of more original works (but, at the same time, it's difficult to do anything in a cyberpunk universe without taking from Neuromancer and Ghost in the Shell and the like), the execution is largely there. I will say that V isn't a particularly memorable protagonist (maybe that's a maleV thing, or maybe he hasn't grown on me like Commander Shepard did). However, the world feels well lived in, the quests feel largely comprehensive, and it's ultimately just plain fun to play.

review:
It's come a long way since release.  The graphics are great, better optimized, and the bugs are more quirky than game breaking.

The DLC Phantom Liberty really helps to flush the game out with content.  It can be a little bit of a lore dump at times, but once the world opens up you'll be clearing the map of points of interests and quests, progressing and growing your character.  

I've played with all of the talent trees and builds at this point and they're all fun and viable in their own ways.  You used to be able to re-work your attributes for in game currency, but now you can only do that once.  The game then only allows you to re-work your talents, but your attributes are fixed.  I find this to be a strange design choice, as it would be infinitely more fun to set your builds at your base (apartment) and experiment as you enjoy the story and events.  

The combat is fun.  You feel super powerful, but on the higher difficulties the enemies can drop you super fast.  The driving is also much improved from launch.  

If you want a first person action game with RPG progression systems and a dystopian story with a fully fleshed out open world, this is a must play.

review:
I honestly loved playing Cyberpunk 2077. Night City feels absolutely alive — every street, every neon sign, every random conversation makes the world feel real and immersive. It’s one of those games where you can just walk around for hours and still find something interesting.

What surprised me the most was how good the story is. I got genuinely attached to the characters, especially Johnny Silverhand. The game mixes action, emotional moments, and dark humor really well, and some of the side missions were honestly better than entire storylines in other games.

The gameplay is super fun too because you can approach things however you want. One minute I was sneaking around hacking cameras, the next I was running in with a katana like a complete maniac. The freedom to build your character your own way makes the experience feel personal.

And visually? The game is gorgeous. At night, with the rain, neon lights, and music playing in the background, it feels like you’re inside a sci-fi movie. The soundtrack deserves a lot of credit too because it perfectly matches the atmosphere.

The game had a rough launch, but after all the updates it has become something really special. Now it feels polished, immersive, and genuinely memorable. For me, Cyberpunk 2077 ended up being one of those games that stays in your head long after you finish it.

review:
You can never get enough of this world. Right off the bat, go experience the game.. just go and play and experience the miriad of stories and characters it hides inside this marvelously crafted world.

Storytelling is apt for the genre and the effort it puts into the choices and outcomes for the quests is breathtaking to say the least. They don't do it for just the main questline (if you can figure out which one is the main story by half way mark that is), they consistently do it for even side quests which is amazing. You may not have the emotional connect with the side quests as you might expect from a Witcher 3 fanatic but that's okay. In this world where the line blurs between humanity and machines and where emotion is a data chip, it is expected that you take the deaths in a monotonous tone and it doesn't affect us much, no matter how much depth the characters might have. But given that there are stories that hits you and you will not see it coming. To do that level of immersion in this hopeless city in itself is a great achievement.

Now coming to the world building. The world is a living breathing city. Each corner and alleys have something to tell. Each district has a unique style. Each have their faction and a history. And the people you meet there, well they add more to the world. And they entangle well with the missions where you have the choice to go which route you wanna go. I once tried climbing an impossible sky scraper just to avoid paying 50 eddies and guess what, I was greeted with the only scissor lift mechanism in the entire game. And to think they have an option to enter the building via the garden at the top floor is an icing on the cake. Brilliant game design, that I felt so satisfied with each different way I try to enter a building for the missions. I could give a 10 outta 10 just for that. But there is more, the abilities not only add to your combat prowess but also how you could potentially do the mission without much turmoil as it lets you interact with the buildings in 3 different ways atleast.

Combat was sluggish at the beginning or so I thought until I discovered how to equip the cyberwares. Oh boy then started the real combat. As of now, I haven't yet experienced two different playstyles. Netrunner plus SMG fun. The possibilities are many whether you go with a Katana with the reflex tree, or you go stealth with throwable knifes with a cool build. Or you could go guns ballistic with ricochets which was my initial build. Then later I switched to a melee build with blunt weapons and these finishers are the best in the game personally.

Just to finish it off. Gameplay time after first complete playthrough is 230 plus hours. No more said :)

review:
Cyberpunk boiled down to bones is an upgraded version of GTA, full stop. For quests, instead of doing yoga or goofing off at a port doing nothing for hours like in GTA, you will have the most insane side quests AND get paid for it. Just to give a glimpse into what that could mean - you pick up an AI pistol off a dead guy in a shady alley and it makes you kill 50 people with it while it talks to you, you drive a creepy clown to a specific burrito vending machine (found everywhere in the city btw) only for him to kill the people standing in front of it for no reason, you eat Chinese food from a stand with a Japanese man and he calls it ♥♥♥♥ to the chef’s face, you use a light machine gun to mow down a homeless camp while they charge you, you have a street fight with a guy and take away his car in front of his pregnant girlfriend (dw tho, it makes sense and he ends up better off), the list just goes on. 

How is the game though? Overall it runs amazing and have had no major issues. One crash an hour in or something when I blew up 8 cars at the same time by accident (dropped a grenade when I went to change weapons into a car pile up). Never had an issue again. The city is beautiful, the quests are awesome, the game takes way over 100 hours to complete, the city and surrounding badlands is huge map wise (yes, you get fast travel points everywhere), the cars are fun, the clothing is awesome, the choices during conversations will require a few playthroughs (just bought the game for my main account so haven't finished the nomad one yet), the physics make sense, and the save button is the most used thing you will ever use (very very useful for going back if you screw up a convo or fight). For the story, it is a must play. 

Anything wrong with it? Yeah, nothing major but "stuff happens" sometimes. You could be on a hill trying to spawn in your car after a quest and it may explode on another car (nothing happens it's just annoying because you need to respawn it - ps it is NOT common), a quest could get stuck if you go into a cutscene and you weren't standing in the place they told you to be in (just reload last save, takes 4 seconds), people speaking can sometimes overlap (NPC 1 finishing their sentence and NPC 2 starts talking on the last word or something), loot can get stuck on bodies that you can't dispose of or loot, that kind of stuff. Most importantly, does it impact the gameplay? 100% no, it just "happens" and you reload and move on. 

Why the pos recommendation? The story and character development is worth it, full stop. Once you start, you can't put the game down because you want to complete the 270+ missions they have available. There is a LOT to do lol. 

^ With all the above said, any negatives? Yeah, this game is way too into LGBT stuff. One character that you do street races with added in randomly that they were trans for 0 reason (you can't tell at all btw, knowing the fact added nothing to the gameplay) and then they suddenly had a personality shift to get revenge on their partner's killer mid race despite the dude dying fair and square. Made 0 sense and was forced. You could also talk about this girl that has the EXACT same romance lines as if you were a woman (because she's apparently gay) and you don't find that out until the last quest where she leaves the city. The lines were double dipped and the devs were lazy there 100%. As a straight male, she just shuts you down out of left field. Last major character was this cop you did quests with. The game gives you an option to start a gay relationship with the guy, keep him as a friend, or leave entirely all in the same conversation prompt. Does the game force you to be gay or bi or whatever? Def NOT, but you will have the "option" to if you wanted it. Wish there was a feature to turn on a "straight male" mode to avoid it altogether but I guess they want night city to truly feel degenerate and without morals. "No happy endings" right? It does not impact your gameplay, it just makes it slightly annoying to see. I am totally hyperfocusing on this despite it not even being 1% of the game because it could have been perfect with an option to let you avoid all that stuff. 

Right, enough said about that. Cyberpunk is worth every dollar and you should dive right into night city choom. Thanks for reading and God bless.

review:
We are all aware of this game's horrible launch state, but with 2.0, the Phantom Liberty DLC and subsequent updates, I believe CDPR has turned that launch disaster into what it was always meant to be, that being one of the greatest games in recent memory. Something I'd happily call this generation's New Vegas.

Pros:
- Incredible worldbuilding through lifepaths. I would recommend a new player starting with Nomad but Corpo is my favorite
- Well-written and complex characters that feel like actual people with everyone hiding at least something under the surface, no matter how insignificant they may be to the story
- Great story with a well-handled approach to many complex themes along with emotional hard-hitting moments (Especially Phantom Liberty)
- Incredible amounts of replayability, that can be owed to rewarding, deliberate and varied buildcrafting (especially on higher difficulties), satisfying gameplay, multiple endings, and of course, mod support (I have way more hours on the GOG version dw)
- Great soundtrack
- Fashion potential is off the charts, especially with mods

Cons (if I really have to nitpick):
- Some jank is still to be found if you look for it
- The open world sometimes feels a bit lifeless, but you'll be too busy doing gigs to care Lol
- The first ten levels or so are pretty slow to do, especially on harder difficulties (despite which I can still absolutely recommend them regardless)
- Some guns are straight up useless for 80% of players, which ends up diluting gunplay a lot more than it should

Despite some minor flaws, I'd still say this game has 100% earned its roses now.

so i have a love hate relationship with this game. its either the best bad game ive ever played or... the worst good game. let me start with some bad things that are really just my own personal opinion:
this is just saints row the third with a better story. it feels like it was designed to be 3rd person but youre locked to a first person view. why i say that is the movement sucks. the worst movement ive seen on this modern of an fps title. this feels like a ps2 or maybe ps3 game in its horrible movement and useless skill tree. the skill trees basically lend themselves to two play styles, stealthy tech play or tanky run and gun. thats it. youre going to end up op af at some point either way though. not very well thought trough. the game no matter what your previous choices are going to funnel you into a few end game scenarios which honestly are based on that choice at that time and dont really make sense to how you may have played the game previously. thats really frustrating because i got heavily invested in my characters story and the people around them to watch a lot that not matter anymore cause of my choice to do one thing vs another. these rather kind of mundane choices (i mean compared to the hundreds of potentially more impactful choices you made previously) create a shift in the other characters and yours.... like mf'r please, dont tell me my character now thinks a totally different way because of this one thing that was actually in line with the way i was already playing. really choice is an illusion in this game cause theres only a handful of preset endings. lots of empty space in the world where there is absolutely nothing.... nothing.  buggy as hell still to this day. crashes, poltergeists that attack you (some times its invisible cars, some time ive found random static objects that will hit you and knock you down), the gps and mission tracking suck and are broken af, most npcs are lifeless and when walking the streets it just feels dead, this whole games just feels rushed. even though theyve done a bunch of updates, it just feels incomplete. i played through all possible endings even the secret ones and then the game just stops. of course you can stil complete it then go back to your last check point and continue playing. but theres just the same side jobs youve done 1000 times and you even run out of those. theres no option to replay missions, just have to start from scratch with a new game and character. what this game needs is more content inside the world, more dynamic npcs, a new game+ mode where it increases in difficulty but you get to keep your endgame stats and skills, some sort of random encounter type missions, missions beyond the endgame, bug fixes, better endings that make more sense, better mission tracking etc etc etc.......

but this is why i got hooked: the story felt real... its some amalgamation of blade runner and fight club yet it brings you in and you just accept it as reality. then it puts this earth shattering revelation in your hands and your mindset does change. johnny silverhand is a part of me now and i him. judy, claire, viktor, jackie, reed, river, panam, mitch, theyre all a part of me too. and for awhile your choices seem like youre going somewhere, like youre making a difference. like theres hope. the game is going to steal that from you in a horrible narrative shift that was obviously just slop cause they couldnt figure out how to tie it all together in the end. anyway sorry back to the good stuff, REFUSED/SAMURAI.... im 40 and refused has been a big part of my life since i was a kid. the themes are so identifiable and i empathize with silverhand so much. though maybe a child in a mans body, he was taking action where others talked and sold out. down with the corpos. SAMURAI FOR LIFE.

but alas, im left with a sour taste in my mouth and nothing left to do in this game. it has ended, ive seen everyway it can end. no point to go back and play again cause even though there are choices i would have made differently, none of those earlier choices matter, we all get the exact same end game and the only choice that matters is right then in that moment. i hope they can swing a cyber punk 2 and that they bring V and johnny back together. i hope they take queues from games like fallout and incorporate a reputation system and give us more dynamic endgame outcomes. more lore and more things in those empty unused spaces of the game. more dynamic npcs....

final thoughts: buy it, play it, savor the journey cause it will end abruptly. its worth it, even for all of its flaws.

review: The first time I bought Cyberpunk 2077 it was when i originally received my Steam Deck, I booted the game up and made my character but the game didn't drag me in quite right, it felt almost like it was wrong or something. Come now years later I tried to play again and made different choices than I would have before, I was instantaneously drawn into the world and landscape of Cyberpunk 2077, the storytelling and philosophies threaded throughout each story beat are intoxicating. There is so much to be said about this game with how much has gone into each mission, the relation to how similar this fictional world is meant to be in parallel to us in real life, the character developments and breakdowns. Cyberpunk 2077 is a cinematic masterpiece with a beautifully in depth thought out and explored worldbuilding and atmosphere.

review:
[h1]Cyberpunk 2077: A game that everyone must try[/h1]

The graphics:

Nowadays a game like this is hard to find, it remains within a small select group of games that I and many others would consider near perfect. And here's the reasons why: Starting with the obvious, the graphics. Cyberpunk boasts some of the greatest looking visuals within gaming as a whole as it takes full usage of the neo-lit world. It truly immerses you into the position of V as you're put into this ginormous worst-case scenario world. It is also openly used as NVIDIAs golden goose for gaming visuals, as it's spoon fed the newest features such as higher frame gen and the exclusive path tracing which I am lucky enough to try. And it is nothing less than breath-taking. 

The story: 

CDPR is known for they deal with their stories, as primarily seen within how they adapted The Witcher. Similarly Cyberpunk 2077 is merely adding onto the Cyberpunk world created by Mike Pondsmith, and it does it more than enough justice. The story perfectly resonates with the main source material and is able to encapsulate you into it immediately. After the heist and with V's slowly deteriorating health it was able to make you feel true sympathy for both V and those around you. It makes you feel for characters and at times it really feels like you're sharing their misfortune. And with the decision-based story (albeit sometimes it doesn't make full use of it) it allows for you to carve your own path within Night City. 

Overall no game is without its flaws. For instance, the NPC depth could have definitely been further worked upon as sometimes the walking animations can feel out of place and their reactions uncanny and unrealistic. Furthermore, the bugs. Now I remember the horror movie that was Cyberpunk on release, and they did indeed build upon this game from the ground up. But sometimes I do find myself encountering a few bugs that really feel like they shouldn't still exist within the game's current state. 

Cyberpunk holds a special place within my heart as both the base game and DLC allow for CDPR to show the true extent to what they can do with games. I hope this review either convinces you to buy the game or at least makes you aware of Cyberpunk 2077's greatness.

review:
Cringe fest. Cutscene sim, npc interactions full of questionable dialogue options 

The writing’s lacking. The story’s slow paced. Contains excessive drawn out cutscenes & forced dialogue segments. Visuals are the only glue holding this mess together and even animations look stiff. I appreciate the aesthetic, but i feel like.. Cyberpunk is a whole genre. Creating a cyberpunk themed world isn’t enough to carry a relatively barebones, generic first-person rpg. I’ve tried to get into this game numerous times. It’s just not my cup of tea. I’m mid cutscene now and there are cars ramming into walls outside. The difference from launch does look night & day. But yeah i wouldn’t call it ‘fixed’ simply playable

It’s amazing how much time was sunk into development and the game still blows. Nothing new here, all the themes feel borrowed & somewhat familiar. Still doesn’t work 100% i genuinely do not understand the hype. The driving feels worse than GTA clones such as Sleeping Dogs. It also feels like they’re trying desperately to appeal to a younger audience. You’ve seen this done already. Even Stray captures the worldbuilding.. better?

Put it like this, i’d rather watch Blade Runner with a film buff that explains why the directors cut is the definitive edition over playing this game any day. And i’d rather not do either of those things

review:
Cyberpunk is a really good game. It is considered a reference on multiple fronts which combined result in a highly entertaining experience. If you get the chance, buy it and play it. 

Cyberpunk is an ambitious project in terms of scope and technical capabilities. Despite its rough start, it's undeniable this is one of the most good looking game there was when it came out, and the dimension of the project is not something that many studios would dare to tackle for an entirely new IP. I'm happy they did and we should give them credit that they took it to the end, to ensure its mostly failed launch was just a light stain on their track record.

This game was designed with realism in mind and it will pull everything out of your graphics card to make it happen. At the same time, it is still somehow flexible to be able to run on something as restricted as a steam deck. It's impressive. If you have the necessary hardware, your eyes will feast. It's a game from 2020 but still used as a tech demo reference in 2026. That's saying something. 

I feel the Cyberpunk can be best described as a linear game built on top of a massive (open?) world. Yes, you can take quests in mostly assorted order but you do feel they're all leading to the same outcome. Your decisions don't shape the world, you're just following the pre-destined path, but this path is highly entertaining. 

To be fair to the devs, open world games are tough. It's hard to get them right. Most just end up being too sprawling with little substance (looking at your Starfield) and struggle with pacing. In Cyberpunk, the devs decided to use this massive maps to sprinkle curated experiences all over the places: the quests, the gigs, the NYCP arrests, etc... All in all, making sure in every region, that there was at least something interesting happening there once.

It's not a game where the world on its own has the built-in dynamics to entertain you by itself, but CD PROJECT RED left plenty to do everywhere. I personally loved the gigs, secondary to the plot, because it provides that kind of experience you get with Hitman: "here's a constrained location and goal, there are multiple ways to fulfill the goal, go and have fun". The NYCP arrests were a bit more trash tier casual events for me. I didn't really connect with them and I failed to see their appeal. 

The world building and design this project did is amazing and they just deserve praise.

The weapon systems are interesting; the way gameplay changes based on the implants you decide to install on your character is peak. The implant and skill system is solid and built to ensure that only your imagination is the limit in deciding how you want to play this game: in your face bruiser, long range assassin, sneaky cyber-runner, a combination of the previous, etc. There are more combinations than the time you'll have to play them.  

Phantom Liberty is DLC done right. The DLC has its own review page but this doesn't subtract from the value it added to the experience, although you can't help but to feel that they are detached. The way it intertwines with the main story is superficial. To be fair, it would technically very hard to have it any other way.

Regarding music and soundtrack: I remember spending hours driving to the radios in GTA 3 and Vice City. That was no longer the case in Cyberpunk. I mostly kept the radio quiet and can't really pinpoint why. Maybe I just got old, the samples I got from the radio didn't really connect on the first listens and then I just ended up turning off the radio. Or maybe it's a sign that in general I didn't feel them appealing enough, despite the random sampling. I know there are at least 2 or 3 banger tracks, that I felt welt placed, some of them during key dialogs that really helped to shape the mood. In the places it really mattered, the score was there and provided the necessary lift. However the passive listening experience (radio) was not the best. Maybe it's just the reality of Night City of being very self-imposing with its piercing ads and obnoxious jingles.  

There was nothing I really disliked in this game. Only things that I would not mind the see changed into the next iteration of Cyberpunk 2077, if that ever ends up being a thing: 

1. V's personality was extremely bland. I picked a corpo path and the way V spoke just sounded often like a low education gangster. This dissonance between personality and background killed immersion.
2. I would like to see the design philosophy behinds the gigs more present even in the main quest. Right now everything is absolutely scripted. One way in, one way out. I feel "The Last Of Us" showed us there's is flexibility for having both. If you have level designers on your team that can develop maps like the gigs, I'm sure these can still flow into your main quest.
3. I felt sad the gigs never really tied properly with the world lore. The story is baked-in and its not reactive to decisions you took in some of these gigs. 


It's a great game. Be ready to commit some time to it :)

review:
I've been gameing for more than 30 years. And this game surely takes place in the list of the best I've ever played. Verty fruitful and exciting. Passed it 2 times, 40-50 hours of juice. Let me introduce prod/cons as I see them:
+ The plot, arcs, characters, dialogues, several intros and several endings, the style. Maybe times this game made me laugh and several cry. When I say "characters", I also mean their model, animations, voicing and so on. Including the character that you play for. The style of everything in the game is a 100% hit. Including advs, signboards and so on, my respect to the designers and story authors.
+ Action. There is a lot of action. Yes, this is real 3-D action game, where you can set the difficult challending enough for you. Shooting, fighting, moving, all the stuff. Driving from the first-player-view is also implemented quite well.
+ Mechanics. There are several ways to evolve and become more dangerous. I played 2 times and 2 totally different combat experience. And I know at least 2 more ways. The cyber-related options and mechanics are implemented in an interesting and well-played ways (on of my game completions done with cybernetics maxed).
+ The level/map. Yes, the city and its outskirts are rather huge, their design, road net, fast travel map - all looks and works quite fine. There are main story lines, additional story lines, separate missions and additional engagement, enough things to take care about.
+ Graphics. I'm not the most demanding player but I'd call this nice. Rather huge map with seamless traveling across by foot/car or fast-travel with short loading. City views, well-looking models during dialogs and so on.
+ UI/UX. Interface is good. I did just a pair of key remappings but the whole interface worked well for me, maybe some minor improvements could be done but really not that important.
- There are still some bugs. Non of them prevented me from progressing stories neither crashed the game so they are quite minor. Mostly related to some transport.
I heard the the amount of bugs was greater in the beginning. But now it is quite low.
So it definitely worths the price!

review:
TLDR: Having never played it before this, I don't have bad memories of a broken and unplayable game. Instead I was able to go in almost completely blind and enjoyed the game a lot.

The game actually surprised me quite a bit, as I was expecting a bland or shallow game (entirely due to residual internet disdain that I was exposed to), but in truth the story was great and had a lot of fun moments. The actual gameplay is really fun and more dynamic than something like Skyrim or Fallout, which are the closest games I've played to this sort of thing. Mixing fast movements with quickhacks and fun abilities makes this game really good in and out of combat. Katanas especially... those felt really fun to parry bullets with.

The driving is SO fun in this game, which is good because they love to do sidequests that involve a LOT of back and forth. Even playing on the standard difficulty I felt super overpowered for most of the game, which resulting in clearing enemy hideouts really quickly. That almost meant I spent even more time driving, which I was able to enjoy. It's especially fun when you save up enough money to get a car with tons of health and built-in machine guns, purely to cause mischief.

The game has some flaws here and there, but they're pretty minor gripes. Most of them involve slow elevators, and the fact that your character flashes you every time you change gear. Outside of that, I enjoyed the game enough that I immediately went and watched Cyberpunk Edgerunners after finishing this game, and was sad when there weren't any more quests to do. Highly recommend this if your computer has the specs to run it.

review:
To me, Cyberpunk is a modern iteration of some of the overall themes and tone of the original Fallout. Cyberpunk is a game about you against the world a true uphill battle where there is no possible way of winning. The world is built around you losing, and you know that. V knows that. But we keep fighting; we have to.

While Fallout 1 leans into a pulp vibe and focuses more on racial themes and the failed foundations of social Darwinism, Cyberpunk focuses on the crushing aspects of late-stage capitalism, where the sky is filled with ads and the only way to make a living is by getting yourself killed. The system is built to destroy you, to isolate you so what is your answer? How do you keep yourself alive? You build connections, you live your life, and you fall in love.

review:
Easily the best AAA game I played in the last ten years, and probably one of the best action RPG and open-world game too.

Didn't finish the main story yet, nor played the DLC, though the story and narration is solid. The choices feel interesting and impactful. 

The city really feel alive, dangerous, and overall represent well the ultra capitalist corporation-dominated dystopia its supposed to be. The universe is interesting with a lot of detail and fluff being present in the game.

Encountered very few bugs during my playthrough for now.

Playing a stealth build with some support netrunning is really fun, feel like HITMAN but with a giant open-world and plenty of interesting zone to explore different approach. For instance some areas of the cities offer a great environnement for outdoor long-range snipper shots, while other are more compact and enclosed, perfect for infiltration.
I like how the game use its setup to offer levels with verticality, and a really good amount of plausible physics, move and tools to make the most out of them.

On the negative side, the progression curve is a little weird, in particular in Very hard difficulty but not only. Basically, it's really hard at the beginning, but suddently after the 20 first levels everything feel quite easy and less challenging.
The voice acting and dialogue are quite inequal, the protagonist for instance has sometime quite unsufferable edgelord lines which does not really feel in-character or fitting the condition they are facing. They are sometimes way too phlegmatic for the stuff happening around them. Otherwise the most important NPC are way better, with strong identity, struggles, goals, development during the story and various way of dealing with what's happening. You can easily feel attached (or repulsed) to them.

Overall the tone remain quite edgy, sometimes boomer-esque, but that fit the Cyberpunk universe, which is so maximalist it becomes really goofy at some times, lightyears away from the very serious and eerie neo-noir aesthetic of Blade Runner or the first Deus Ex and its never ending socio-political schemes and conspirations. That is not a problem at all, just don't expect any profound political or metaphysical meditation from this game because it will not deliver that. Though, I think the setup (and the game !) is really good at exploring the unheimlich/unhomely aspect of cybernetics due to how common it is in the universe and the first-person perspective.

review:
i want to start by saying i LOVE this game. i mean this game has had an impact on my life and world view.
i platnumed it on playstation and the game was incredible. however i decided to get it on pc for modding. now i don't have an impressive pc by any stretch but the game seems to run fine the mods are the only thing that seems to cause crashing or lag spikes. even so it's totally worth it and this is the only game i have EVER bought full price that wasn't below 20 bucks. i'm very picky about that but for this game i let it slide. i'll run down the game here
story:8/10 the story of the game is great first time you experince it if you have no spoilers. it's packed full of action and emotion i laughed i cried and i got royally pissed at points of betrayal or stress. 
world 10/10 i am convinced there is not a single game on earth that rivals the world building of cyberpunk 2077. the comics the table top the ingame lore the slang and city and how the whole story fits is amazing to me.
gameplay 8/10 due to some things being absent and some technical  reasons i won't say it's the best ever but it is arguably extremely fun with enough replay value simply for the gameplay alone. from slowing down time and slicing through enemies to ging bezerk and throwing a dude at another dude to hacking into someone's brain to kill his little gang friends from a corner there is a lot to do in both combat and normal play. drive around, get drunk, kill some corpos and pet a cat named nibbles. this is enhanced with mods i have one where i can just blow all my money on black jack. it's a very fun game even vanilla.
graphics 8/10 10/10 [with mods] this game is stunning, even in vanilla the world is beautiful at the right times of day.
characters 10/10 i mean V, jackie, misty, jonny silver hand, rougue, goro, adam smasher, songbird all the characters are very memorible if one of these characters don't stick with you long after the game i am convinced you have no sense of personality or depth understanding. 
music 10/10 so about the emotinal stuff yeah this is abig part of it, the score for the game was always one of the best parts. from subtle details in the music telling a story to how amazing it sounds it is very impressive, the amount of depth to rebel path alone is actually insane,
 bugs/performance 6/10. okay talk about the bane of cyberpunk's existance. it made a huge comeback in 24 with pl and 2.0 but it still has a lot of issues. expect bugs. expect some crashing. this game is not perfect nor is any other.
driving 4/10 driving in this game is really annoying you either get a car that can't turn a corner or get a car that drives like it's on ice. really weird but it has gotten better over time and you can get mods to fix it. 
character customizing 5/10 VERY run of the mill, there's features that are useless like changing ♥♥♥♥ size but yet you can't edit V's body or have body cyberware. no third person either. fallout is way better here get a bunch of mods to edit your v.
to wrap it up this is a solid game i give it 8/10 and it's in my top 5. not alot of games impress me like this one does. it holds a special place in my heart despite it's flaws.
alos you can have sex with someone in a tank so there's that. get this game it's a must have for anyone who likes rpgs.

If you bounced off it at launch, this is the time to come back.

review:
TL;DR: For the majority of my time, the game was actively fighting against my playstyle. Both in terms of gameplay (locking me into dogtown for hours) and narrative (V constantly acting and speaking in ways that don't match my choices)


IMO, you either have a silent protagonist with player agency OR a narrated protagonist with a fixed story.
This game is a nice example of why mixing those doesn't work.
Player actions have a very VERY negligible effect on the main story. As V keeps acting and speaking in a way that only matches a specific 'canonical' character. It's very immersion breaking if you try to go off the path.

The sidequest were infinitely more emotionally engaging than the main story. As they were more condensed, had more likable characters and were less cluttered with "choices".

Then there's the DLC... which just turns the open-world off. It forces you through a multi-hour long 'call of duty style' linear campaign where you have to unironically escort the 'badass' president of america after crashlanding right infront of you.

Then having to defeat a mecha-boss while tag-teaming with the president... the game just lost me.


Maybe I'll try again in a few years, staying closer to the 'the intended narrative'

review:
Cyberpunk 2077 isn’t just a game.
And Edgerunners isn’t just an anime.

Both present a futuristic world full of chrome, violence, and massive corporations… but at its core, it reflects something very real: real life.

Today, people often burn themselves out chasing ambition, money, status, or validation.
In Night City, it happens through cyberware and implants.
In real life, it happens in quieter, less neon-lit ways—but it still destroys people just the same.

David Martinez wanted to prove his worth to the world.
V just wanted to survive in a system that was already stacked against them.
And honestly, how many people today are living versions of that same struggle?

Cyberpunk constantly reminds us that when ambition goes blind, it stops motivating and starts consuming you instead.
People stop living for themselves and start chasing something that may never fill the emptiness.

And that’s what makes this universe so special:
behind the neon lights, intense action, and the absolutely unhinged amount of chaos (and yes, NSFW energy) in Night City, there are real lessons about humanity, loneliness, love, trauma, and the price of trying to “win” in a broken world.

There are no perfect endings in Night City.
Just like there are rarely perfect endings in real life.

But people still love, still dream, and still fight anyway.

And maybe that’s the real message of Cyberpunk.

(Also, half the city definitely needs therapy… and the other half needs to log off for a bit.)

review:
Cyberpunk 2077 Review:-

After playing for around 16 hours, I can honestly say this game surprised me in a very good way.

When I started GTA 5, I mostly treated it like an open-world sandbox. I enjoyed driving, causing chaos, and exploring, but I was never deeply invested in the story. Cyberpunk 2077 feels completely different to me.

In Cyberpunk, I am not just treating it as an open-world game — I am treating it as an open-world story-based choice matters game. The storyline is genuinely great and emotional. Some moments actually hit hard emotionally, and the characters feel memorable.

What I love the most is that my choices actually matter. I feel like the story changes depending on what I choose, and that makes me think before making decisions. It makes me more attached to the world and characters.

The gameplay is also really fun:
✔ Cool cars and bikes to drive
✔ Great action (not too hard, not too easy — balanced difficulty)
✔ Amazing world design
✔ Freedom to do many things

But the biggest difference compared to GTA or many other open-world games for me is hacking.

I absolutely love that I can use intelligence and strategy instead of just shooting enemies. Hacking enemies, using quickhacks, scanning, and planning attacks makes combat feel unique and smart.

Honestly, after 16 hours, I understand why people love this game so much. It feels immersive, emotional, and gives me freedom while still making me care about the story.

Highly recommended if you love story-driven games where your choices matter.

review:
A good game, but a game that falls short on most of its original promises. 

[h3] Gameplay [/h3]

I’m pretty sure the original vision of the devs for the gameplay was to land somewhere between an action RPG and an immersive sim. Which could have been a cool concept if it worked out, unfortunately it really didn’t. 

For the immersive sim side of things it’s all super simplistic. Sure most of the time there are a couple of approaches to handle things. However “couple” is usually just two or three - either you go guns blazing, do stealth or pass a skill check. Skill checks are almost completely irrelevant, the game isn’t super hard even on hard so I’ve always had enough points to dump into some skill tree to unlock the route If I wanted to. And even if I hadn’t, there always was an alternative route quite clearly highlighted. I’ve never felt some consequences of my gameplay related choices and in every scenario it was visible how the devs wanted to accommodate every play style. As for stealth, its mechanics are rather rudimentary, NPCs are easy prey for the player and in general it doesn’t feel that good. Even the Phantom Liberty DLCs, which could have been stealth heavy, didn't make it fun.

As for combat it’s OK-ish. After the initial shock that headshots don’t do insta kills and absolutely ♥♥♥♥ iron sights that are close to unusable before upgrades I’ve quite enjoyed the flow. Between hacking, tossing grandes and a lot of running and gunning it was quite fun. However, even with some interesting augmentations it all got quite stale after some 30 hours. There really aren’t much of a difference between enemies, or at least they don’t matter when you are shooting at them so every encounter started to feel quite the same. And also by then I’ve mostly settled on my build and experimentation really wasn't possible - since perks affect chances of crit hits, aim accuracy and everything, whole weapon classes become unusable by mid game since they had to be neglected in the skill tree. Also as enemies scale to your level you A) don’t feel that much power, maybe expect against the most basic of gangsters and B) enemies are bulletsponges throughout the whole game. Lot of the encounters towards the game weren’t fun but just annoying. 

As for the role playing side, first there is the character customizations. There are 5 main skill trees all of them filled with many perks that usually are just some stat increase, granted not simple stat increases, but + 5 % crit rate for pistols is still just a stat increase. Cyberware works in a similar aspect so there obviously are ways to stack some stacks to create some ridiculous builds. However, chances are most common players would need to copy such builds from some guides which is boring. I felt that in games like idk Witcher 3 the skill system was much more legible, had much more impact and different builds were immediately distinguishable. The second part of role playing would be players' choices, of which there are many, and most amount to nothing. Sure there are a couple of endings, some characters can die, but it’s nothing compared to previous CDPR experiences. 
[h3] Worldbuilding [/h3]

The amount of detail in this game is ridiculous. Be it city streets, suburbia, badlands or insides of random apartment buildings, diners, hotels everything has been crafted with immense level of care. Combined with things like TV broadcasts, the Night City can be really immersive and feel alive. Even the factions that occupy the space are well written and their behaviour makes some level of sense. Granted, a lot of the work was done for CDPR as they were working with already established IP, but still they’ve managed to bring it to life really well. 

My only gripe in respect to worldbuilding is that CDPR really should have hired some technical advisors. Granted most players don’t have a degree in electronics engineering, but as a guy who has, some of things look really stupid. Sci-Fi smart guns, implants and things like this are ok. I can suspend my disbelief for it. But for things like idk parabolic antenna the physics is set and won’t change.

[h3] Story and Quests [/h3]

The story in general is a mess. Clearly ruined by rushed development and it will never be fixed. And the problems start almost immediately, when after a too short route specific start you are presented with an epic montage of budding with Jackie, a montage that would be fun to play, but not just a mp4 for the player to build attachment to him, before he is unceremoniously killed. 
It’s no surprise that things didn’t get much better after that. Like it’s not terrible and especially a lot of the side routes work quite well, but it could have been so much more. 

On the topic of side routes, a lot of them are really great. But the problem is they don’t plate that well with the main story. Especially when it comes to Johnny, his character was really erratic with how he acted in different side routes, not to mention the DLC. One time V and Johnny had a profound understanding for each other, only to start the next mission where suddenly every understanding was thrown out of the windows and just animosity remained. Johnny as a character doesn’t work at all because of this.  

However, in respect to Johnny/V the story would have never worked without some time limit mechanic. If nothing else the devs should have made it a toggle when creating a new game, or at least make it so you cannot complete every sidequests - with the game having a couple radically  different main routes each with a couple of endings. Most of your decisions already don’t amount to much so I would gladly take a more linear experience in exchange for the main story conflict to actually play a role in the story.

I’ve found sidequests to be really lacking. There are like two somewhat good side quests you don’t get a call for. And even those you get a call for by your fixer there really aren’t that many. And for some great ones you can easily get locked out of them by picking wrong choices, which isn’t a bad thing. But most side quests or gigs still follow the basic formula of go there, kill enemies, maybe retrieve something and that’s about it. It’s a shame given how well the world is crafted. 

[h3] Technical Aspects [/h3]

While I’ve had only a couple of crashes, I’ve encountered quite a large number of bugs during my 50 hours with the game. Only a couple required a reload - as they soflocked me, but way more “gamebreaking” were bugs in bosses behaviour. Three of the bosses in the main story got stuck in the air and let magazine after magazine be dumped into them. On one aspect this ruined their impact quite a bit - especially when it came to Adam Smasher. On the other hand given they were bullet sponge garbage that lasted twice as long as they should have I don’t really have a problem with it gameplay wise.

[h3] Conclusion [/h3]

Despite my critique for the first 30 hours the game was really good and Phantom Liberty was especially a standout. The game should have been in the oven for at least two more years and then maybe it could have been great. Cyberpunk we got is just kinda sad in this aspect. Still it’s worth playing just for the great setting, despite it being rather poorly utilized.

review:
Cyberpunk 2077 went from a game with huge potential to one of the best RPG experiences I’ve played. After 100+ hours I still keep finding new quests, characters, and details around Night City.

The atmosphere is incredible — the city feels alive day and night, the soundtrack is amazing, and the story actually made me care about the characters. The writing and side quests are on another level, especially with Johnny Silverhand and the major questlines.

Gameplay is also much better now. Combat feels smooth, builds are fun to experiment with, and the expansion takes everything even further. Whether you play stealth, hacking, blades, or full cyberpsycho mode, the game gives you freedom to enjoy it your way.

Night City is honestly one of the most immersive open worlds ever made.

Highly recommended.

review:
I spent more than 208 hours in the world of Cyberpunk 2077, and during that time I managed to earn the platinum trophy as well — I think that alone says a lot about how immersive and engaging this game is. Night City is incredibly detailed, alive, and atmospheric, always offering something new to discover, whether it’s a quest, a story, or an interesting character.
The story and characters are outstanding, especially the depth of both the main storyline and the side quests. It’s rare to find a game where you genuinely care about the characters and where your choices actually feel meaningful. The progression system and the variety of builds kept the gameplay and combat fun even after hundreds of hours.
The game has improved massively since launch, and today it delivers an amazing RPG experience. The graphics, soundtrack, and overall cyberpunk atmosphere are simply incredible. For me, getting the platinum trophy never felt like a chore — it felt like completing an unforgettable journey that I enjoyed every step of the way. I can highly recommend it to anyone who loves story-driven open-world games.

review:
This game is a solid 9.0/10 for me. I was expecting an almost Hollywood-level sugar-coated narrative you would see in generic action flicks. To my surprise, it made me really sad that V lives in a world with choices and trade-offs that come to a bitter end, simulating reality in that regard really well. The surrounding characters are also very well written and never stick out one bit in the plot at all, all their personalities resonate well with what they deal with or what they will deal with as the plot goes on. I couldn't get past completing more than two endings because I would just get grief. This game really captured what it's like living in a big city where everyone is a blip in a large population. It also captured what people always say about good stories in games that I've never seen in the usual genres i play until the point i felt empathetic with how everything transpires.

The gameplay is good, especially for an RPG. It has the most action-packed combat depth compared to the limited selection of RPGs I have played. There is some jank that isn't game-breaking, although it still requires some TLC. No, thanks to it being a rush job, I'll hedge my bets that needing to be a console release made it very hard to optimize as well. I heard from multiple people that CD Projekt Red cared enough about their product to fix it instead of shilling it to a community of fans that become both nostalgic and jaded over time because they have to fix it themselves to then make it playable like classic fallout games.

The graphics are good, nothing out of this world, especially in the case of the rendered models of the characters, they pale in comparison to the world around them. Where it does ramp it up is how the developers really captured the claustrophobic layout of cities, with buildings forming small bazaars on street levels. The buildings themselves had a lot of signs, pipes and other small details caked on them for that extra pop giving u the stimulation from city settings u would picture irl, and the detritus all around the city adds to the world-building really well. I would say it's the best modern city layout I've seen in an open world game yet, with the added flair of a futuristic cyber punk aesthetic.

review:
Cyberpunk's a good game for the most part; it's definitely fun. While it does have more than a few bugs, most of them are just immersion-breaking rather than game-breaking. Only once did I have to reload a save to fix a bug that wouldn't let me progress.
The game itself is just okay in my opinion. Combat is weird and is either trivialized with quickhacks or made very difficult if just relying on pure brute force. The crafting system isn't explained at all and I went about 10 hours without using it whatsoever. Even now I still haven't used it that much.
The story is the main driver of the game, with everything I make V say seems to matter in some way. It's refreshing to have an impact on the story, where there is no illusion of choice. 
Overall I recommend, just not as highly as other games.

review:
I skipped this game at launch after hearing about performance issues, and finally picked it up at the beginning of 2026.

Cyberpunk is actually one of the best games I've ever played. The city looks insanely beautiful all the time, like every street corner and building has got so much detail it honestly feels like it could be a real place and not just a game map. Sometimes I would just drive around for an hour because the lights and rain and music together felt so cool. CD PROJEKT RED is crazy good at world building. The story is also super well written and emotional. The characters feel real and you actually start caring about them after awhile. Especially V and Johnny, their conversations are really deep and makes you think about things differently. There’s so many moments that hit hard emotionally. Gameplay is fun too for anyone that enjoys buildcrafting but honestly the main reason this game stands out is because of the atmosphere and narrative. It feels more like art then just another open world game.

review:
I stayed away from this game for five years due to how bad the launch was, and after it being in my library for the past six months I decided to give it a shot.

This game is phenomenal. I have not felt this way about a game since New Vegas and I do not say that lightly. From start to end this game had me hooked, immersed and coming back for more. The gameplay loop is engaging and always adding a new spin to it, keeping the experience fresh. The world is immersive yet in a way that isn't merely a flashy spectacle that doesn't take away from the core gameplay. The characters are top notch and well written, so even if I don't agree or like what a character is doing I can completely understand the thought process and decisions they make.

If you were like me and steered clear of this game because of the launch and its horrible performance, I highly recommend you play this game, you won't regret it.
