review:
Please, if you can buy this game, i beg you to do it. 

I can’t put into words how good this game is. It is not perfect, the start was VERY rough, but in 2026, it is one of if not the best game you can play. Depends on what you like.

-RPG elements : Not the most fleshed out, after all you are playing as your V and not a completely made-up character. Still, they’re very, very good. Also it‘s important to mention that this decision opened many great possibilities in the game development that otherwise wouldn’t be possible.

-Combat : I would say a benchmark. The way you can customize your fighting style to what you want to do is ridiculous. Everyone can find their style.

-Visuals, graphics : THE benchmark. Demanding, of course, but in 2026, doable. And stunning. The game is extremely beautiful.

-The story, worldbuilding : This is the best part of the game by a longshot, and that’s saying something. The game treats you like a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ adult for once, and you go through so many difficult, nail-biting, decisions that genuinely make you question who you are as a person. Not only is it very entertaining and downright inspirational, but very believable as well. You genuinely feel like a part of the story that is happening, somewhere, not so far away. 

Overall, i have not played a better game in my life. And honestly? I’m pretty sure that if CDPR doesn’t knock it out the park with the Witcher 4 or project Orion, i’ll never will :)

review:
Cyberpunk 2077 is easily a 9/10 and is definitely in my top 5 favorite games.

The game has come a looong way since launch, speaking as someone who SOMEHOW played through the initial release on PS5. There's still plenty of.. quarks but not nearly as many game breaking bugs as before. More likely than not, it's actually one of my mods that breaks the game now.

The world is big but not massive. Everything is very densely packed together for the most part, and I think that works for the benefit of the game. There's a very solid variety of cars, and even some cyberware that makes traversal a lot of fun.

The story is really good, basically from start to finish. There's absolutely some areas that could've been fleshed out more but overall I think it's fine. The "Choose Your Path" and Jackie downfalls have been spoken about enough and are what I feel weigh down the start of the game the most, but beyond that I think the main story holds up perfectly well. There's ample side missions to take part in, a lot of which have self contained stories, some short, some a bit longer. There's rarely anything that's as simple as "go to point A, talk to NPC, go to Point B, kill some bad guys" that a lot of Open World RPG's/FPS games tend to lean on.

There's plenty of opportunity to make interesting builds if you pay attention to your weapons, cyberware, and skills. Although I tend to lean on mowing through people with A shotgun, Katana, and Rifle combo, playing around with the Netrunner and stealth side of the game is equally as fun and rewarding.

Visually, needless to say, the game looks great. I'm a glutton for pain so I choose to play with Path Tracing so my performance is.. questionable to say the least but without Path Tracing, whether on normal Ray Tracing or neither RT or PT, the game runs pretty well for modern standards (which is a low bar, but still). The game is very CPU dependent which is my primary bottleneck so results may vary.

Gunplay in the beginning feels kinda sluggish and sometimes unresponsive but within an hour, maybe 2, you can get enough skills/guns to vastly improve the feeling of combat. At surface level, combat is nothing crazy but if you mix in the Netrunner and various other abilities, you can add a lot of depth to the combat and how you approach missions.

And finally, Panam is the only romance option that's worth it and I will die on this hill.

There's so much to say about this game but most of my complaints are minor issues that can easily be looked past and likely won't ruin your overall experience. I highly recommend this game and look forward to the sequel.

review:
I discuss the story.





The story is very weak. You're motivated by 'becoming a legend' and then by just surviving. And what are you trying to survive? A completely contrived situation. The entire story was built around one dumb decision that no rational person would make (putting the chip in their own head). Johnny does nothing and is just a hitchhiker the whole game. The dialogue options are so limited, you have very little freedom with regard to narrative story decisions.

And that's a big deal too because this game makes you spend so much time on the story. Many hours of dialogue options that mostly lead to the same outcomes anyway and cutscenes and walking with super slow NPCs or being a passenger in their car. 

I do actually have fun with the gameplay when the game lets me play. But everything to do with this story, V's motivations or personality and my inability to make any real decisions in the story made me so bored. I finished the main storyline and wasn't a fan of any part of it. 

It also annoys me that you can't fully turn off music in this game. I don't like the music and wanted it off but it kept playing from multiple sources throughout the whole game.

review:
I know a lot of people love this game, but this game kind of blows and every aspect is really subpar.  Some individual things:
1. All the characters are pretty edgy and I didn't super like any of them except River
2. The entire open world is fairly pointless as the main quest line is fairly short and only brought me to a few places
3. The entire car and driving aspect is fairly pointless as there are no quests where I needed a great car, and I mostly just fast traveled around using the fast travel points.
4. I played on the hardest difficulty and most of the game was extremely easy.  Rarely upgraded my enhancements or added mods to my guns.  Just one-tapped enemies with takedowns or snipers.  Takedowns are completely overpowered and allowed me to insta-kill any enemy if I was behind them undetected, even crazy jacked up brutes.
5. Final boss hard counters stealth and long range builds, required re-watching a cutscene every time I died, and was so difficult that my choices were to either load a save before I started the final mission and do a lot of build tuning + spending >60 minutes replaying the final mission, or lower the difficulty.  I chose the latter.
6. Cutscenes involving cars are pretty bad.  Vehicles move in very unrealistic ways.  People in a car don't even bob their head as the car moves.  Really unpolished.
7. The AI is bad.  Like, ridiculously bad.  Super super simple.  So bad that in the final mission, when there are a ton of reinforcements coming, a good number of them just sat there beyond the final wall and just stood there as I slowly peeked and one shot each of them with a sniper.
8. Cyber enhancements are largely minor and don't really make a lot of difference for the build I did, as the AI is super bad.
9. The game almost feels like Borderlands with its tiered guns, bright colors for component loot, and damage pop-ups.
10. Story is whatever.  Lots and lots and lots and lots of waiting for characters to talk and to walk.  Holy moly so much waiting.

I'm sure there's more that I'm missing, but I'm tired of playing this game and tired of writing this review.  Overall, everything that's done in this game was just done better in other games.  GTA has a far better open world.  Deus Ex has far better gameplay and story.  Borderlands has far better loot.  Almost any game out there has better AI.  It's just really annoying to be playing such a mindlessly easy game, filled with waiting sections, and then get to a final mission (that's actually super quick if you ignore side content) that is again super easy but then get to a final boss that is ridiculously hard.  At no point in the game did I feel that I had experienced something new, something innovative, something polished, or something meaningful.  It's an arcade style game that tries hard to be taken seriously, but lacks the depth to do so.  Truly the definition of a game that is as wide as the ocean but as deep as a puddle.

review:
I`m nearly at the end of my first playthrough and will most certainly miss Night City when it`s all over. Doing every little piece of content (personal morality allowing) has been joyful and the Phantom Liberty DLC is a wonderful addition to the main game. 
For me, each plotline had just the right balance of drama and humour and kept me on my toes with the decision-making, trying to be a good(ish) person while doing bad things in a bad city. So along with this and the differing approaches to combat, the character I created made this very much MY Night City story.

[u] Bugs [/u]
I`ve experienced very few bugs. The main one was auto-drive not always taking me to the Dogtown entrance (which can be a little annoying if you don`t know to check on the way) and another was a single mission which consistently alerted everyone in the room to my presence, but only if I used stealth (a quick look online solved that). Other than that, I experienced one CTD which was caught by an autosave (losing no progress) and a couple of occurrences of one of the many npc`s being weird. For such a deep, vibrant city experience I`d call that a win.

review:
Hated this game at launch. Now I can't stop thinking about Night City.
Bought it day one in 2020, crashed every 20 minutes, refunded it, swore I'd never go back. Four years later I came back. Easily one of the best gaming decisions I've made.
Night City feels alive in a way no open world has for me. Not because it's massive but because it's dense. Every alley, rooftop, and abandoned room has something to say. Once I wandered into a random apartment with no quest marker, no objective, and found a heartbreaking story told entirely through text messages on a laptop someone left behind. No game has given me a moment like that before.
The characters are something else. V and Johnny Silverhand's dynamic has a depth I genuinely didn't expect going in. The side quests aren't filler either, some of them are more memorable than the main campaigns of other RPGs I've played.
Phantom Liberty is legitimately great. No regrets.
Honest criticism: crowd AI still feels thin. NPCs don't react dynamically enough when things go sideways around them. The city looks alive but the interactions beneath the surface are still shallow. And a lot of choices feel weighty in the moment but don't actually ripple outward into the world. For the sequel I'd love to see CDPR build something truly reactive, not just at major story beats but in the small everyday texture of living in that city. That's the version of Night City I want next.

review: I bought Cyberpunk at launch. It was an unfinished nightmare of bugs, unbalanced gameplay, and horrific instability. I was pretty sure I'd hate it forever but after coming back to it last year and beating it twice it's both one of the best fps games ever made and one of the best open world games ever made. The world feels densely packed like Sleeping Dogs while having the scope of something like Red Dead or Ghosts of Tsushima. The universe is absurdly well thought out and brought to life so vividly its hard to believe this is the mess I played years ago. Side quests are divided into jobs and gigs and while the gigs feel like a relic of late 2000s/early 2010s open world games the side jobs are almost all phenomenal and have a cinematic feel that many main stories in other games don't have. Gunplay and melee combat are snappy and satisfying, hacking is a blast and elevates fights in such a fun way, the driving isn't great but it's good enough I can play with a mouse and keyboard without wanting to grab a controller and through my PC through a wall. It's made better by the fact that there's some really fun vehicles, especially for people who are fans of the movies this is influenced by (yes of course I want to park the Akira bike next to the Blade Runner noodle shop). CD Projekt Red deserves all the grief they got for this game at launch but I've loved the story, characters, world, and gameplay that Cyberpunk has become now that it's. You know. A finished game.

review:
Cyberpunk 2077 is more than just a video game; it is a deep dive into a fascinating and complex world. Playing this game feels like stepping into a living, breathing city where every street has a history. Night City is built with incredible detail, and the game does an amazing job of showing how advanced technology changes everyday life.

One of the best parts of the game is the rich lore and the detailed in-game technology. The game explores how the physical world connects with advanced digital systems, which makes the setting feel very grounded and realistic. Learning about how the cyberware works and mapping out the history of the different corporations is incredibly engaging. The game puts a lot of effort into explaining the technical side of its universe, making it a joy to explore for anyone who appreciates well-thought-out digital and physical networks.

review:
7/10

Decent game. Not an RPG, mostly a visual novel with RPG-lite elements and no impactful dialogue options until the end of the game. The world is empty. Nothing to interact with besides merchants. Gameplay is decent; I enjoyed my sandevistan katana reflex/cool/tech build. But the gameplay loop gets repetitive. you spend 10 minutes killing people and then listen to NPCs drone on for 30 more minutes. The loot system is whatever, enemies all drop the same ♥♥♥♥.  Youll find one legendary weapon and use it for most of the game. I only looted stuff just for cyberware. The AI is dumb, hard mode only buff their damage. Alot of the side objectives are all the same copy-paste open world objectives (escort, kill, steal, listen to NPCs) but some of them actually had a decent story. I did enjoy a lot of the characters though and also leveling up the skill tree.

Not a bad game but it’s not what I was looking for. I really wanted a true Cyberpunk RPG experience.

review:
God I could write a novel on my mixed feelings on this one - approaching 6 years of 2077 here, good lorde.

Few games had me as hyped as Cyberpunk 2077. From its 2015 reveal to the delayed 2020 launch, I was completely locked in, in 2015 I basically discovered the cyberpunk genre through the world Mike Pondsmith created. It felt like the perfect middle ground between overdone fantasy and surface-level sci-fi.

This was the only game I have ever preordered. I played it at launch on PC and, despite bugs like T-posing, broken AI, and weird visual glitches, I still enjoyed my first run. A lot of the bigger issues felt fixable, and I was more excited for what the game could become. Things like deeper RPG systems, meaningful AI, gang dynamics, and customization all felt like they were coming later.

That is where the disappointment really sets in. Even now in 2026, after patches and Cyberpunk 2077: Phantom Liberty, it never became the RPG it was presented as. If you were (like me) expecting something closer to The Witcher but in a cyberpunk setting, this is not that.

And yet I have played through it six+ times.

Because what is here is still incredible. Night City is one of the most detailed and immersive worlds ever made. Just driving around, watching the skyline, and existing in that space is unmatched. It honestly feels like the scale and ambition of the world came at the cost of the systems meant to support it.

Amazing world, unforgettable vibe, but a missed RPG opportunity, and they clearly knew it from how they focused on all its positives in Phantom Liberty.

review:
341 hours.
4 full playthroughs.
57/57 achievements.
Base game + Phantom Liberty completed.

At some point this stopped being “playing a game” and started looking more like me paying rent in Night City.

I originally told myself I’d do one playthrough, see the story, maybe mess around with a few builds, and move on like a normal person.

Instead, 341 hours later, I’ve done 4 playthroughs, unlocked every achievement, and somehow kept finding reasons to come back. Different builds, different choices, different endings... and yes, convincing myself “this time I’m making different decisions” only to get emotionally attached to the same characters all over again.

Night City is one of the most immersive worlds I’ve ever experienced. The atmosphere, soundtrack, side quests, characters, and the insane amount of detail make it dangerously easy to lose track of time. “I’ll play for an hour” is one of the biggest lies I told myself during these 341 hours.

And then Phantom Liberty showed up and somehow made everything even better. The writing, missions, characters, and overall tension were incredible. It doesn’t feel like DLC—it feels mandatory.

Combat stayed fresh the entire time, whether I was sneaking around like a professional merc... or accidentally turning every mission into a small-scale war crime.

After 4 playthroughs, 341 hours, and 57/57 achievements, I can honestly say Cyberpunk 2077 went from “a game I wanted to try” to one of my all-time favorite gaming experiences.

10/10. Would ignore my sleep schedule for Night City again.

review:
Cyberpunk 2077 is probably one of my all-time favorite games. I 100% completed it on Xbox, and when it released on Mac, I bought it again and did another full playthrough. Across both platforms, I’ve spent around 500 hours in the game.

If you start breaking it down, there’s something to nitpick in almost every part of it. But CD Projekt RED has this kind of magic that makes the whole world just work, even if individual pieces aren’t perfect.

Despite its flaws, the game offers one of the most beautiful worlds I’ve ever seen. The art, combat (both melee and gunplay), music, sound design, quests, story, characters, and DLC — all of it came together to create one of the most memorable gaming experiences I’ve ever had.

I often get tired of games in the second half. Sometimes I drop them, sometimes I push through. But Cyberpunk was different. I actually delayed finishing it as much as I could, just to stay in this world a little longer.

A timeless masterpiece.

review:
[RU]
Cyberpunk 2077 — игра не «на века». Сомневаюсь, что через десяток лет кто-то сильно захочет в неё вернуться, но в то же время чувствуется, что авторы вложили в неё всего себя, и игрок получит массу положительных впечатлений. Другими словами, возникает ощущение, что игра сделана для игроков, от игроков — это сразу выделяется, и в ней приятно провести время. Сама по себе игра несложная: никогда и нигде не ставят настоящее испытание, но при этом предоставляют множество различных способов решения возникших ситуаций.
На фоне всего контента основная сюжетная линия, на мой взгляд, относительно короткая, однако здесь полно второстепенных заданий, наполненных продолжительностью, качеством и деталями. За этими историями интересно наблюдать: персонажи харизматичны, сюжеты продолжительные и детализированные, а также зачастую оставляют вопросы, о которых можно подумать. Причём эти вопросы касаются не только игры, но и в целом ситуации в мире, людей, неоднозначности решений и их последствий.
Временами встречаются баги, но я не наткнулся ни на один софтлок, и в большинстве случаев они даже слегка забавляют.
Однозначно рекомендую ознакомиться тем, кто хочет провести время в качественном RPG и лишний раз поразмышлять над вопросами, которые в ней возникают. Однако я не думаю, что через 10+ лет после выхода её будут называть культовой.

[EN]
Cyberpunk 2077 isn't a game "for the ages." I doubt many people will want to return to it in a decade, but at the same time, you can feel that the developers poured their hearts and souls into it, and players will get plenty of positive impressions. In other words, it feels like a game made by players, for players — that stands out right away, and it's genuinely enjoyable to spend time in. The game itself isn't overly difficult: it never throws real challenges at you, but it offers a ton of different ways to tackle emerging situations.
Against all the content, the main storyline seems relatively short in my opinion, but there are loads of side quests packed with length, quality, and detail. It's fascinating to follow these stories: the characters are charismatic, the plots are drawn-out and richly detailed, and they often leave you with questions to ponder. Moreover, these questions go beyond the game — touching on the state of the world, people, the ambiguity of choices, and their consequences.
Bugs pop up from time to time, but I didn't run into any softlocks, and in most cases, they're even mildly amusing.
I definitely recommend it to anyone who wants to dive into a quality RPG and mull over the questions it raises. That said, I don't think it'll be called a cult classic 10+ years after release.

review:
I'm glad I waited for so long to give this game a try because at this point it feels like a complete game and one great RPG to dive into. I've played all the previous CDPR games and you can feel the the through line that get's them to this point and some of the lessons they must have learned with adapting the Witcher. They have a fantastic ability for your character to be a living and breathing personality before you even start making choices that would sway them. V isn't as characterized as Geralt was, but they are a veteran of the world they are thrown into and it shows by the options you are given. 

Gameplay wise, they are focusing on a shooter/RPG hybrid where you have plenty of abilities to mix and match to make it the way you want to play. For myself, I did a stealth hacker and not sure if by default that is one of the more powerful ways to play the game, but wow you really start to wipe the floor soon. Beyond some set pieces, I realized early on that you can level very quickly and that sets you well above most enemies you will encounter. You will probably need to turn up the difficulty a bit to have that dangerous feeling they are intending their to be in the world. Cause by the end I'm hitting overdrive and forcing enemies to kill themselves before wiping out the remainder of enemies with a smart gun. f

As far as the story goes, its fantastic, but it has almost no build up unless you take the time to dive into the side missions. I would not rush the main story here as I think you will find that it is a pretty unsatisfactory trip without all the details you find in the side missions. The choices wouldn't have the weight they do without V having established the identity that he did. Though there are some filler quests in there so it might be best to play it by ear as well so you don't just get lost in the more boring quests. 

Finally, Phantom Liberty is probably the best part of this game and I hope that the story it tries to tell and it's themes are something you see again. It's gritty, serious, and full of conversations with hidden depth. If you buy this game, don't miss this DLC with it.

review:
Pros:
1. Good story(Main + side)
2. Very nice cyberpunk world
3. Fun build to play with (hacking is fun)
4. Good visual
5. Good voice acting

Cons:
1. The world looks alive but is not intractive
2. Driving and biking are wonky - just not fun
3. Too many upgrades available on cyberware
4. Limited meaningful choice in main story
5. Character customization is pointless as we only see the arms throughout the whole game in first-person
6. The quest comes in a long essay which is annoying, could be done more intractively, with shorter texts

Cyberpunk 2077 is basically an FPS wearing a cyberpunk skin.
The hacking and cyberware look deep, but in reality they’re just extras on top of gunplay. Night City looks amazing, but it feels like a fancy backdrop—NPCs aren’t interactive and the world barely reacts to you.
Character customization is almost pointless since you only see your arms the whole game.
Story is decent, side quests are actually the best part (Judy Alvarez storyline carries), but don’t expect meaningful choices.
Verdict: Fun game, but not a real cyberpunk RPG. If you don’t like FPS, skip it.

Resolution Scaling: DLSS - Quality
Texture Quality: High
Frame Gen: OFF
Ray Tracing: OFF
Crowd Density: Medium

All other advanced settings to high or max numerical value

I’m not that far in the game yet but so far cyberpunk is incredibly fun. I’m playing on the steam deck and though the frame rate might not be top of the line it still looks really good and I’m surprised I can get even a consistent 40 fps before any frame gen. Truly amazing. 

I would say if you’re playing on a computer with an outdated gpu and you really care about frame rate maybe hold off, but if you don’t care about a little bit of artifacting then you can use lossless scaling or the in built frame gen for better frame rates. One other criticism is if you like to play on keyboard and mouse, just know the driving on keyboard and mouse is about as bad as driving on keyboard and mouse is in any game; so not great. I like to keep a controller connected specifically for the parts where I’m driving and play keyboard and mouse anywhere else. Other than those criticisms, very good game solid 9/10.

review: As a big fan of the Witcher 3, I went into Cyberpunk 2077 with high expectations, but I can definitely say that those expectations were met. This game is a true work of art, the map is large and incredibly fun to explore, and Night City feels alive with beautiful graphics and atmosphere. I often found myself wandering around the city for fun, just to appreciate the scenery and check out the random tiny details the developers included that really makes everything come to life. The story is also excellent, I like the flawed personalities of the characters and the ethical dilemmas the game makes you consider, and the missions feature unique mechanics that I thought were a lot of fun. The combat system is also very flexible with many different weapon types and cyberware options for all sorts of different builds, and the game features a nice variety of enemies you can test your skills on. I also thought the soundtrack and radio songs were great, and appreciated the excellent voice acting in the quests. My only complaint about the game is that although most of the bugs were fixed since its initial release, I still encountered a few crashes and weird glitches throughout the gameplay, some of which were pretty funny. Overall, I'd give this game a 9.5/10, definitely an incredible and memorable experience!

review:
Look, I was ready to write off Cyberpunk 2077 after that 2020 trainwreck. But damn 100 hours later in 2026, Night City looks insane. Best visuals in any game, period.

The City Breathes
Neon everywhere. Holograms flickering. Rain turning streets into mirror mazes. Every alley feels hand-built, not copy-pasted. Night hits different lights bounce off puddles like it's real life.

Overdrive Mode Broke Graphics
Ray tracing on steroids. Every neon sign, car headlight, streetlamp full path tracing. Puddles reflect everything accurately. Shadows have actual depth. Interiors look like movie sets. It's VFX-level stuff in real time.

DLSS 4 Makes It Playable
Path tracing murders GPUs, but DLSS 4? Magic. 4K Overdrive at 100+ FPS on decent RTX cards. No more ghosting or blurry messes textures stay crisp even moving fast. RTX 5090 hits 9x performance boosts.

The Game Slaps Now
Update 2.0 fixed everything combat's buttery, story with Keanu hits hard, Phantom Liberty's spy thriller with Idris Elba is peak gaming.

Skip it? You're missing out. Night City's at its absolute best right now. RTX card? Turn on Overdrive. Trust me.

review:
This game is honestly one of the best examples of how a rough start doesn’t define the final stage.

At the beginning, things were honestly not great but the way the developers turned it around deserves real respect. Right now, it’s easily one of the top games.

The main story is very engaging, especially the opening with Jackie who is such a well-written and memorable character that really pulls you into the world. The side missions are just as good (sometimes even better) and almost all of them feel meaningful rather than like filler content.

Gameplay is where the game really shines. You get a lot of freedom and most missions can be approached in multiple ways, depending on your playstyle. It makes the whole experience feel dynamic and very rewarding.

And the DLC? Absolutely A M A Z I N G. One of the best expansions I’ve ever played, high quality, great story and it adds even more depth to the game.

This is a game that deserves a second chance from anyone who gave up on it early. It has grown into something truly special and I genuinely hope even more people give it a try!

Highly recommended 👍

review:
This game has come around in a way that's hard to quantify.  While it was utterly broken on launch, over the intervening years it's become one of the best RPGs ever made, and handily meets the high bar left by CDPR's previous masterpiece, The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt.

Even more impressive than the base game is the quality of the expansion, Phantom Liberty.  More than just adding content, it expands the already unmatched world of Night City, adds new characters, conversations, etc. but most of all, it's just got that sauce where you know you're engaging with something special.  That lift in quality has spilled over into the rest of the game, and now it not only feels feature-complete, but even a bit transcendent.  When you play this game, you don't feel like you're watching virtual puppets.  Those first-person conversations are so immersive, the biggest flaw is that you can't choose to respond however you please.

The gameplay experience is straight out of Deus Ex.  It's an immersive sim with deeper RPG elements than most games of the genre, and with more satisfying moment-to-moment combat than most as well.  The animations and sound design are stellar, and the visuals are simply among the best to ever be seen in a video game.

One aspect that deserves special praise is the music, which ends up conjuring the same transportive vibe as something like Grand Theft Auto, except instead of transporting you to Los Angeles in 2013, it transports you to a fictional futuristic city.  They do this by inviting tons of real-world artists to make music as in-world musicians, a creative challenge that leads to a suite of music that feels like you stepped into a Spotify playlist from an alternate timeline.  The effect is undeniable: the moment you're in a car driving through the city and you hear I Really Want to Stay at Your House, it feels like returning to a city you've been away from for a while.

I think that's the strangest great thing about this game: it could have easily been purely shock factor and spectacle, porn-adjacent slop with no artistic value.  And ironically the game dabbles in this regularly, but there's always a feeling of intent behind it.  The awkward, piercing moans of an orgasmic energy drink advertisement say more about the depravity of Night City than any monologue could.  In fact, with gore and nudity being so inescapable, it means that the moments of the game that really shine are those moments of deep, abiding humanity.  When a character opens up to you about her past, and lets you explore her home, you have a moment of realization that I've experienced numerous times in real life, "Oh thank God, I've found someone real in this place."  For a fictional video game to make me feel that way about characters made of polygons and pixels is perhaps the highest praise I could possibly give.

My, my, my—this game is truly stunning. Visually, it’s absolutely beautiful, and the storyline is far more emotionally impactful than you might expect. It never really gets boring, and I found myself consistently engaged; in fact, scratch that I am STILL engaged. Story-wise, this is easily one of the best—if not the best—games I’ve ever played. I highly recommend it, especially if you enjoy narrative-driven games.

Overall, the game feels like playing a more sophisticated version of GTA, with deeper storytelling and a more immersive world. That said, the police system isn’t nearly as advanced, which makes it pretty darn easy to escape during chases. Of course, this isn’t meant to be GTA, so it’s not a dealbreaker, but nonetheless, it would make the game feel more polished.

On the technical side, I’m running the game on a Mac with an M2 chip. It performs smoothly at around 30 frames per second, which makes it very playable. The only issue I’ve encountered is with mouse and keyboard input—there’s some input lag, which makes it less smooth and honestly not very enjoyable without a controller. Nice thing is though, with a controller comes haptic feedback ;)

review:
OK.
Cyberpunk 2077 was a tough one for me.
I started this game years ago, when it was still a buggy mess. I couldn't get into it. And not only because of the bugs. I couldn't get into the story. There was too much happening, too many characters introduced, too much cyber-lingo I didn't understand, until I was phasing out during the conversations. I gave up after the first 15-20 hours.

Came back to the game after Phantom Liberty, and decided to start a new character. It was a bit better, less bugs, and I had a better grasp of the world, story and characters. But it was still a bit much. I got lost with how much the game was throwing at me. I forgot who each person was, Got calls or messages from people I was supposed to know, but didn't remember, and couldn't give a s*** about. And I realized I wasn't enjoying the most important part of the experience: the world, the characters, the story. I was enjoying the combat, but going through the game just checking every icon on the map, not caring about anything or anyone.
I stopped, and uninstalled.

After two years without thinking about it, I stumbled upon the great Edgerunners show. And it pulled me back into this world. I decided to do things right this time. Installed the game, and modded it to improve the experience as much as possible with the "Welcome to Night City" mod list.
Started a new game, and went in with my mind set on really paying attention to the stories, not just completing tasks. And the game finally clicked for me.

This was a hard game to get into, specially since I am more of a casual player nowadays, being a dad and having two jobs. It took me 9 to 10 months to finish it, because I can't just do the main missions. I like seeing as much as possible, completing every fixer gig and side mission. The only things I didn't fully complete were the crimes, assaults, criminal activities and races.

Well, after so many hours, I was on the final stretch, and I couldn't stop. Stayed awake all night. I needed to see how it ended. And I am so glad I did it. It was rewarding, exhausting (in a good way). There are so many little things that make the world so authentic. Little call backs to previous events, characters I crossed paths with that show up, and recall what happened previously. It's fair to say, doing all those missions was worth it.

I really enjoyed some of the side content, and some deep, strong and uncomfortable topics the game touches on. I liked the moral ambiguities that are depicted. (Almost) nothing is ever black or white, and you're faced with some nerve racking decisions to make, many times not fully knowing the consequences of your actions. These are the things that stayed with me the most.

The ending was bitter sweet, but so fitting to this world. And I'm happy with the one I got. V's story ended on a sad but hopeful note, and open enough to interpretation that leaves room for me to imagine what comes next. Also, the people I cared about (mostly) got their stories wrapped up in a satisfying way, and I'm so happy I got to know them. 

Although I'm NOT PLAYING THIS GAME EVER AGAIN, or any other open world RPG in the near future, I couldn't be happier of having decided to come back and giving Cyberpunk this last chance. It finally did it for me.

Can I recommend this game? YES. ABSOLUTELY YES. Specially with a few mods to improve the experience.
It's not for everyone. It's very hard to get into, it demands too much of your time and energy, it has too many characters, too many stories, and they don't always work in sync with one another. It can get messy and confusing. The gameplay systems can also be a bit overwhelming, specially if you're not familiar with RPG mechanics.

But Night City is a place I'll never forget, and its people and stories will be in my mind for many, many years.

review:
A flawed masterpiece...

Pros:
- Graphics are gorgeous. Played without raytracing (didn't feel it added enough to warrant the drop in FPS), mix of high to very high otherwise, 4k around 100 fps throughout the whole game with one exception near the end where fps tanked in one scene (but it fixed itself before combat started).
- The gameplay is great with many ways to play. Gunplay is good, but melee feels very satisfying as well with different options. Netrunning is also a very valid option, turning you into a god towards the end if you want, and more fun than you might expect.
- Stories are interesting. The main one is pretty good, but especially many of the side quests. CDPR clearly is still very good at making things interesting. There are also some 'go here, shoot him, collect that' quests, but many are truly good stories.
- Acting is very good overall, and it was fun having Keanu around (not to say his character is especially likable, but he has depth and development).

Cons:
- BUGS! Oh my god, I can't even imagine how bad it must have been at launch since people say they fixed it up so bad. It's not like it's unplayable, but I've had many issues. Cars spawning upside down falling from the sky, camera stops following you while driving, getting hit in the street by invisible cars, getting ragdolled for no reason, motorcycle suddenly teleporting somewhere else the moment you get on... it goes on. I've not encountered many gamebreaking bugs, only had to load an old save once or twice (aside from quick save and quick load to fix things like the camera when driving), but it's still annoying.
- Driving is really mediocre. Speed is faked, you apparently only drive about half the speed it says (someone did the math), and it doesn't feel fast at all. At one point I tested, I was passing other cars going 240 km/h and it felt like there was maybe a 30 km/h difference between us. Slowed down, they were going 70... Cars also have pretty bad handling. The traffic also has an issue where in the distance it will show fake cars... then when you get closer they disappear and when you get closer still, other cars (less) will spawn in. It's very jarring and not handled well at all.
- The endings suuuuuuuuuck... I've seen a couple now, not all of them, but from looking online it doesn't seem like it gets much better. For some reason, CDPR seems to REALLY want to make sure you end on a low note. No happy ever after for V... The problem with this is, it makes everything feel futile. And that might not matter the first time, but this is a game made to be played many times. 
- The worst was (minor SPOILER) when I'm making all my decissions just to get back to my in game girlfriend, as we said we would get away and leave the city together once my problem was fixed... only to get a voicemail during the credits that she's left already and wishes me a nice life... WTAF?! I felt so cheated and it made no sense at all. It just felt like CDPR saying FU to my face. Well FU2.
- There are also annoying design decissions like how after every quest, the game automatically switches you to the main quest again. But the main quest is actually not that long and if you push too far in it, you get locked out of the rest of the game (and towards one of the unhappy endings). Also when you get a call about some other quest, the game switches you to that quest and you have to manually switch to what you were doing if you don't agree. There is a setting to have fixers not call you but only text, but I don't want to deny myself content just to fix this stupid design.
- Quests also lack proper descriptions. In your journal you only see Johnny's ramblings. That in itself is pretty cool, but with how many side quests there are, often I will not remember why I have a certain quest and Johnny does not really tell me. So then a cool story might still devolve into a fetch quest again because you have no idea what's going on.
- Cops are mostly still irrelevant... They are VERY easy to run from, they give up chasing you real fast. But, the one time I decided to try fighting them, already being a netrunning god, I died within a minute because the game escallates insanely fast. I was out in the desert, and infinite cops just spawned around me, until maxtaq showed up and just flatlined me (right through several cheat deaths, didn't stand a chance at all). This means there is zero reason to fight them, and zero reason to fear them when you don't actively engage them. So they're irrelevant, bad design.

All in all... yeah, it's still a great game and anyone that likes this type of game should at least give it a chance. I just think it's such a shame that they made certain decissions that actively make it worse than it could and should have been.

review:
When I first installed Cyberpunk 2077, I didn’t expect much since the last time I had seen footage of it was back in the beta. But once I actually started playing, it completely surprised me. Honestly, it’s one of the best games I’ve played so far.

I haven’t even finished the full story yet, and that says a lot. There are so many different paths you can take and what feels like an endless number of decisions to make. Every choice adds something new to the experience, which makes me want to keep playing and see how things could turn out differently.

Another thing I really like is how much freedom the game gives you. Not just in gameplay, but also through mods. The modding community opens up even more possibilities and lets you customize the game in creative ways, which makes the experience feel even more personal.

Overall, Cyberpunk 2077 ended up being way better than I expected. Between the choices, replayability, and creative freedom, it’s a game I’d definitely recommend to anyone.

review:
After the recent mega update, and really sitting down with this game for the first time...I can say this is one of the best single player games I've ever played, and one of the best games period. It's basically a first person GTA 6 before GTA 6 set in a real world inspired futuristic capitalist dystopia. That level of story and world building. With MUCH better combat and RPG mechanics, it's not even comparable.

The lore/setting and world building are incredible. It's set in a sort of dark semi-near future Tokyo/China inspired city state called Night City with crazy verticality, which is owned and controlled by several huge corporations and has apparently separated from what used to be the USA. 
It's often funny and scary at the same time. The commercials on various TVs you'll walk by remind me of "interdimensional cable" from Rick and Morty. They really hit home that corporations have taken over the world in 2077, but also have depictions of violence and sexualization that are taken to comical extremes. If you play it in front of your wife or GF she'll be like "WTF" at many points so play it after she goes to bed.

The number of ways you can build your character is awesome. 
You can have:
-small pp
-less small pp
-no pp
-crazy movement sliding teleporting double jumping assassin builds
-big pp melee builds (blunt, blades, and fists...and even blades from your fists lol)
-small pp sniper stealth builds
-close range throwing weapon ninja stealth builds
-hacker tree where you basically use slowmo V.A.T.S to place different negative effects on enemies
-driving is legit better than GTA 5, and many dedicated racing games. Really unexpected how good the physics are. I leave a controller plugged in and hot swap. Driving with wasd in any game makes me want to slap a baby.

I recommend playing on hard, it makes you actually need to use strategy and think about smart upgrades to survive.  Some of the boss battles were tough and rage inducing when you get 2 tapped lol. But it's worth it. You can do it. 

What's great about the difficulty settings in this game is that hard/very hard doesn't mean enemies become bullet sponges like the majority of other games (*cough* Borderlands) when they're lazy with it. At harder difficulties, the enemies are smarter and will flank and push you out of cover, shoot you though walls with tech weapons, quickhack you etc. Another thing is that guns all have unique bullet veolocity, tiers, upgrades etc. What is also something I've never seen in a game and most noticeable when your movement is upgraded is that you can dodge enemy shots by sliding, crouching, strafing, jumping, whatever. But not like, RNG in the game allowed you to not get hit based on whether you're behind cover or not. In slow-mo especially, you'll actually see the enemies shoot where you were or where they think you're going to go. There's even a tool tip that points this out.  It's awesome. Did not expect the combat to be so deep and rewarding of creativity.

There are 100 ways to tackle each fight/enemy. Full hacker build is like ez mode and feels like cheating (but still fun) if a person wanted to do that, even. Or you can turn yourself into a literal cyborg and chop people's legs off with 20 foot long metal wires that come out of your arms, shoot missiles out of your hands, or install "Gorilla Arms" and run around making your enemies' heads explode with robotic super punches. And that's just the "arms" slot. There are like 10 different body slots... 

11/10 game. One of the undisputable GOATS, and crazy with how supremely mid it was at launch.

review:
I remember being very excited for this game when it was first announced, only to end up avoiding like the plague after seeing all the disappointed reviews and videos of just how broken and unfinished it was on launch.
Fast forward a few years and several major patches later, the Phantom Liberty expansion and Edgerunners anime tie-in from Studio Trigger (which I still haven't seen yet as of writing this review) are announced. 
I finally decided to pick up this game when it and the expansion were on sale last year, and I'm glad I did. Most of the hours I have as of this review are from a single playthrough. I'm a completionist who likes to do absolutely everything, so it probably won't take that long for most people.
If you've been thinking about getting this game, I would definitely recommend it. It's no Witcher 3, but I don't think CD Projekt will ever be able to top that one.

review:
This game is absolutely amazing.

When i first bought the game I was quite sceptical due to the games release but it was nothing like i thought it would be. I'm a big fan of futuristic sci-fi type games and this was nothing short of a masterpiece. This game genuinely changed me as a person and when i finished my first playthrough i couldn't wait for my next one, the different endings and dialogue options leave so much room for different playthroughs and the cyberware too for example my first playthrough i did a katana sandy build, but on my second playthrough i chose a monowire netrunner build (which is crazy fun).

Don't even get me started on the dlc, the first mission/introduction was flawless and action packed. the characters are amazing in both the base game and dlc with every character having a different backstory with some being happy and some being extremely depressing (reed). The addition of the relic skill tree was such a smart move and it adds more options and improvements for certain builds.

The amount of side quests this game has is insane, with gigs, side biz and main side quests (if that makes sense). The game also has 4 romance options (panam is a baddie btw) i have only done 2/4 so far and the separate quest lines for all of the romance options was a great idea.

The redemption this game had is insane, from the bug filled release in 2020, to the absolute perfection it is today with completely revamped aspects for example the skill tree, which originally felt quite boring is now extremely helpful and easy to understand.

In summary, buy this game asap it is incredible from the story to the characters to the soundtrack, its perfect.
5/5 stars, 10/10

R.I.P Jackie
R.I.P Johnny

review:
One of my all time favorite games. My hours are a bit misleading though, since I had the game on another platform. 

*Great Linux Desktop (Nobara Linux or Arch Linux) compatibility & works great on Steam Deck as well. 
*Optimized well, I heard launch was bad, but that's old news at this point the game runs great, it barely lags for me. 
*Story & writing are amazing, choices matter, many endings to choose from.
*Great systems (builds,cyberware, weapons) I like it complex.
*High to low difficulty for casual or players who want a challenge
*Character customization is insane, with a male & female voice actor, customization of literally everything about your character. It gives freedom and gives life to your V. 
*Interesting cast of characters, antagonists
*Phantom Liberty is intense, rewarding, and overall fun! A great spy drama, but also sci-fi. One ending 
[spoiler] even has influence from Alien Isolation [/spoiler] , had me on my seat. But I won't go into too much detail on that, you'll have to play through it yourself. 

I could go on all day, but it's a great game. I put it on my top 5 right by the Dying Light series <3. These are great developers, who yes had a rough launch, but in the end they fixed it and turned it into a great game.

review:
Cyberpunk 2077 is honestly one of those rare games that feels bigger than just a game — it’s an experience, and revisiting it on PC after already putting around 70 hours into it on PlayStation has only made that more clear.

I already loved my time with it before, but playing it on PC feels like seeing Night City the way it was truly meant to be experienced. The jump in visuals, smoother performance, higher frame rates, and overall immersion completely transform the game. It’s not just an upgrade — it genuinely feels like rediscovering the entire world again.

Night City itself is one of the most impressive settings ever created in gaming. The scale, atmosphere, verticality, and detail are on another level. Every district feels distinct, alive, and layered with its own identity. Whether you’re walking through neon-lit streets, cruising through crowded highways, or just taking in the city at night, there’s a constant sense that you’re inside something special.

What makes Cyberpunk 2077 truly stand out though is how much substance there is beneath the style. The story is gripping, emotional, and deeply personal, with characters that actually stay with you. V’s journey, combined with Johnny Silverhand’s presence, creates a narrative that feels both intimate and massive at the same time. It’s one of those stories that sticks in your head long after you stop playing.

Gameplay-wise, the freedom is incredible. Whether you lean into hacking, stealth, brute force, or a hybrid build, the game gives you so many ways to approach combat and progression. On PC especially, everything feels sharper and more responsive, making the gameplay loop even more satisfying.

And visually — this game is absolutely generational. Cyberpunk 2077 on capable hardware is one of the best-looking games ever made. The lighting, density, reflections, and environmental detail can genuinely feel next-gen in ways that few titles manage.

After experiencing both PlayStation and PC, I can confidently say Cyberpunk 2077 is a must-play title. It’s a game that pushed boundaries, evolved massively, and now stands as one of the defining experiences of its generation. This isn’t just a comeback story — it’s a modern masterpiece that every gamer should experience at least once.

review: Are you 60+ years old wishing your heydays were not behind you? Do you still use outdated punk slang while thinking that smoking, drugs and alcohol is what will give you access to the cool kids club? Do you think the system is rigged and everything revolves around your idea of the world? Then this is the game for you. Man, do I feel dirty after rolling in this mudpile. It is visually stunning, controls are easy to learn and there are times that you are having fund, but that wears out fairly quickly. I logged 80 hours because I nearly made it through a first playthrough and got bored near the end. Started from scratch thinking things would be better just to be reminded of how bland the actual storyline is. You max out your stats way before even making a sizeable dent in the main plotline. NONE of your decisions make any difference (replayability is 15/100). Most characters are straight out of the lost-and-found box and the creators pushed a great deal to prove that it is possible to cram so much nonsense narrative into a single game. Pretentious and pointless. Clearly too much effort went into polishing and not enough into making a good core.

review:
Cyberpunk 2077 + Phantom Liberty
An Honest Review From Someone Who Almost Quit

I nearly bounced off this game twice and I'm glad I didn't.

The honest scores
Main game: 7/10. 
Phantom Liberty: 10/10. 
The world and side characters: 10/10. 
The RPG systems once they click: 8/10. 
If you want a single number, call it an 8 - 8.5 but that's not the whole story.

It is not the best game ever made. It is absolutely essential if you have a capable PC and the patience to let it breathe. 

Slow down
The game will tell you everything is urgent. It is not. I hit the main story point of no return in around 18 hours without feeling like I was rushing. The narrative momentum pulls you forward and the writing frames everything as life or death. Stop and turn around.

The enjoyment starts when you invest in the world rather than sprinting through it. Do the NYPD scanner huscles and gigs. Talk to the people. Spend your money on cyberware at the ripperdoc rather than hoarding it or on cars and apartments. You probably don't need to buy guns and you mostly end up with iconic weapons anyhow. Upgrade your build deliberately. The game only opens up when your character has shape and you have context for the world you're moving through.

If you hit the point of no return feeling underpowered and confused, you went too fast. The game will never tell you.

The side characters and their stories are frequently better than the main quest. Some of the best writing in the game is tucked into missions that look like filler on the map. 

Pick a lane and go all in
Do not spread your attribute points across multiple trees. Pick a build direction early: stealth, netrunner, blades, tech weapons and commit. The game does not tell you this and it will punish you badly in the early hours if you don't. The viable endgame builds are fewer than the skill trees suggest, though each plays differently once it coheres. That click moment, when combat finally feels like yours, is worth getting to and it's awesome.

On normal difficulty the game stops being challenging well before the halfway point, and I say that as someone who isn't a hardcore gamer. For me, the regular enemies feel like nothing much but the bosses are often dull and spongey. This is much worse on harder difficulties where the regular enemies feel about right but the bosses are nauseatingly boring. The gun handling feels good and the skill trees are much improved since launch, but a lot of the praise you'll read for both assumes a difficulty calibration that isn't there. If you want a test, play on hard from the start but be prepared for the early hours to be rough before your build sticks.

The world
Night City is one of the most extraordinary environments ever built in a video game. The density, the verticality, the neon and rain and rot underneath the corporate gloss. There is nothing quite like it. However, it sometimes feels like a crowd scene in a film rather than a place that exists without you. But this was with the crowd setting a bit lower which I had to do with my PC. Your milage will vary here and we'll get to that soon. The NPCs wander but they don't quite convince outside of scripted moments.

Dogtown in Phantom Liberty is smaller, more curated, and fixes all of this. Every person there feels like they belong in that exact spot.

The open world underneath the beautiful surface is largely checkbox busywork. Scanners, gigs, and assorted map icons follow the same compulsive loop you'd find in any Ubisoft title, just dressed in better writing and a more interesting setting. You can mostly ignore it. But it's there and I enjoyed the ticking the boxes towards the end and quite liked getting all the acheivements. But I am a lapsed Ubi addict.

The music
The soundtrack has a reputation for being exceptional and largely gets a pass in reviews. The reality is more uneven. Three or four radio stations are really great. Pacific Dreams is the obvious standout and earns every word of praise it gets. But several stations feel included for demographic coverage rather than tone, and a game that otherwise obsesses over atmosphere occasionally jumps into something weird mid-drive and pulls you straight out of it. Not a dealbreaker and the sountrack is great, but it's not this perfect thing people say it is. 

A note on VRAM
My 4070 Super has 12GB of VRAM and that is borderline enough for this game with path tracing on. If you push it too hard you won't get crashes necessarily, but you will get texture pop-in and stutters that make the game feel broken when it isn't. You need to monitor this actively.

Download MSI Afterburner and run the overlay while adjusting settings. Watch VRAM usage in real time. This is how I worked out that DLSS Quality was pushing me over budget while DLSS Balanced brought it under control, and that reducing shadow quality clawed back additional headroom. Those multiple shadow settings were key and you'll need to spend hours with settings. Those two changes together made the difference between a stuttery mess and a stable, beautiful game. I spent roughly ten hours getting settings right. Budget your time. If you have 8GB you will struggle significantly. 12GB is workable but requires careful management and 16GB will give you proper breathing room.

You need a real PC
Steam Deck verified means it runs but it does not mean it does the game justice. Half the point of Cyberpunk 2077 is standing in a rain-soaked street watching neon reflect off everything. That experience requires proper hardware. I cannot believe this game shipped on PS4. That decision forced compromises you can still feel throughout the base game and is surely one of the reasons that Phantom Liberty is so much better.

The sweet spot on my 4070 Super was 1440p with path tracing on and DLSS Balanced. 4K sounds better but you sacrifice too much ray tracing fidelity for the resolution. Path tracing is breathtaking for a game where the entire aesthetic is built on light and reflection. It is worth the settings investment to get there.

Phantom Liberty — I say finish the main story first
Do the main story first, then go to Phantom Liberty. I played it mid-late game, I was maybe 40 hours or so in and spent more than 20 hours in Dogtown meeting extraordinary new characters, then returned to the main game feeling completely disoriented. People I'd forgotten, momentum lost, a massive tonal shift back to a story I'd half left behind.

Phantom Liberty is a different and better game within the game. A well-paced spy thriller with outstanding writing, a paranoid atmosphere, and some of the best character work in any game I've played in years. It is essential. But treat it as a second act rather than an interruption. It's almost like another game. The two endings are worth playing seperately. Reload and do it again. Proper good stuff and I thought far better than the endings of the base game.

Overall... the onboarding is crap. The power curve collapses way too fast. The three lifepath options vanish after the prologue despite being sold as defining choices that shape your experience. These problems were never patched out and they got overshadowed by the redemption story.

Should you play it?
Yes, if you have the hardware and the patience. No, if you want to sprint through it or you're on modest specs.

This is the Crysis moment of this generation. A game built ahead of the hardware that can fully run it. Path tracing on a capable PC is what it was always supposed to be. Phantom Liberty is what the whole game was always supposed to be.

I'll come back to Night City in ten years on whatever hardware exists then and think whoooaaa. I'm confident of that.

review:
So I've had the game for a bit and played through the launch which has had enough said about it and the updates have been good overall.

Positives its a pretty good game and the narrative is fun to play through once in a while.

Some of the builds can get pretty fun.

I want to see what they do with the sequel.

Negatives-

The scaling... you actively become weaker as you level up as enemies outscale abilities. This just feels pretty ick as cyberware armor can get hilariously strong around lvl 20-30 (borderline invincible in some cases) but post that falls off like nobodies business and the gains from unlocking other aspects of the skill tree don't measure up to a very specific deliberately lower level scale character. Also suffers from enemies getting more spongy rather than smarter/more varied in ability which again means that keeping a character in a level sweetspot makes you feel stronger overall than as a maxed out/close to end game V.

Missed opportunities- the game feels like a lot of this. Gangs treat you the same whether you've been massacring them or playing non-lethal/non-aggressive towards them. This feels like missed opportunity to actually interact with the city. Furthermore there are 3 options to pick from on who supports you at the end... this feels off as there are several side characters you have moments you just want to scream at the screen to lend V a hand as they basically move heaven and earth for the sake of these side characters.

Missing content? This gets a question mark because some of it feels like a bit of deliberate mystery vs there is something missing but particularly with the Peralez section there are quite a few dangling plot threads that feels off. From Peter, to Mr. Blue Eyes, to Night Corps to potential AI interference it just drops off the map. Again that one could be a deliberate V needs to learn to leave things alone moments but it still feels off. Bartmoss is another where it feels like even if its not real might be something to check out rather than a throwaway easter egg.

Won't say who but some of the fixer's feel woefully underutilized in comparison to others. Even from a purely mission count standpoint it feels a bit like they were supposed to get some additions like Mr. Hands did in Phantom Liberty but just didn't. 

Stealth gameplay- clearly got targeted over time as the updates went on. Very much a case of the toolbox might have been overtuned at the start but the game clearly has a "correct" way to go about stealth now rather than just silencers/netrunning. Some of the decisions I'd say were good but very much a case of this is the way the game is intended to be played not how people were doing it.

V also really could have used a personal antagonist and while yes the setting has the system itself as antagonistic fundamentally his vendetta's are Johnny's by proxy and trying to stay alive. While its very on brand for the corporate machine to not notice an insignificant cog its also possible to have some kind of personal connections a la Adam/Johnny which V is really just lacking.... dunno could have kept the impersonal nature overall as the overarching V vs the corporate culture but still had a bit more connection to one of its cogs. 

Internal inconsistency.... there are things in the narrative that don't really add up to me. A case example being Regina's fascination with cyberpsychosis despite it being a kind of resolved thing... hell V has the opportunity to meet a reformed cyberpsycho embedded in a sidequest and the phenomenon isn't exactly unknown in the setting both causes and treatments and the game actually has an in universe example of a treated individual so.... ehhh. But there is a bit more floating that doesn't make sense.

As for the DLC.... the interaction between the NUSA and V is kind of a head scratcher. Ostensibly by the time they meet he's done something that they've quite literally fought world wars trying to do and were gearing up for another and they know about this... and its kind of an oh well no big deal you are still a hodunk merc lowlife.... it just feels more than a bit off if you stop to look at it for a moment. This kind of highlights another issue where the game feels very vertical slice of the setting and drops in a lot of little details that include the wider setting as hints and nuggets but functionally operates like it doesn't matter. Again referring to the Bartmoss thing is brushed over so quickly that it really doesn't give credit to what just happened. The man literally responsible for the state of the world and is acknowledged as such 50 years later and broadcast information about him is still referred to was just found to have been active for years after everyone thought he was dead and... what no one bats an eyelash at what might have been going on?

Anyways with all that said I'm still going to recommend the game for a play through or two and a return every once and a while for the things that make it worth playing. Just be aware its not flawless by any stretch and I hope that the next one follows in the witcher franchises footsteps and continues to improve from game to game.

review:
This was pretty bad.  Going through the tutorial, I was pretty excited to finally hit the streets.  When I did, and tried to get my character properly attired so I could FEEL like a cool cyberpunk character, I see the most tiny and fugly assortment of clothes I've ever seen in a sandbox game.  The clothes shop put far away from where V's apartment was as if the developers themselves were embarrassed by it.  On the way, I saw two clunky armed robberies, one that was easily solved by snapping everyone's neck, the other a huge gang in a food cart pod that I ran away from.

The encounters were so bad, and the aesthetic options so disappointing, I just instantly realized I do not want to dedicate a bunch of hours to this game.  So much polish put into the main storyline and a thin veneer of cyberpunk aesthetic everywhere else, the minute you step away from the campaign path I feel like I'm in a cardboard town.

review:
My personal gripes for disliking the game are as follows: The way it is designed means you can do any kind of run or build an all around character and let me tell you the content and world building is to die for. But it begins to fall flat when there is not much variety in weapons due to them just being scaled into tiers of 1-5. This was a great RPG to play but there is no pacing with the side quests and a lot of the purchases you make can be avoided and you can just focus on getting Cybernetic augments and that becomes the game as become and evolved lifeform. You can ignore hacking, you can ignore melee combat you can ignore being a stealth build. Which is fine and proper but once you find a gameplay style that works for you the entire game stops feeling fun and its just a "go here and do this, kill x amount of people, get paid."

Still one hell of a story, one I admire but you see the flaws. V's story is one where there are no good endings only what you willing to sacrifice to go big or die in an unmarked grave. Once you put speed bumps on the story I find you can easily break this the perk systems so your playstyle moves from FPS to mass murdering simulation. Good if you want that but I honestly wish I knew more going into the Cyberpunk universe. Its a very old RPG and the California setting really did not to it must justice if you ask me.

I adored the characters and the journey. Hell I almost 200 hours into it. But its so good it misses out in other areas and many of the character customization features are designed for looks not for real immersion. Sure its got quality but to me its just another grind fest where you can ignore all the side quests and till have a fine time. That is a red flag for me. I have always been a fan of CD Projekt Red but trying to do a dystopian rogue AI landscape works except there are no monsters and the ruins of civilization for a corporate supremacy becomes a boring environment quickly. I felt drained and disappointed after completing the main missions in both Vanilla and Phantom Liberty because nothing improves in the world its just you signing off permanetly what is a well written world where technology replaces a good degree of mythology but so much is missing.

I would have loved to see real mutants or hybrids which do exist lore wise in the game but again, so much time and money was spent on making a high fi retro vibe for all designs and culture you see inside of 2077 it removes everything else. You remain a boring human with boring yet flashy character designs or customizations to your body but they really do nothing. You are in a vice grip of conflict that coexists such interesting character story arc's that once you realize not many of them end well the experience becomes draining but a ride worth taking once. Repeated playthroughs with making different choices offer some thrill and then its gone. Violently, you are left looking for the next thrill, firefight or something you didn't discover. Like the desert of the Mohave, this place dries you up fast. 

You are in for a wild ride but don't expect any rewards long term. Feels good to become a badass and literally become the center of attention but how that plays into exploring anything beyond that? Its a hollow shell like those drained oil fields or trash heaps, and the world becomes very boring. I give them a 9/10 but personally the more I play and beat the game over and over again, the more I dislike 2077 with a heavy heart. I would have liked to see a game modeled after the original source material this RPG was molded by decades ago. But alas, my purist pursuits of old world RPGs and their iconic names means this game could never have lived long to my expectations. 

Still I was fine paying 60$ for it, the quality and content is there but once you milk it all.....don't expect it to give anything back with interest. I am happy to say now I know what the hype is about. CD Projekt Red can make a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ killer story which remains emotionally charged and damaging. I just wish there was more options and variety. I don't hate this game but I expected more when it came to roleplaying. This plays like "Doomplaying" given how your main character is running out of time regardless of what happens.

A flawed game that still delivers one of the most memorable journeys in modern RPGs.

review:
Despite it's poor launch, Cyberpunk 2077 has grown to become a great game.

Good setting, ambiance, voice acting ties the whole world together in a nice little package.

Having done a 100% achievement run, this game can grow to be a little tedious at times due to its repetitiveness in terms of side content, but dive deep into the world and be BEING in Night City is something that is unforgettable.

Add to that the Phantom Liberty expansion that adds a whole new area and quests to explore. Cyberpunk 2077 has become one of those games you keep thinking about even when you're not playing.

The somewhat damaged quest however leaves a bad taste in my mouth. Why is the game a survival horror all of a sudden? It makes zero sense.

Overall, if you like neon drenched cities and just driving around a city that feels alive. Nothing can beat this.

Overall a decent shooter but as an RPG it falls extremely flat.

review:
It's ok. Just ok. I understand why people were disappointed by the game on first release. I didn't really have any expectations going in to the game and I feel pretty lukewarm about the game. If I really got my hopes up before release I definitely would have been really let down. 

The graphics look really good and the environments are extremely well designed but the city feels oddly empty. Needs more random events happening to keep your interest. Combat is passable but a bit boring. The world is grim and gritty and not super pleasant to spend time in. Surprisingly few romance options for a game of this size but maybe I've been spoiled by Baldur's gate 3. 

I would say if your hardware is great and you really like gritty sci-fi cyberpunk then by all means purchase the game. As is, I'm really just playing it right now because there's nothing else that interests me in playing.

review:
Game is trash now. They FORCE you to agree to a UELA that includes arbitration clause. So sick of companies doing this. If you refuse to accept their unreasonable agreement then they don't let you play a game you PAID for. AND NO refund will be issued...

Arbitration agreement means you wave your right to have any case against the company overseen by a judge. Instead, companies can hire someone private who will also charge you a fee to deliberate cases privately. 

Look up Arbitration clauses. Major corporations are literally abusing people with these every day. They draw out cases till the average person can no longer afford to pay the arbitrator. It's absurd and it should be illegal!

review:
Cyberpunk 2077 is a breathtaking experience, visually, mechanically, and narratively. This is my second time 100% completing the game (first on Xbox, now post-Cyberpunk 2077: Phantom Liberty), and it still manages to pull me in in a way very few games can.

The jank and existential threat to your sanity that came with launch? Long gone. What remains is a genuinely polished, deeply immersive masterpiece that leaves you wanting more and maybe questioning your life choices along the way.

The base game alone delivers a thrilling story, packed with hours of side content, gigs, and jobs to keep any merc busy. The characters are compelling, and Night City feels truly alive… even if it’s mostly chrome, neon, and bad decisions. Since the 2.0 update, the game feels like an entirely new experience compared to my original playthrough, smoother, deeper, and far more engaging.

And then there’s Phantom Liberty, which is not just the icing on the cake, but the icing, the cherry, and the entire bakery. It’s that good. The expansion constantly shifts your focus, subverts expectations, and keeps you in a state of tension where you’re never quite sure who you can trust. Who’s really your choom? Honestly… probably no one.

The writing is exceptional. It takes the already strong character work from the base game and pushes it even further, diving into themes of loyalty, control, and survival. By the end, the idea of a “good” ending feels almost irrelevant, because in Night City, there really aren’t any. Not for V, and not for the people they care about.

I won’t spoil anything, but this game sticks with you. The sounds, the atmosphere, the skyline, it all lingers long after you log off.

And yeah… you are breathtaking.

review:
The kind of stupid fake RPG people that don't actually like RPGs like. Terrible RPG mechanics with big skill trees that just change very little. This is a game with cybernetic implants yet there's so little ability to meaningfully change or augment your character. So much is just % stat modifiers and it's all so bland and creatively bankrupt.

The game creates a narrative of urgency, only to drop you into an open world encouraging you to waste as much time as possible in Night City. This kind of gameplay-narrative dissonance really takes away from the storytelling, in my opinion. The story itself seems decent enough, there's good voice acting and facial animations, but the game refuses to break first person which can make the storytelling feel stiff and limited by this decision. I find it hard to immerse when protagonist V just feels like a camera & voicebox watching people talk rather than a living part of the world.

Combat is very bland & repetitive and there is just so much of it to get through. Driving is the absolute basic level of serviceable. Character customisation is okay, but again it's largely first-person so it feels a lot like fluff. Music was really not for me. Graphics are mostly great though.

This is a crowd-pleasing 'RPG' for those that just want to mindlessly kill things and experience a drawn out story, but for me it all just feels so empty and soulless despite trying to like it.

The main progression feels a bit railroaded, but gives enough flavortext to give the feeling of vast options and makes the game far more enjoyable.

Runs at a smooth 60fps at 1440p High with no ray tracing on a 3070. I've noticed that regardless of where you're physically at, the framerate seems to drop for whatever reason in the late game. It runs at 1080p60 with RT up until about 3/4 of the way through the game where it'll drop to 45-60. I turned off RT and switched to 1440p, and performance is a solid 60, so I recommend RT off and resolution high. It's a shame since RT looks amazing in this game.

review:
Despite the long production time, the many delays, and the launch that was good but hype and entitlement held up to an unrealistic standard, this is truly a masterpiece. One of the greatest games ever made. 

Based on the tabletop rpg from the 80's and insanely faithful to the lore of that setting and world. It's easy to see that CD Projekt Red are genuine fans of the source material. 

Night City feels alive, it feels real, and after a while it feels like home. A dysfunctional and messy home but a home nonetheless. 

 The characters have nuance and multitudes to their personalities and it never feels like they're waiting around for the player to show up. 

The story is one for the ages that explores themes of death, immortality, justice and vengeance. 

The addition of the numerous updates that smooth out the early kinks, as references to the show and ttrpg as well as the DLC, Phantom Liberty, only add a pristine cherry on top of the experience. Phantom Liberty keeps the developers tradition alive of expansions truly worthy of the base game. 

If you enjoy gaming at all as an entertainment and storytelling medium then this is a must play.
