review:
What a great game that I wish I had picked up sooner!

What kept me away from FromSoft game's was the complete hard-stop I experienced back with DS1 (PTDE) at Blight Town.  Manditory difficult jump(s), poison, with high fall probability in a scaffolding-type ladder/maze area... died so many times until I simply got frustrated and uninstalled the game... although I had thoroughly enjoyed it up to that point.

Things I love about this game:
1) large beautiful open world just begging to be explored... having trouble with a boss? go explore, level up, and come back later
2) exploration is often well rewarded
3) tonnes of sorceries, incantations, armor, weapons, Ashes of War, and summons to mix'n' match for a variety of play-styles and builds
4) crafting can turn pickups into useful items... like bombs, cures, xp boost, item find boost, etc.
5) map marker system super helpful
6) There are "natural" spots in the game near site's of grace that make "time efficient" XP farming "easy" when you were either (a) looking to reach item stat requirements, (b) wanted to force a level up to spend all your runes before attempting a difficult fight, or (c) needed more runes to purchase items.  The first spot I used was inside a castle just outside the final boss room... there was a giant there just down the steps that I could kill easily, rest, and repeat whenever I wanted/needed more runes.

What i wish the game had:
1) a side quest tracker / journal - the site's of grace point you along the main story path, but you have to manually make note of "side quest details" you find via conversations with NPC's... if they mention a place/person make sure you come back to talk to them later after you've visited who/where they the mentioned to move the side quest along.  Sometimes it's hard to know if the conversation was just delivering background/lore or giving you a side quest.

Things I'm not a fan of:
1) side quests can auto-advance / skip milestones if you pushed ahead too far either via exploration or progressing the main quest.  very easy to do if you didn't realize you had a side quest with the absence of a quest tracker / journal
2) I generally don't do well with "jumping puzzles" and die... so more a me issue than the game.  There are a number of spots/areas where parkour (and selective falling) is important... especially if you want to explore/traverse all areas... so far I've managed to accomplish most without too many deaths.

Things I hate about the game:
1) Nothing comes to mind.

At the time of writing this review I have yet to complete the game.  I'm nearing 130 hours on my second character... I abondoned the first character (80-90 hrs) after figuring out they had "botched side quests".  I've yet to decide if I'm going to try the "backup/restore my save file" method for reaching all the endings or if I'll just replay the game 3 times... I have a feeling I'd replay this game anyway, but getting the achievements out of the way in this playthourgh (via save backup/restore) could take the pressure off and/or eliminate the possibility of not unlocking or choosing the desired next ending achievement by mistake.

review:
this playthrough makes mee wish there was a middle option for reviewing.

this time i wanted to explore everything and do everything, i just gave up half way through and went to beat the main bosses and finish the story. you can make fun of me for saying there is too much ♥♥♥♥ to do in this game. but there really is.

my latest playthrough ends at like 127 hours and i stopped bothering to explore the second half of the game and it's areas. i once again get to radhan at the dlc's final boss, but give up on it because i don't think it is worth trying to finish. if it is your first time, it is really cool, it's amazing and there is so much to do you can just do it forever. once i get the stuff i like, i don't want to waste time to go places when i won't get anything good.

so graphically, it is a great looking game, it all looks dark and gritty, but clean and nice at the same time, areas are unique and diverse and all are interesting to explore and see the unique architecture of it all. weapons all looks nice, and there are some that are phenomenal. 

the music is always good, it is one of fromsoft's best details in all their games. from the simple minimalist music of the areas which really help give you subtle ideas of how it feels, and can get your into the mood of how wrong a place actually would feel if you were there. it is incredibly immersive and it doesn't have to blast full soundtracks at you to get the idea. then you get slightly more engaging combat music which lets you know you are currently in danger, and then the fantastic boss music. it is just always so good to listen to and some bosses are elevated because of how good their track is.

gameplay wise, i have some problems here, coming off of playing all the souls games recently. this one feels good and has a good speed to it. the weapons are honestly all fun, i used more weapons this time than i ever did and even basic variants, with customizing the weapons skills can make it really fun. wether you use new things, unique weapons, legendary, or the basic ones fitted to your playstyle and preference.

my problem is, in the base game it really isn't THAT bad, there are some annoying things but it can kind of be overlooked. mostly poise being a complete joke and being absolutely meaningless unless you wear heavy armor, as well as getting lots of high poise enemies late game which makes you just want to use heavy weapons yourself because they tank through anything that isn't a heavy attack from anything small.

the dlc messed me up, and i shouldn't have entered it before i beat the main game because i burned out on exploration and using items/weapons as i played through it. it also bring a new scaling system which makes a lot of things obscenely too strong until you explore around enough and find the scaling items to boost your level, and even then sometimes it doesn't even feel like it is enough to even the playing field.

while i would personally say that like 50-60% of the base game is amazing and the other 40% is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ just to make the game feel more difficult than it already is, or the lock on system absolutely ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ itself whenever an enemy has a long neck. like ancient dragons, they have two lock on points and one you can't hit because it is too high while the head is too far in front of the body so the camera likes to face the opposite way meaning you can't see anything the enemy is doing. the dlc i would say is more ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ than fun because of scaling, and a lot of enemy designs. bosses are cool and cinematic, but even tough enemies later into the dlc are awful designed. bosses that move super fast, read all your inputs and have such fast attacks that you can't avoid them without constant roll catching, or just decimate any shield you bring because of how much they attack. fire knights are abysmal because of their infinite attacking they can do. not only are they fast but they can dodge and summon homing magic that has a delay to it. so they catch you in a loop of dodge until they decide to get close, or risk getting it and taking a lot of damage just to try to get them to stop.

the last thing i think to talk about would be the exploration. there is so much of it and it is nice, the world ids packed with groups that roam around or people on patrol or sitting around. it really does feel like it is lived in. but there is so much that you spend so much time running around looking for things and exploring and it honestly feels like a good portion of what you do is just filler for the actual content of the game. there were too many times where you would explore somewhere thinking there could be something cool only to find either nothing. or needlessly tough enemies that aren't worth fighting, that end up sitting around a single crafting item. which if you use that system, it might be useful. but it got to a point where i picked up crafting materials and never opened my menu again. so it was essentially meaningless junk. just to make you feel like you were being rewarded.

like. this game is great, i like it. but the amount of time it takes to full clear and explore, and the enemy designed can really hurt the game and it's enjoyment and really made me just not care about fighting a lot of bosses or going through a lot of areas. since it just kind of got to a point where i felt like i was going to do these things and waste my time most of  the time.

at first i always thought it was dumb to look up a guide to find things in souls games, but replaying elden ring this time makes me appreciate that you can just skip all the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ and filler and get right to the good stuff that will help you enjoy the game better.

it's worth it to play if you want. and while i am not the sweatiest thousand hour gamer on this game, i still find these games can be hard, challenging, or fun because of this. but i feel like this one would be the hardest of all of them, so keep that in mind if you weren't sure if you would want to play this or one of the souls series instead.

review:
Guess that I am preaching to the choir from a very big choir by writing something positive about Elden Ring. But, well, the game does deserve it.
The world is fantastic and huge. It is filled with so much content that I have never done 100 percent of the game despite being on my fourth playthrough by now. The story is rich and immersive and subtle and says things. It is such a privilege to be able to experience it. The combat is also fantastic, although it requires a lot of creativity as you mix together incantations and spells with your normal move sets to duds and defend against attacks. Like for example with ground attacks, you do not just have to jump attack over them. You can jump up and do the Renala Moon spell, which lifts you into the air and avoid the attack at the same time. Pretty cool, but does require creativity.
And man, I did buy this game on a discount on sale. But it is honestly so fantastic that even at full price, the game feels like it is permanently discounted. It is worth more than $60. (Not that I am encouraging game companies to raise their prices though.)

review: This massive RPG delivers on the promise of a vast, breathing, living world to explore. Everything feels alive and you do, indeed, feel like an outsider while playing. This "me against the world" theme is common in FromSoft games, and the formula works. The only thing I take issue with are the moments of the game which expect you to (for example) find a hidden wall in a random room to access a whole other region of the map. I understand that this is appealing for some, but I simply do not have the time to explore every nook and cranny of every building in this game as it is gargantuan. Nevertheless, the boss-fights are fun and can be as challenging as you like; NG+ offers a cool way to replay the game too, and completely restarting on a fresh save offers a chance to find hidden content because there is lots. As someone who has only previously played Sekiro, this was a 4 out of 5 star game for me. However, I do understand that I am not the target audience for this game. As such, I assume that if you enjoy the FromSoft formula, then you will love this.

review:
One Ring made by the evil lord Sauron Hidetaka Miyazaki, One Ring to rule them all, One Ring to find them, One Ring to bring them all and in the darkness bind them, In the Land of Japanese software development warehouse building where Shadows lie.

Elden Ring is not worth your time due to the profound curse placed upon the game's core design by the evil man who made it. The game aims at being infinitely replayable, but each of its assets is designed in a narrow fashion - so much so that the world does not feel as open as it is rather episodic. Each and every instance in the game is instrumented to be seen and encountered in a specific way which makes the experience of world exploration predictable and less exciting after playing for the first time. The dificulty ranges of the various explorable regions of the game world are awfully imballanced, so much so that it's less enjoyable to avoid choosing a linear path through the game on later playthroughs. The game world's incredibly size makes this issue even more frustrating - in order to experience something new on a subsequent playthrough, the player has to cross massive distances which are increasingly less and less aproachable the further he is from the starting point, ultimately discouraging exploration and narrowing the game experience to a single, most optimalized path of progression. There are only so many ways to reexperience the game's world without being severely discouraged and punished. And so ultimately, the game world's size and the quantity of its assets is severely nullified as the various paths to approach them are tragically so very limited.

The game's world becomes eventually too predictable and the premise of experiencing the game in a fresh way nonexistent, which is not fun. Let it be pronounced and heard, Elden Ring could be legendary, but it is not, and won't be, unless FromSoftware developpers cease to practice the habit of forgetting a game once it's been created, despite the quantity of bothersome features still present and still widely acknowledged. One may recall the pathos of never properly finishing Lost Izalith in their previous game Dark Souls, an area of the game so very unique and all the more so disappointing for being critically underdevelopped. I feel very simillar about Elden Ring.

As a former world of warcraft player, I see fit to pronounce a variety of ideas which could severely improve the experience of Elden Ring if they were ever developped, as they are present in that particular game, and although a very different game at that, still possesing a massive world and the spirit of an rpg game. 

Those ideas would be:
There should be a variety of start points in Elden Ring, or at the very least, it should be possible to cross into other regions of the game from Limgrave without being stupefied and severely punished as much as is the case now. There should be more low level regions surrounding Limgrave so as to increase replayability and the possibilities of exploration on future playthroughs. And yes, despite the existence of various teleportation chests in Limgrave, the fact that the places you are sent to are completely above Limgrave's difficulty makes them less relevant as a travel feature. There is not enough enemy variety at each of the game's various places - an occasional "rare enemy spawn" or the promise of a "rare item drop" would be a solution to this issue. And lastly, there should be more paths connecting the various regions and places of the Elden Ring world and more accessible ways to connect the game's many features.

In conclusion, Elden Ring nowhere near as fun as enjoyable after the first couple of playthroughs, it becomes stale and predictable rather too fast, and that is a completely nonsensical feature for a game of such high ambition and such quantity of the game's features.

I was only able to put in like 2 hours on MnK before I got the to point that I simply could not play anymore, I felt too clunky and combat was agony. GET. THE. CONTROLLER. 

Now on to the actual review.

This game is unlike anything I have ever played before. It literally just launches you into the most hostile, uninviting, and dangerous setting and that's it. All you have is the "Armor" you are wearing and the will to become the Elden Lord. This game will NOT hold your hand, and it might seem weird and awkward at first but it is the best thing they could have done. The exploring alone is unparalleled, the map is deceptively ENORMOUS, and trust me, if its your first run, you will not BELIEVE the magnitude of this game. 

The boss fights.
Cinematic. One of the biggest reasons I die so much is because I get so distracted by the beautiful animations of the boss fights, its incredibly hard not to with the flashes of light and movement sequencing some of these bosses have. Are the fights hard? ABSOLUTELY! Are they incredibly frustrating? YOU BET! Does being stun locked and hit by 6 hits in a row when you accidentally ran out of stamina and held your shield and get killed when the boss was literally one shot and now you need to get a new controller cuz yours somehow ended up smashed to pieces on the floor make you want to rip out your hair? Yeah... But when you finally defeat it? BLISS

Be patient, put in the hours. oh my god its so worth it. Since I got my controller 2 days ago i've but almost 30 hours into the game, and I selfishly wish I wasn't an actual adult with a wife so I could sink in 20 Hours a day into this masterpiece and not worry about anything else because I literally DREAM of this game when I am not playing it. 
Huge reccomend.

review:
I initially bought this in 2024, playing it for about 50 hours before stopping and resuming back in March-April of 2026. It didn't click for me until i respec'd my build into a battle-mage, and that's when i fell in love with the game, leading me to sink 80 more hours (and more since i plan to start NG+) and grab the DLC.

I also recommend playing it completely blind, as to not ruin the fun of surprises and being side-tracked every five minutes. If you can't help it, a walkthrough video or two is fine since their quest system is verbose and tedious, barely offering you guidance to get some specific items. It baffles me on how some people are meant to discover certain quest paths.

Despite the steep learning curve for first time souls-players, this game is very much worth it. If you are skeptical, buy it on sale. The DLC is also worth the steep price tag. As of this review, it has never gone on sale, so don't bother waiting for one and only question yourself if you'd like more Elden Ring before buying the DLC.

review:
this might be one of the worst games i've ever played; i genuinely don't see where the glaze comes from. i always try to look for positives in a game first, but it's hard to find ANYTHING to like about elden ring. no, i did not finish the game, but with a game like elden ring you don't need to finish it to gauge its quality. after all the game is extremely repetitive and lackluster (an excuse of an open world game), which is one of my main gripes.
i don't inherently have an issue with parries in combat systems, but the way that elden ring does it is not satisfying at all and feels extremely forced and, not only skill-less, but also soulless. sure, you can dodge attacks, or make a really cool build, but in the end the gameplay always bogs down to, "parry, swing, swing, parry, swing, occasional unparriable attack, swing, swing, big stun damage choreographed attack..." repeat until you either die or win. there isn't really much merit in making varied builds if it doesn't change the gameplay at all. that's like, if when equipping a sword as demoman in tf2, instead of swinging the sword, you throw it in an arc and get headshots for more damage. that's effectively what elden ring is with its build "variety". or, of course, you could just make a super OP build... but then where's the gameplay? what SKILL are you guys seeing in this? learning enemy patterns doesn't count, because you'll be doing that in ANY game. ergo, there is nothing that makes elden ring's combat unique save for its own poor game design.
the world is pretty good looking, but it felt extremely empty and unrewarding to explore. you could either find some niche enemy to fight, or a guy that sells you incrementally better weapons, or both, or neither. maybe it gets better as you explore the map. but the open world shouldn't force me to progress its story for it to... open the world (side note, i don't mean the game can't gradually open up its world or be something similar to BOTW, but one, i was already WAY past the tutorial, and two, it isn't acceptable when there isn't anything to explore in the first place.)
i can't speak on story much, but from what i have seen, it feels extremely misguided, fragmented and meandering. what is the message of elden ring's story? i get that not every story needs to have some super impactful message, but, if elden ring isn't one of those games, what is there to merit about its story? the fact it has cool character designs? you can go to some random place and find a tiny piece of lore? this isn't ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ undertale, it doesn't work.

seriously i do not recommend this game to anyone who has any self respect and actually wants the games they play to be of quality and substance. this might work as parryslop for the majority, but it really isn't all that.

review:
♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.

This game has changed my life. I am not joking. Many people talk about how the Soul's games challenge them in real life just as much as in game, but it actually cured my mental health. Not as escapism, but as a solution to my problem's with addiction and rage. Elden Ring challenges you; in your patience and resilience, as well as the gaming industry; with masterclass in game design. 

Elden Ring is the greatest game ever made. Period. Almost everyone here in the reviews are saying the same thing, it's basically fact. It's tied with Portal for being my favorite. It is FromSoft's magnum opus. I struggled to get into this game for over three years. I originally got the game near launch, and it was my introduction to the Soulsbourne series. I knew they were hard games, but I didn't expect it to be so hard to GET into. The performance wasn't great on my old system which also hindered my progression. I put it down. I started playing other games that didn't challenge me. They were easy in comparison, easy to play, easy to enjoy, just...easy. I felt a little empty, defeated, and I felt bored. I bought Sekiro knowing it's different to the other games in the series thinking maybe this one's for me. It turned out to be the most difficult of the bunch and I didn't get too far lol. But a few months ago I re-downloaded Elden Ring (I had 3 hours on record), and gave it another shot. I had a beefier PC and I just felt the FOMO, because I knew it got amazing reviews. I stepped into the Lands Between and I was hooked. 

I failed for hours, I shut down the voice in my head saying "told you so", I shut down the rage, and I took a deep breath and went again and again. I beat the first boss and I felt an adrenaline run, this high that felt more powerful, more invigorating than my addiction. I beat the next boss, and the next and the next. I was hooked. I hooked on the process of beating them. I got to the end of the game in tears not only for the masterpiece this game is in story, gameplay, world building etc. but also my progression as a human being. I'd grown so much. I was in therapy for my health during this whole time and in every session I was relating my health to Elden Ring. I was obsessed, obsessed with the real life results. I felt purpose in areas I lacked purpose before. I tackled my issues like I tackled bosses. I learnt their weaknesses from failing again and again, I learnt openings, I learnt control, and patience. I wanted to beat this game (addiction) so hard I could beat anything else thrown my way. I've been clean for six months now and I am so filled with joy and hope. 

I know I spoke about mainly me in this review, but we already know about how amazing this game is, and I think it is important to share our experiences with it. I can definitively say this game is art, for the reasons listed above. This game is perfect and I will forever cherish my time with it, wishing only to experience it all again for the first time. 

Good luck, have fun. Don't you dare go hollow. 
julesy.

The great Elden Ring was shattered.

In our home, across the fog, the Lands Between.

Now, Queen Marika the Eternal is nowhere to be found, and in the Night of the Black Knives, Godwyn the Golden was first to perish.

Soon, Marika's offspring, demigods all, claimed the shards of the Elden Ring.

The mad taint of their newfound strength triggered the Shattering.

A war from which no lord arose.

A war leading to abandonment by the Greater Will.

Oh, arise now, ye Tarnished.

Ye dead, who yet live.

The call of long-lost grace speaks to us all.

Hoarah Loux, chieftain of the badlands.

The ever-brilliant Goldmask.

Fia, the Deathbed Companion.

The loathsome Dung Eater.

And Sir Gideon Ofnir, the All-knowing.

...and one other. Whom grace would again bless.

A Tarnished of no renown. Cross the fog, to the Lands Between, to stand before the Elden Ring.

And become the Elden Lord."

review:
Elden Ring: A Breathtaking Baptism of Fire
A Masterpiece Reviewed from the Perspective of a Soulsborne Virgin

For years, I avoided the "Souls" genre. The infamous reputation of punishing difficulty, unforgiving mechanics, and the dreaded "YOU DIED" screen kept me safely in my comfort zone. But the sheer gravitational pull of Elden Ring was impossible to ignore. I finally took the plunge, fully prepared for misery. What I found instead was one of the greatest, most awe-inspiring gaming experiences of my entire life.

🌍 The Lands Between: An Unrivaled Sense of Discovery
From the very first moment you push open the heavy doors, step out into Limgrave, and witness the glowing, colossal Erdtree dominating the horizon, you realize this is no ordinary open world.

Zero Hand-Holding: There are no cluttered minimaps, no endless checklists, and no GPS markers telling you where to go. You are guided purely by your own curiosity.

Organic Exploration: See a bizarre, ruined castle on a distant cliff? You can go there. Stumble upon a hidden elevator in the woods? It might take you to an entirely breathtaking underground universe. The sense of genuine discovery is unmatched in modern gaming.

⚔️ The Crucible of Combat: Brutal, Fair, and Addictive
Let’s address the elephant in the room: Yes, Elden Ring is incredibly hard. The enemies hit like freight trains, and the bosses will relentlessly test your reflexes and your sanity. But here is the secret that veteran Souls players know, and the profound lesson this game taught me:

Death in Elden Ring is not a punishment; it is an education.

Every time you die, you learn a new attack pattern. You learn when to dodge, when to block, and when to strike. The combat is weighty, deliberate, and requires your full attention. When you finally land that killing blow on a boss that has wiped the floor with you twenty times, the adrenaline rush and the sense of sheer, hard-earned triumph are absolute perfection.

🛡️ Why It’s the Perfect First "Souls" Game
Previous games in this genre often trapped you in a narrow corridor with an impossible boss, forcing you to "git gud" or quit. Elden Ring fundamentally changes the formula by giving you the ultimate weapon: Freedom.

The "Walk Away" Strategy: If a boss is destroying you, you don't have to smash your head against a brick wall. Simply summon your spectral steed, Torrent, ride off in the opposite direction, explore some dungeons, level up your stats, find a better sword, and come back as a demigod to exact your revenge.

Spirit Ashes: The game introduces summonable spirit companions. Having a pack of spectral wolves or a legendary knight fight by your side is a massive help for beginners, taking the heat off you so you can heal or attack.

Limitless Potential: Whether you want to be a massive armored tank swinging a sword the size of a car, or a squishy wizard blasting enemies with cosmic lasers from across the room, the game accommodates every single playstyle.

🏆 The Final Verdict
Elden Ring didn't just teach me how to play a Soulsborne game; it completely rewired how I experience video games. It doesn't treat you like a child. It demands your respect, tests your perseverance, and rewards your bravery in ways no other game does.

It is a dark, beautiful, and unforgettable masterpiece.

Final Score: 10/10 – A generational leap in gaming.

If you, like me, have been terrified to try a Souls game, let this be your sign. Step out of your comfort zone. Arise, Tarnished. The Lands Between are waiting for you.

review:
Elden Ring (Elden Ring) is one of the most ambitious open-world action RPGs ever made. It takes the core “Souls” formula—methodical combat, challenging boss fights, and cryptic storytelling—and expands it into a vast open world that genuinely rewards curiosity. Exploration is the game’s biggest strength: stumbling onto hidden dungeons, optional bosses, or entirely new regions often feels organic rather than scripted, and the sense of discovery is consistently strong.

Combat is deep, flexible, and highly customizable. Whether you prefer heavy melee builds, magic-focused playstyles, or hybrid setups, the game gives you meaningful tools to approach encounters in different ways. Boss fights are a major highlight, often visually spectacular and mechanically demanding in a way that feels rewarding once you learn them. The world design also supports experimentation, letting players tackle challenges in multiple orders rather than forcing a strict path.

That said, Elden Ring isn’t without flaws. The difficulty can be uneven, with some bosses feeling dramatically harder or more polished than others. The open world, while impressive, can occasionally feel repetitive in its side content (similar dungeon layouts, reused bosses, etc.). And the game’s storytelling remains intentionally vague, which some players find immersive, but others find too opaque or emotionally distant.

Overall, Elden Ring is a landmark game that successfully translates the Souls formula into a massive open world without losing its identity. It’s not perfect, but its sense of scale, freedom, and challenge makes it one of the most memorable RPGs in years.

review:
# 🎮 Why *Elden Ring* is worth your time

Imagine stepping into a massive, haunting fantasy world where **every hill hides a secret, every ruin tells a story, and every enemy could destroy you**—unless you outsmart it.

That’s *Elden Ring*.

Developed by FromSoftware and shaped with worldbuilding by George R. R. Martin, this game takes the brutal combat of *Dark Souls* and drops it into a **fully open world called the Lands Between**, where you can go almost anywhere from the start. ([Wikipedia][1])

It’s not just a game—it’s a **challenge, an exploration sandbox, and a mystery you slowly unravel**.

---

# 🌍 The Core Experience (What You Actually Do)

* Explore a **huge open world** on foot or horseback (your mount “Torrent”)
* Fight enemies and bosses using **precise, skill-based combat**
* Build your character however you want (magic, swords, stealth, hybrid, etc.)
* Discover hidden dungeons, secrets, and lore without hand-holding
* Optionally play **co-op or PvP** with other players ([Wikipedia][1])

There are no quest markers telling you what to do next—**you figure it out**.

---

# 🔥 The Pros (Why people love it)

### 1. A truly massive, rewarding open world

This isn’t filler content. Exploration constantly pays off with weapons, bosses, and secrets. ([GameScribe Daily][2])
You’re encouraged to wander—and you’re almost always rewarded.

### 2. Deep, satisfying combat

Combat is **deliberate and skill-based**—timing, stamina, and positioning matter. ([TechRadar][3])
Every victory feels earned, not handed to you.

### 3. Huge build variety

You can play however you want:

* Heavy knight with a giant sword
* Spell-casting mage
* Agile rogue
* Weird hybrid builds

The flexibility is massive and replayable. ([Play Versus Games][4])

### 4. Rich lore and atmosphere

The story isn’t spoon-fed. Instead, you piece it together through:

* Item descriptions
* NPC dialogue
* Environmental storytelling

It creates a **mysterious, immersive vibe** unlike most games. ([GameScribe Daily][2])

### 5. Tons of content

There’s easily **100+ hours of gameplay**, with more than most players will ever fully complete. ([Trusted Reviews][5])

### 6. Genuine sense of accomplishment

Beating a boss in *Elden Ring* feels like:

> “I earned this.”

That emotional payoff is one of the game’s biggest selling points.

---

# ⚠️ The Cons (What might turn you off)

### 1. It is HARD—very hard

There’s no easy mode.
You will die. A lot.
That’s intentional design. ([Trusted Reviews][5])

### 2. Minimal guidance

The game barely explains:

* Where to go
* How quests work
* What stats do

New players can feel lost or overwhelmed. ([My Blog][6])

### 3. Story is vague

If you want a clear, cinematic narrative—you won’t get it.
You have to **interpret and piece things together yourself**.

### 4. Some repetition in side content

Not every dungeon or boss is unique—some reuse ideas or layouts. ([TechRadar][3])

### 5. Occasional technical issues

Minor performance hiccups or pop-in can happen, depending on platform. ([My Blog][6])

### 6. Not very accessible

* No difficulty settings
* Limited accessibility features
  This can exclude more casual or time-limited players. ([My Blog][6])

---

# 🧠 Real Player Perspective (from the community)

From Reddit, one player summed it up bluntly:

> “The feeling of wonder and triumph… is like nothing else” ([Reddit][7])

But others note:

* It can feel confusing or frustrating
* Some bosses or systems feel unfair or unclear ([Reddit][8])

So yeah—it’s not universally loved.

---

# 🏆 Final Pitch

If you want a game that:

* Respects your intelligence
* Doesn’t hold your hand
* Rewards persistence and curiosity
* Gives you unforgettable “I DID IT” moments

👉 *Elden Ring* is one of the best modern RPGs ever made.

But if you want:

* Relaxed gameplay
* Clear direction
* Easy progression

👉 This might frustrate you more than entertain you.

---

# 🎯 Bottom line

**Buy it if:**
You like challenge, exploration, and deep gameplay systems

**Skip it if:**
You hate dying, getting lost, or figuring things out yourself

---

If you want, tell me what kind of games you usually like, and I’ll tell you straight up whether *Elden Ring* will click with you or not.

review:
The exploration is incredibly tedious thanks to how broad and depth-less the open world in. Content is highly repetitive and you really have to search every nook and cranny to find items that are potentially extremely useful. In prior Souls games there were a limited number of paths, but you could always expect a path to lead to something interesting, useful, challenging, or any combination of those. In this game the open world is just there to waste your time as you mindlessly slog through your 4th crypt that both looks, feels, and plays exactly like the last three in the hopes that this one drops something you might need

Far too difficult compared to prior Souls games, and not in a way that I find engaging. Rather than demanding that you face challenges head on, the game throws absurd enemies in your way, then strongly encourages you to go around exploring the map until you can take down your opponent through mostly brute force. I got all the way to the sword-dog boss, couldn't beat him, then summoned and felt like I had cheated myself out of the legitimate souls experience and haven't been able to get back into it since. Sword-dog just had far too many attacks, extremely long sequences with small windows of opportunity for punishment or recovery, but summoning made it completely trivial because all the aggro got soaked up by my spare. Why do I have to pick between ferociously difficult or mind-numbing? The entire series was known for a hard, but fair philosophy that rewarded patience, and here we are with this stinker of a title

I went back and played through the bulk of Dark Souls 3 with the impression that I had just grown tired of the series, but my enjoyment for it hasn't faded at all

review:
What a weird game - exceptionally high highs, really low lows.

If this is your first FromSoft game (as I imagine it was for a majority of its players), this game will be an undisputed masterpiece.

For Soulsborne veterans, not so much. The game is still great, but its "merely" great.
If you've played your fair share of FromSoft games, you will inevitably feel how derivative Elden Ring can feel. 

"What's this spell? Oh its literally Lightning Spear. Hey look, its the Claymore! Same moveset and everything. And this item? Ah its Red Tearstone ring with a different name". Elden Ring is essentially Dark Souls 3 with some of Dark Souls 2's design ideology, wearing a Breath of the Wild costume. The open world is a fun addition, but I personally don't think it adds anything exceptionally meaningful to the formula. Having to scrounge around for resources to craft a basic fire pot is not mechanically interesting enough to warrant the extra time wasted doing so when in previous games you can just buy them off a merchant.

Then there's the balance. Of course brainless mongoloids will mindlessly yap about gitting gut (which is sometimes completely valid), but this ignores the problem the game has. Some builds will tremendously struggle and some will breeze through due to the design ideology of the enemies and bosses.

Enemies are fast, often use ambushes, and have combos that can almost 100% to 0% you. This goes X2 for bosses. Late game bosses are caricatures of themselves - they have a stupid amount of HP, they are insanely fast, they jump around the map while you impotently try to catch up, they are almost always huge so that locking on while they do their anime dashes makes it hard to keep track, and they can easily combo you from full to dead unless you have 50+ Vigor. 

UGS enjoyers will struggle immensely, UGS + no summons is basically unviable. The game very much seems to have been designed with summons/co-op in mind, which can be either good or bad depending on your preference.

The game at least looks phenomenal - not graphically, textures/effects/lightning basically haven't improved since DS3. The game looks incredible aesthetically. The Liurnia landscape is a painting (literally), and Nokron is one of the most breathtakingly beautiful spaces I have ever seen in a video game.

The endings are underwhelming, but of course they are - that's GRRM's signature move.


TL;DR - a masterpiece of normies, a pretty good if flawed entry for seasoned FromSoft players

review:
Elden Ring is different from previous Souls games, but it as amazing - the developers achieved expanding the Souls gameplay fomula without destroying the core.

If I had to describe the game in simple terms, I would say it is a bit like Dark Souls + Far Cry. There is a ton of exploration that gets rewarded, lots of POIs and map objectives and it all sits on top of the core Souls combat system.

The game looks AMAZING, it is stylish and still keeps a dark and brooding atmosphere.
The game is HUGE compared to other Souls games, there are so many placed to explore, they all look great and different and there is always something to find there - NPCs, bosses, loot, ... The first playthrough (whole map explored, most of the loot, almost all achivements) took my about 100hrs + ~30hrs for a NG+ playthrough to unlock all missing endings and do all NPC quest lines.
The game brings a lot of QoL improvements compared to older Souls games, the gameplay overall is very smooth. Stakes of Marika alone save so much time and frustration. And there are some new features like Ashes of War, Spirit Summons and crafting.
The music is good, but I would rank it below all 4 Soulsborne games, not by a lot, but still. A lot of the soundtracks are "generic epic music", but it has two or three special tracks.
Bosses overall are more difficult compared to previous titles - the bosses are more aggressive and active and a lot of them have mutliple phases.
The boss design in this game sometimes is quite lazy though and can be very frustrating:
- Input reading
- Unnaturally delayed attacks
- Very, very long attack combos that leave very little time to attack yourself
- Attack combos that look the same but add extra moves when it is convenient
- Animation & stagger cancelling !!!
The game was entirely completable with a purist solo melee-only build, but quite frustrating at several points. There is only one boss I didnt manage to beat on my first playthrough without using a Spirit Ash, but finally managed it on NG+ with the purist build after a dozen tries. And its not that the boss is just difficult, it is simply unfair (read issues above, especially the stagger cancelling is unheard of - the boss staggers, but cancels the stagger animation to instead hit you before you can finish your own attack trying to take advantage of the stagger - wtf).
At the same time, the game is also completable for casuals and players new to the series - there are dozens of buffs that can be applied before and during a fight, there is a ton of strong ranged options and players can summon Spirit Ashes and other players for help. 

And this gets me to the few negative aspects (on top of some lazy boss design).
While I applaud the effort to make the game more accessible to new players, I think the difference between using and not using the tools the game provides you with, is too big - there is just too much "possible" help in the game and it really trivializes most bosses - many bosses can be soloed by your summons. When I summoned a Spirit Ash for the aforementioned boss fight, I suddenly managed it first try, without changing anything else.
The game feels less tight and a bit less well designed compares to other Souls games - towards the end the game started to drag a bit and a lot of map design decisions feel a bit random and less deliberate. But thats to be expected when the size of the game increased so much.
And as always, there are some issues with input lag/ignoring, but combat overall feels very fluid and smooth.

I cant really complain much, the game is amazing, almost perfect, Game of the Year 2022 was well deserved.

seriously though, this game has no business being as massive as it is, so much so, that by the end of it , it felt like i've just gone though 3 fromsoft games back to back ! 8/10 !  as cool as it is , sometimes less is more.

review:
I wish steam had a mixed review option
Is it a good game? Yes
Is it a good FromSoftware game? No

This was the first time I was disappointed in a FromSoft game back when it came out in 2022. Bought it again this year to give it another chance and see if it changed my mind, but it didn't, even after using a busted build for the whole playthrough (poise breaking STR build with frenzy spells on the side).

The game is not as fun as the previous titles and doesn't try anything new or interesting to change the action/combat loop like they did with Bloodborne or Sekiro, and gave us Dark Souls IV: Open World. Elden Ring is like Skyrim, it's a deeply flawed and weaker game that will forever be remembered because of its aesthetic and art direction.

review:
Elden Ring is one of those games that doesn’t try to impress you — it just throws you in and lets you figure things out on your own. There’s no proper direction, no step-by-step guide, and no one telling you what to do next. At the start, it feels confusing and a bit unfair, but slowly you begin to understand how everything works… and that’s when it clicks. You die a lot, you get stuck, and you question your build, but every time you come back a little better. And when you finally beat something that once felt impossible, it actually feels earned.

The freedom is what makes it special. You can play however you want, build your character however you like, and explore at your own pace without being forced into a single path. The world is quiet, sometimes beautiful, sometimes unsettling, but always interesting — every area feels unique, and there’s always something hidden waiting to be discovered. It’s not an easy game, and definitely not for everyone, but if you stick with it, it becomes something unforgettable. After finishing everything, I can confidently say it’s one of the best games I’ve ever played.

review:
Elden Ring is a good game. Extremely overrated and overhyped but still a good game.

The game is praised for its open world but the world has nothing to offer besides Legacy Dungeons and pretty views. The rewards you often get for exploring is combat. You can't even use so many spells and weapons because your build doesn't allow for it. The starting open area is the best one but every thing that comes after is a dip in quality. The starting Legacy Dungeon is also the best and densest. FromSoftware's fixation on making everything vague just for the sake of it harms the game rather than making it more immersive, fun and enjoyable. Most of the time you are running across an open area hoping to find something good but again, all you are rewarded with is the beautiful art of this game and not something that immerses you in the world.

Going from level-based game design to a fully fledged open world also requires you to add some interesting content in the open world. Hiding a large area that you accidentally found whilst exploring is fun until you realise that area had basically nothing and you just rode across your horse for a boss fight. There are so many areas with books scattered around so why can't I read them? Why do I always have to read item descriptions to understand what's going on? Those items often provide only a line or two worth of lore. This stupid game design needs to change if they are making an open world game.

The smaller dungeons follow a very basic formula. You go in, explore until you find a lever, that lever unlocks the boss room, kill the boss. That boss you fight is either the easiest part of the dungeon or the most stupid enemy-type that ever graced your screen. Often times you can just trade blows with the boss but sometimes they put a boss which is too big for that arena, has no stagger or duo bosses which are not designed to be a duo. There really are no good small dungeon bosses in this game.

The first Legacy Dungeon, Stormveil Castle, is the best one. The second one, Raya Lucaria, is also good but not as good as the first one. The third one, Volcano Manor, had a lot of potential but it's quite short. The fourth one, Leyndell, is huge but again, not a lot to do there except kill enemies or find something that doesn't go well with your build. The last one, Elphael, is also good but it's not close to Stormveil. There are no Legacy Dungeons (or at least they are not good enough to be called that) in two main areas. 

I am not too happy with the bosses in this game. Many ♥♥♥♥ bosses have been copy and pasted in different areas. You fight the same ♥♥♥♥ boss multiple times. I have no idea how they thought that THIS boss is good enough that players won't mind fighting them for the 5th time. Most of the main bosses are good and it's fun to learn their moveset but some bosses have complete ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ attacks and hitboxes. The first main boss you fight is infamous for his delayed attacks and relentless combos. I think DS3 found a really good balance between the player speed and the boss' speed but Elden Ring is more in favor of the bosses rather than the player. I don't enjoy dodging a 5-10 move combo for 5-10 seconds just to land one attack. There are not many dodge and attack windows in this game (unlike DS1 and DS3). You dodge,  dodge, dodge, dodge, attack, dodge, dodge, attack. Not fun.

All of this criticism doesn't mean the game isn't good or fun. I had a blast on both of my playthroughs but Elden Ring, in many ways, is a step back for FromSoftware and it not as great as everyone makes it out to be.

One thing Elden Ring excels at is build variety. You have so many options to go for and change your playstyle. There are so many different types of weapons like swords, greatswords, colossal swords, daggers, katanas, whips, axes, greataxes, freaking perfume bottles, wands and even shields to play from. Not to mention all the cool armor sets you can mess around with to create a pretty unique looking character. 

Despite all the negativity in this review, I will still give Elden Ring a 8.5/10. It's a good game but not the pinnacle of open world games. Not even close to that.

review:
Since day one player, over 1300 hours, one of my top most played games, lots of time streaming and making memories with friends AND NOW...no more.

For the TLDR, game bad. Carless greedy company, the worst gambling of loot boxes I've ever seen, no concern for the quality of experience for the players, laughable attempts of quashing the constant surge of cheaters, severly limited servers with no option of choice, insane ratio of in game currency value to the dollar and on top of all that is an extreme lapse of quality of in game content.

Understandably, there are many different points of view and likes accross many different personalities so there is great varience in opinions and therefore not everything is for everyone. Aside from previous satement there is still some measure of quality that is objective and I'd like to dive into those over personal feelings or experience.

Most importantly is quality of Publisher/Developer. Naturally, the ideals and desires of the two are often differing, conflicting, independant, selfish. Why not, one will care more about face, money and status while the other concerns themselves with quality of product and it's users. Uncertain as to which one is more reasponsible for the copious negatives BUT there is almost nothing possitive to say about anything purtaining to the responsibilities of quality in game that either company oversee. Gambling is quite possibly one of the worst things to be implemented in casual and competitive gaming in order to create a strive for goals and the rewards of their completion and PUBG is without a doubt the worst one in the gaming industry containing: Several different tabs to visit to obtain in game cosmetics, a couple different unique currency PER TAB, obscure ways to obtain said currency, painfully slow gain of currency, the value of the items woeully insignificant to the total value of game time and possibility to earn, multiple loot box systems with a miniscule chance to earn anything valuable, grotesquely unfair ratio of covertion rate of real dollar to premium currency with next to no way to gain outside of spending real money.

There remains a fair deal of cosmetic content to gain without dropping a single dollar and it REMAINS a choice for the user if they wish, which I sadly have chosen and lament the thought of calculating that total value. Possibly sitting somewhere in the highest of tripple diggits. As it stands, not an ounce of this game is pay to win, all of the currencies prelude to just cosmetics and PUBG has some of the flashiest character designs, gun vehicle and even lobby skins BUT unlocking them is akin to an enternity of scraping your nails on a chalkboard and hoping that the next minute might yield gold.

Another point is the game mechanics. As a drop in, loot, survive and winner winner chicken dinner it is safe to say that, while not being the first in it's genre, PUBG has set the ultimate ground work and become synonymous with the game type, Battle Royal. Veritably the most saddest example of a "where are they now?" scenario like a slap in the face is that of PUBG now, similar to discovering your childhood hero is infact in rehab for various illegal activities. Which isn't to say the overabundance of cosmetics and crossover deals has interfered much at all with the overall game experience, just look at fortnite, everyone loves a bit of whimsy in their lives. Saddly the level of detail in gunplay, the strategey unique in many Mil Sims, the squad based gameplay and skill levels and outcomes have ALL been on a rapid decline. Quality of gameplay systematically making way for the glam and glitter designed to distract players into painfully forcing themselves into the meat grinder with the epitath of "just one more".

Futhermore understandable is the claim of "get good" in a game of competitive challenge. With most games, a progression from beginner to top player is meant to scale with the player, combining challenge with reward further cultivating an overall positive experience which provides opportunity to "get good". However many new player, returning player or just a casual drop in and out player are met with matchups agaisnt skill levels that belong only in the eSports level, denying the first five to ten steps required to obtain "good" status. Personally to say after 1300+ hours I have very little skill to show for time spent, of which at one point I commited time and effort to train myself. Learning in game physics, selecting guns to master an understanding of, developing of tactics and even perfection of the drop system, like the broken shattered dreams laid before me I am left with naught but ruin and frustration. After all that I'd like to point out, only ever casual gaming, never trying for anything other than drop in and play. That level of standard for a game is grotesque in it's entirety, solidifying the game primarily as a private club of people built only on the extremely skilled player. 

Throughout the many years PUBG has been alive, it has seen far more than it's fair share of cheaters. Myself and friends of mine have personally seen some of the worst cheating in our gaming experiences, from the simple terrain cliping or expliot, wall hacks, auto aim, zero recoil to some severe diobolical cheating. I personally witenessed a kill cam reveal a player from across the map, lock instantly onto various people positions throughout the map, move through ground, wall and building in seconds to end up right behind me. While the appeal of a free game is very attractive to many players, there is always a little caveat of the previously mentioned problems such as high loot box focus, agressive battle pass, small to moderate percentage of the game locked away ABOVE ALL lies the biggest achilies heel in the form of cheaters. There is ZERO recompense for these cheaters, bans easy to work back around, accounts to be made as infinatly as you can create emails. Understandably there can be little done sometimes, if someone wants to cheat and ruin other players games, they are going to BUT one simple way that can stop cheaters or at least a vast majority of them is a game price. You can't bounce back from a ban anywhere near as easily if it costs you financially.

Finally as a nail in the coffin, I have not once seen a game require in order to simply use a feature of it you phone number, personal phone number, information which is only likely needed to track or steal information. Security is an extremely garbage reason to require personal information in order to play ranked modes as opposed to quick match. Systems across the whole of game space in the entirety of ever have never once needed that type of feature and it should come as a precursor for what either the game devs or publishers really care about which is tracking it's users.

Whilst personaly I've had my own ups and downs, possitive and negative memories, pros and cons, loves and hates and despite this length of a review, I could fill this space double over with my own personal thoughts and experiences. This game and it's makers care and hope for only one thing, the money they can bleed out of it's users while they distract players with the flashy lights of pretty outfits and instill false hope that the random chances are remotely achieveable. Solid 0/10, this game deserves to be laid to rest.

review:
PUBG sits at the top of the charts with the kind of consistency that demands acknowledgment even from people who have zero interest in playing it, and I am one of those people.

Krafton essentially invented the modern battle royale template with this one. Everything from Fortnite to Warzone to every other drop in and survive game that followed owes its existence to what PUBG established, and that legacy is real regardless of how you feel about the genre it spawned. Being the blueprint matters even if the blueprint isn't your thing.

The problem is that being first and being best are different conversations and PUBG has always sat more comfortably in the former than the latter. The graphics have improved from the genuinely rough early access state but never quite shed that utilitarian quality that made the original feel like a tech demo that accidentally became a phenomenon. For a game that charges what it charges and has the playerbase it has the presentation has always felt like it was running to catch up with its own popularity.

The genre itself is the real barrier for a lot of people. Dropping into a shrinking zone with a hundred strangers and spending the first ten minutes finding a weapon is a specific kind of tension that either hooks you completely or leaves you cold, and there is no in between. If it doesn't grab you in the first few hours it never will.

Battlefield scratches the large scale multiplayer itch with actual structure, defined roles, vehicles, and objectives that make every match feel purposeful rather than arbitrary. That's a completely defensible preference and not one that requires much of a fight to justify.

review:
[h1]Overall Verdict: The Only Battle Royale That Actually Scratches the Itch[/h1]

I came to [i]PUBG[/i] looking for a break from the other "popular" battle royales, and honestly, none of them ever scratched the itch quite like this one. There is a specific kind of intensity here—especially in the final circles where everyone is locked in holding sniper angles—that is just unmatched.

[h2]My Experience[/h2]

At the start, I spent a lot of time just fighting the map itself. I’d get so caught up in the sheer scale of the world that I wouldn't pay attention to the time or the encroaching circle, leading to a lot of preventable deaths. But, once I got the hang of it, that same massive map became a total sniper’s heaven. 

The difficulty curve, however, is wild. I’ve had sessions where I’ve cleared 20+ kills, feeling completely invincible, and other days where I struggled to find even a single kill. The jump in difficulty between lobbies is insane, which makes for a pretty jarring experience sometimes.

[h2]The Good & The Bad[/h2]

[b]Pros:[/b]
[list]
[*] [b]Unrivaled Intensity:[/b] The final circles are genuinely heart-pounding.
[*] [b]Sniper Gameplay:[/b] The map design makes for the best long-range combat in the genre.
[*] [b]Satisfying Highs:[/b] When the game clicks, it is an incredible rush that other BRs just don't deliver.
[/list]

[b]Cons:[/b]
[list]
[*] [b]Matchmaking Inconsistency:[/b] The skill gap feels massive; some days I'm dominating, other days I'm getting stomped immediately.
[*] [b]Mode Fragmentation:[/b] While you can toggle into FPP while playing TPP, it’s frustrating that some modes are strictly locked to FPP only. I really wish there was more flexibility to choose your perspective across all modes.
[*] [b]Confusing UI:[/b] Personally, I really don't like the menu system. It feels confusing, messy, and way too busy.
[*] [b]Ranked Access:[/b] I've never even been able to queue for a ranked game; the mode just doesn't work for me at all.
[*] [b]Technical Concerns:[/b] I’ve definitely had moments where I had to report players for what felt like blatant cheating.
[/list]

[h2]Verdict[/h2]

If you want a BR that feels tactical and rewards patience over pure twitch reflexes, this is the one. It’s flawed, and the matchmaking can be a rollercoaster, but when it’s good, it remains the gold standard for the genre.

review:
7 Years of Loyalty, 0 Days of Optimization
I’ve been playing this game since 2017, right from the day it launched. I’ve stuck with it through every meta and every map, but my patience is finally running out. It’s painful to watch a game with such unique gunplay being slowly killed by corporate greed and poor technical management.

The Reality Check:
Performance & Freezes: It is 2026, and we are still dealing with stuttering, random freezes, and general performance issues. I have a high-end build, yet the game feels like it’s struggling to breathe. Why is optimization always at the bottom of the priority list?

Skins over Substance: Instead of fixing the core mechanics or addressing the long-overdue move to a new Unreal Engine, the developers are obsessed with pumping out "sloppy" skins and ridiculous cosmetics that ruin the game's atmosphere. We wanted a tactical battle royale, not a digital fashion show.

The "Engine" Excuse: We keep hearing how "difficult" it is to transition to a modern engine. Krafton is a multi-billion dollar company. Smaller studios manage to upgrade their tech, but here we are, stuck on an outdated foundation because the focus is strictly on monetization.

Killing the Vibe: The game is losing its identity. Every update brings more "nonsense" content and less stability.

Verdict: If you’re a veteran from 2017, you know exactly what I’m talking about. We love the core of PUBG, but the management is running it into the ground.

Dear Developers: Stop painting dresses and start fixing the engine.


"I cannot recommend this game in its current state. As someone who has been here since 2017, it's heartbreaking to see the devs prioritize K-pop skins and expensive progressive crates over basic game stability. We are still waiting for the UE5 transition while the current client suffers from performance drops and freezes. Great gunplay, terrible management."

The realism is one of the strongest aspects. Gunplay feels grounded, and every fight has weight to it. Movement, animations, and general responsiveness sometimes feel outdated compared to newer titles, which can be frustrating in intense moments.Despite that, PUBG still has something special. Playing with friends creates unforgettable moments, whether you’re messing around or trying to win seriously. The tension, the pacing, and those high-stakes endgames keep you coming back.I would recommend it if you enjoy realistic battle royale games and especially if you have friends to play with. review:
PUBG: Battlegrounds — 9/10
Before Fortnite. Before Warzone. Before any of them. There was PUBG, and it changed everything.
Say what you want about the state of battle royale today — every single one of those games owes its existence to what PUBG built from the ground up. The 100 players dropping on an island, the shrinking circle, the looting, the tension of the final ring — PUBG invented the template that an entire genre just copy-pasted and dressed up differently. This is where it all started.
And honestly? It still plays better than most of its imitators. There's a weight and grittiness to PUBG that nobody has quite managed to replicate. The gunplay feels real and punishing. Bullets have travel time and drop. You can't just spray and pray — you have to think about range, positioning, cover, vehicle sounds in the distance, footsteps on the floor above you. Every engagement feels genuinely tense in a way that flashier battle royales rarely achieve.
Erangel is still one of the best maps ever designed for the genre. Open fields, dense towns, chokepoints, rivers — it forces every kind of combat and never feels unfair. The other maps each bring something different to the table and after all these years there's still a ridiculous amount of variety.
Yes it had a rough early life. Yes the optimization has been a journey. But as a pure, unfiltered battle royale experience built on real tactical foundations, nothing touches the original.
The genre grew up and moved on. But the peak? It was always here.
9/10 — The original. The blueprint. Still the best.

Imagine spending 20 minutes playing "Interior Designer" in a derelict Russian shed, meticulously collecting a Level 3 backpack and a suppressed M416, only to be erased from existence by a guy laying in a bush with a crossbow.

Why You'll Hate It:
The Physics: Vehicles behave like they were programmed by someone who has only ever heard physics described over a bad phone line. Hit a pebble? Your car is now a satellite.

The Combat: You don't "fight." You loiter in a bathroom for ten minutes until the Blue Zone—the game's personification of tax season—forces you out to get shot by a 12-year-old with the reflexes of a cobra on meth.

The Optimization: It runs like a brick through a blender. Your PC will scream in agony just to render a texture that looks like wet cardboard.

The Verdict:
It’s a toxic cycle of looting, running, and dying to things you can't see. It’s miserable, unfair, and technically broken.

I'll see you in the lobby in five minutes.

With realistic weapons, intense combat mechanics, and a shrinking battlefield, every match is packed with suspense. Players must loot for gear, strategize, and use the environment to their advantage, while staying ahead of the deadly blue zone. The game’s graphics and sound design are immersive, and while it has occasional performance issues, it's optimized for a wide range of PCs. However, the community can be toxic, and newcomers may find the learning curve steep. Overall, PUBG remains one of the top battle royales, offering depth and excitement for fans of strategic shooters.

Pros: Tactical gameplay, great maps, intense action, regular updates.
Cons: Steep learning curve, occasional performance issues, toxic community.

A must-try for battle royale fans!

review:
PUBG is not perfect. I've played from the E.A. days so I've seen how bad things have been as well as seen the positives.

Pros: Game has a grounded feel, not super-crazy movement hell for more slow paced gameplay. The hit detection has been massively improved and rarely feels off now. QoL has gotten much higher without killing how the game feels. Personally, I also like the near-instant TTK as long as you have the skills to pull it off. Guns feel mostly varied and solid to use, nothing in the game besides melee weps are nearly useless.

Cons: Some games, RNG screws you so you get garbage / ring spawns on the other side of the map and you travel 15 minutes to the other side just to be instantly killed reaching the ring after 0 action. I personally haven't noticed many cheaters but you'll usually run into one within a few hrs of playtime and others seem to complain of it a lot more. Half of the maps are reaaaally not good with far too much area to span with low vehicle numbers or no cover or just flat out play like butt.

Overall it is a game that has been fixed up from the early days and somewhat forgotten since. It's a game where I instantly understand when someone doesn't like it or feel a mutual rush when a friend hits a nasty shot. There's a lot of good and bad in PUBG but I'd say with some patience you can learn to vibe here.

review:
Hours Played: 320+
Emotional Stability Remaining: Low but operational
“One More Game” Lies Told: Enough to ruin sleep schedules

PUBG was my first proper keyboard-and-mouse shooter back in 2018 when I built my first PC. It wasn’t just a game - it was my introduction to getting humbled by strangers from 400 metres away.

PUBG is a slow-burn paranoia simulator disguised as a battle royale.
It’s 20 minutes of looting, 5 minutes of repositioning, and 0.4 seconds of getting headshot by someone you never saw.

The gunplay? Heavy.
The recoil? Personal.
The footsteps? Louder than your heartbeat.

This isn’t slide-cancel chaos. It’s tactical, punishing, and sometimes unfair - which is exactly why winning feels insane.

I’m running top-of-the-line hardware, a clean Windows install, and fully updated drivers yet this game constantly suffers from micro stutters.

This is NOT a hardware issue and NOT a software configuration problem. The problem is the game itself. PUBG runs on an outdated engine that clearly struggles with modern high-end systems and high refresh rate monitors. Frame pacing is inconsistent, and the experience never feels truly smooth, even on the best possible setup.

The game used to run much better in the past, but after recent updates the performance and smoothness have noticeably degraded.

If you’re using modern high-end hardware, don’t expect a flawless experience. The engine needs serious optimization.

review:
100% classic Battle royal, I've played since release or maybe it was open beta. This battle royal is the roots to many BR's, but it came after H1Z1 and this is how it plays: there are lean mechanics, you can choose games of third person or first, the movements are blocky, but pretty fair and  skill based. The player base is the only downside to the game as the match making isn't fun due to cheaters, robots, or people who've had a lot of hours consistently. There are many game modes and not every game will you face constantly max level players so for the times you don't face cheaters or insane players you have great games that are extremely complex with different ways the fights go down

Example: I've ran over people with vehicles, done drive-by in the vehicles of the game, been killed by people shooting out of the plane vehicles, had enemies and teammates crushed by the "Deployable cover flair" when it drops 2 large cement structures very fast, killed 10 players with crossbows (hard difficulty)(achievement), grenades  of many different varieties (including blue zone grenades - copied from fortnite, but good move), deaths from the many different special weapons that drop from the red crates, and so much more.

One map had a gulag, but i think it's out of rotation now, but they instead also took the concept of being revived by teammates just as other BR's did and that is a good  change.

The game mode of 88 bots and 12 players is the most enjoyable to get warm and ready to face harder opponents. I hope for any of you reading this that you get to play versus people your own level. Whatever you do, dont let your first game be with that lvl 100+  friend... ENJOY IT WHILE YOU CAN.

There is a team deathmatch game mode, it takes place in small circles of random spots on the actual maps so that's awesome for getting warmed up

The games are exhilarating, Balanced well to where all guns are useful, you can choose to play safe, slow, fast, like a rat, or  however you like. Ghillie suits are a thing and they're fun. 

I've only seen 3 bad changes and they were map changes, but the new items added, new vehicles, skins, gameplay (Except the Match Making), emotes, etc... is all pretty great.


Tell me your thoughts?

review:
This game has gone through some wild shifts and changes over the years, and it is a battle royale first and foremost above all others, so if that is not your vibe, steer clear. 

That said, for a free to play adaptation from a pay to play core, PUBG has thrived since it's initial ups and downs of finding a good foothold in a market that quickly got overtaken by flashier and more cartoony competitors.  

This is a great game to just casually knock out a few drops with some friends or goof off in solo que doing self imposed absurd challenges, and the casual mode doubly makes it much less of a chance of just getting completely stomped by people who eat sleep and breath the game while your just trying to play with people of varying skill level and interest in the game. 

As it stands now, it is a solid free to play BR option for the people who prefer less cartoony asthetics with more realistic style firearms. It certainly is not a full hardcore vibe on the realistic feel so won't really scratch the itch there, but overall it has a lot going for it in it's specific niche.  The competitive scene is also quite impressive and for such a random variable element of a game that often comes down to luck over skill in terms of equipment strategies, it is still very entertaining to watch in spite of the inherent lack of a certain degree of mechanical balance optimization just due to the nature of the game itself. 

There are also very amusing custom lobbies that can be tweaked into all kinds of improvised game modes for very silly group fun if your lucky enough to be part of a community that can muster the numbers to host such a thing! 

The journey of improvement and refinement is a constant one that never truly ends however, and these are the things I think could do with some changes to improve the quality of the game and extend it's reach even further: 

[b] CASUAL REWORKS [/b] 

Change casual ques to 90min instead of 60min and allow groups who are queing together to go off the time remaining of the group member with the most time remaining, even if other group members have 0 time remaining themselves. people meet up and decide to do a little casual play at different points in the day, in different circles, this helps make this process not feel like a time triage excercise similar to scheduling DnD groups 

[b] BOT DROP [/b]

Adding a purely co-op comp stomp mode would be very helpful for those of us who enjoy playing casuals but do not want the human competitive element in any way beyond friendly team competition to see who can get the most kills / coolest kills. 

Someone like me can go toe to toe easily in the main matches and the "who's a bot and who's not" excitement of casuals is something I can handle easily enough, but for a lot of people I play with who enjoy a purely casual against bots experience that is not high pressure so they can be comfortable and spend time with friends and loved ones without getting upset or too overly stressed, something like this would be much better: 

The maps are much smaller environments (they can just be zoomed in sections of already existing maps to start with, but eventually custom designed maps using pre-existing assets would be the best course of action to help with differentation at a glance and variety) 

The game mode would be 100 participants, with up to 6 six slots occupied by players in one team replacing a bot spawn from either a pre-que or public lobby (with players still able to launch on less than max numbers) 

Everyone parachutes in at the same time in random spots in the sky above the map and have to steer themselves into locations alongside all the bots, and then it's just a free for all as the bots do not have teams, but they always priortize engaging human players over one another (and are generally coded to miss each other as much as possible when fighting one another) they will never kill downed players intentionally, and as the match progresses and the circle tightens, the bots become universally stronger, more well equipped, and deadlier, all while ensuring their top priority is to avoid the closing circle, up to the point of not stopping to engage until they are inside of it at all costs (and also reducing outside of circle damage to the bots so a ton of them don't just die out there like in casuals) 

I could elaborate a great deal on more specifics and unique elements of this game mode, but this review only has so much room so if the devs would like to contact me directly, I would happily provide additional expansion and development plans to streamline it's inception!

[b] FAT TRIMMING [/b] 

 There is soooo much overlapping emergency key jingling from this games years of desperate reinvention and expansion that needs cleaning up. There are so many absurd sub menus and six different currencies doing the same things that could all be solved by one that make the monetized elements far more complex and annoying to engage with. 

Making a "give me all my stuff I qualify for" button that just lists a history of where and why a thing was given to the player is so much more effective and gives people a less micro management heavy way of cleanly aquiring things. It should also store and log those qualified rewards until that button IS clicked even if the event or whatever was going on has passed, so that people don't accidentally miss out on things they qualified for simply because of an arbitrary timer. 

In addition, make the actual BP shop it's own thing that makes it easier to navigate what is available purely with BP without mixing it with the premium currency shop. You can still put advertisements in the BP shop items directing too premium shop versions, and you can add a premium shop currency buy options for all the BP items too even, but make the designation more clear, and simple to navigate 

Again, I have a massive list of UI reconfigurations and changes to offer in great detail, along with many mechanic revisions and refinements, if the developers are interested. I understand the limitations of development especially on a skeleton crewed F2P investment anchor, but I know how to work on nothing budgets with little resources, so happy to offer my expertise if desired! 

[b] NO MORE SOLO DANCES [/b] 

This one is mildly irksome, but ALL dances of every kind should be group dances. This is a nitpick I will admit it, but it's just unnecissarily exclusionary in a largely team incentivizing game. Add a toggle when activating the dance for it to be solo only if you really want to keep that for more mean spirited players, but don't enforce exclusion. 

I could go on an on and on about ideas for new events, tweaks and optimizations, promotional tie ins and baseline improvements but, again, due to limited space here, I'll just end on saying this game is not perfect by far, and it will be infuriating at times both because of the PVP nature of the game, and some of the games baked in elements at it's core, but if you want a slower paced BR That has less frenetic energy than other BR's and Arena Shooters while still being an engaging experience with a bit more of a "real life" influence, this one is the game for you! And it's free, and there are random little events that pop up free game modes to play with so, it's worth keeping installed just to mess around with now and then!
