review: This is my third review of this game on this store page. The first time I reviewed the game with an extremely positive outlook and message about the game. Once I then edited it for the first time, I got a lot more vile and negative about the game. I want to come on here and do a proper review instead of basing it off of old fun I had on the video game or anger/bs I have dealt with on the game within the last x amount of hours I have put into it. I have played this game since it came out just about. I believe the first time I played this game was early 2016 if not late 2015. I have played over a few steam accounts, on my Xbox quite a lot, and even on my phone. I have also spent a lot of time watching some of my favorite streamers play it (mainly Shroud). I have a lot of love for this game. I love its visuals, I love the weapons, most of the gunplay is quite enjoyable, the movement is quite nice, etc. However, this game has a lot of issues that lie underneath its surface. These issues lie mainly within the community, but some are indeed within the game itself. The first main issue is the cheating, and yes I'm sure a lot of you will read that and think I'm just complaining, have no skill, and got destroyed one time and needed to come on here and mention it. That is not the case. Currently, I am 24 years old. I have spent a majority of my life gaming, on all types of games from survival games, roguelikes, city builders, shooters, so on and so forth. I have tried most TYPES of games that are on the market. I have spent time gaming on numerous platforms.. games have cheaters. Simple as that. It has been proven. Millions and millions of players have been banned from games for cheating, and a lot of the same cheaters make new accounts constantly to continue cheating, even if they have to pay. The only way this is usually stopped is by banning someone through a device ban, IP ban, or some more extensive bans that require a little more of a look into how the person is doing their cheating. It is a problem. In so many games. Unfortunately in some of my most loved games, including PUBG and Counter-Strike, the cheating is quite high up there. With high numbers of cheaters and low successful attempts to get rid of said cheaters. These people exist in high numbers. They show themselves doing so on social media platforms even. They do not care, they are relentless. I recently came onto the community hub of this game to do some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ about it. I will say, It was mainly out of anger, and I went a little far with what I had said. But I had a few gentlemen comment on said forum saying some things that got me thinking. They fought what I had said so hard, coming at me for the way I spoke, for my "lack of skill in the game" that they certainly know because they have TOTALLY watched me play the game, and even trying to attack my person in saying I am immature, my behavior is "inadequate" etc. In this lies another one of the main issues within the games community. The ignorance, and the toxicity. The same ignorance and toxicity that seems to lie within a majority of the population, over any subject that they believe they know every little thing on, while doing little to no research. It baffles me that the world has come to what it has. The people who sit on the internet all day long, commenting to people and/or making their own posts, with full ignorance while trying to call out someone else's ignorance. Everyone has a little ignorance, of course, no one knows absolutely everything. It is just absolutely appalling to me how some people could go into a subject, with absolutely no information or research to back their opinions, just solely based off of how they feel because they think they know it all, and absolutely attack others character, opinions, and ideals over it. Sit down, do a little bit of research, figure out that the world sucks and people destroy things simply for fun. It exists, it sucks, but it does. Don't attack people for the way they feel. And then ironically continue to call that person immature, and inadequate while you are actively showing immaturity and inadequacy in your behavior. Stop reflecting the way you feel about yourself onto someone else who you have never stepped foot near, never spoken a real word to, and never sat down and had a true conversation with to understand what they have been through and what they think. As far as the issues that lie within the game itself, they are simple. The protection against cheaters is inadequate, they have taken away from the games realism by adding items that destroy the games core aspects, they have changed certain parts of the game that just absolutely strip it of its potential. it is saddening. All of this is saddening. And this spans over all of the MMOG video games that are on the markets these days, it hurts my heart. At this point in my gaming life, I will probably soon reach a point where I cannot continue to play largely MP games as it makes me lose hope for the people in these communities on a daily.

review:
[h1][b][i]Diary of a PUBG unc[/i][/b][/h1]
The first time I played [i]PUBG: Battlegrounds[/i] was back when it was still called [i]PlayerUnknown’s Battlegrounds[/i]—[b]you had to pay back then to be a toxic ass.[/b] Despite it being PvP, not my favorite hobby, it grabbed me by the balls, its grip firm yet gentle, and from September 2017 till July 2018 I spent around 250 hours looting, sneaking, killing, dying (mostly dying) and sometimes eating chicken.

I still remember queuing up for my first solo, riding the plane, being mesmerized by the giant map, all the funny sounding names, and one stood out—[b]school[/b]. I thought, [i]“School? Who would want to revisit childhood random boner trauma, as it greeted you in the middle of algebra class, you just sitting there, praying to whoever is listening up there, that the teacher doesn’t pick you to come up front and explain to everyone what the author really meant when he wrote: [b]“In men there are two wolves.”[/b]I’ll go there, it’ll be empty for sure!”[/i]

It was in fact, not empty

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3652187216
[h3][b][i]Hoarder Sim[/i][/b][/h3]
Now, the main reason I even stepped foot onto Erangel, all those years ago, was [b]the looting.[/b] It’s just so zen, yet it’s also one giant [i]risk-reward[/i] game: deciding what to carry with you, what to drop, what to swap out—[b]I get all tingly from just thinking about it.[/b]

But what loot do you loot?
[list]
[*][b]Weapons:[/b]
You need to kill people, and melee-ing is so passé. So, bullets are the answer—[b]headshots[/b] or [b]spray and pray[/b], dealer’s choice. As years passed, the game’s arsenal expanded, which for a loot monke like me, seeing so many guns all around me, it’s basically better than the sex! My current favorites are the [i]AUG[/i], [i]SCAR-L, VSS[/i] and [i]M249[/i]. The [i]Crossbow[/i] also gets a thumbs up—[b]very hard to kill people with, much dying in chaotic gunfights because it’s pretty useless in 99.999% of situations I found myself in.[/b] Looks cool tho! Oh, there’s also grenades. [b]Pro Tip:[/b] do try and kill the enemy if you are using frags, m’kay?

[*][b]Attachments:[/b]
Think of guns like puzzles with a piece or two missing—[b]that’s the attachments, the missing piece(s)![/b]. These little doohickeys are very important, like little [b]gun buffs[/b] if you will. They can improve [b]stability, recoil, magazine size, add scopes, etc.[/b] Hot take—[b]pimping out your gun with attachments is more fun than winning[/b]—(can’t) change my mind!
[/list]

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3652204471
[list]
[*][b]Consumables:[/b]
There’s many things to consume nowadays. Your favorite uncle consumes Fox News, you consume Hentai, and so on. In [i]PUBG[/i] you just kinda have first aid kits, bandages, etc. They heal the body and soul tho. But, there are exceptions—pills, [i](def not Monster)[/i] energy drinks, adrenaline shots, you can fill up on these and get a nice passive boost, pushing you past human limits. Why use first aid when you can heal by overdosing on pills and [i](not Monster)[/i] energy drinks? Plus, sounds cooler when the coroner reads your cause of death too.

[*][b]Gear:[/b]
Ok, so guns kill, attachments attach, and you consume consumables, what’s left? Well, I’m pretty naked right now, and don’t have deep pockets, so where all those [i](not Monster)[/i] energy drinks gonna go? What about ammo? Can my skull stop a Kar-98k bullet? Well, lucky for me, the map is littered with [b]helmets, vests, and backpacks[/b], all there to help my inner hoarder or protect me from other playerses nasty bulletses—[i]they want to steal my precious (loot)![/i] Gear come in three flavours, with Level 3 gear often found in hot zones or care packages dropped randomly during a match. Also, we now have a Jammer Pack. The standard [i]PUBG[/i] Jammer Pack does two things—[b]it’s a backpack and a jammer[/b]—stopping the nasty Blue Zone from hurting us… at least while its battery lasts that is.
[/list]

[h3][b][i]Two in the face, one in the ass’ base[/i][/b][/h3]
But you’re a man’s man’s man! You don’t play [i]PUBG[/i] to loot (I do tho), you play it to be the one who Chicken Dinners!

You have two options for dining, [b]Third Person[/b] and [b]First Person.[/b] Both allow [i]solo, duo and squads[/i]. And for those feeling extra masochistic, or just with big PP, you can drop [i]solo into duos/squads[/i] matches. I? Well, in the immortal words of Demi Lovato: [i]“I do it solo!”[/i]—[b]just me and my trusty right arm.[/b] But both duo and squads can be fun too!

Ok, so now we in: the plane is plane-ing and it's time to decide where to land. You’ll see many locations on the map, some with big names, some with smol names. [b]Big name = Big loot = Hot Zone.[/b] This is where I was taught the true essence of [i]PUBG[/i] combat—[b]don’t be a ♥♥♥♥♥![/b] I learned to loot faster, be more aggressive in gunfights, use my surroundings and audio cues to pinpoint players, and ofc, how to camp when enemies are all around me. In short, [b]hot zones[/b] are essential for your [i]PUBG[/i] growth, shaping men into slur-throwing boys—basically a [i]CoD[/i] lobby for the new generation: [i]“What?! How did he kill me? This ***** **** *** ***** ***** was clearly cheating! Report!”[/i] [b]—Average [i]PUBG[/i] enjoyer[/b]

Also, they have another [i]non-game[/i] related benefit that the penis doctor mafia doesn’t want you to know about—[b]landing in hot zones solved my ED.[/b] The surge of adrenaline, the blood pumping vigorously through [i]all[/i] the veins and arteries—[b]no magic pill can match it.[/b] So, next time, before laying down with [i]pillow-chan[/i], fire up a solo session and land in any hot zone, like [i]school[/i]—[b]you and the missus will thank me later.[/b]

And it’s when bullets start flying, and people start dying, [i]#rhyme[/i], that [i]PUBG[/i] turns into a fat lady and sings—it’s [i]perfection![/i] Even I, a loot monke, ended up loving the gunplay, despite sucking at it—[b]it’s just that damn addicting.[/b] Gunfights are a perfect showcase of your mastery of the game's mechanics and systems. Combat is not about who has the best or biggest gun—[i]brain[/i] beats [i]bullet[/i]! ... But [i]hack[/i] also sees [i]brain[/i] through the wall, 20km away, and can one-shot you, so…

In short, [i]PUBG[/i] gunfights are like getting ready for a date with that deSarmas girl from [i]Bladerunner[/i]. You pulled out all the stops, even bathed—[b]“Smell travels faster than light”[/b] —[i]Blindstein circa 2005 BC.[/i] You put on your best clothes, accentuating those love handles, so she knows you a good provider. And as soon as you step out, a Boeing 420 crash-lands on your head. It was an emergency landing, the pilot ate too much spicy Indian food and Boeing forgot to put toilets on their plane. You’re dead now and the toilet is clogged.

[h3][b][i]Chicken Winner[/i][/b][/h3]
But is [i]PUBG[/i] fun?

Well, let me tell you something you already know. [i]PUBG[/i] ain’t all sunshine and rainbows. It’s a very mean and nasty place, and I don’t care how skilled you are, it will send you back to the Lobby and keep you there permanently if you let it. You, me, or nobody is gonna hit as hard as [i]PUBG[/i]. But it ain’t about how many you kill. It’s about how many times you can die and keep playing. How many hacker headshots you can take and keep playing. That’s how [i]PUBG[/i]-ing is done!

In short, [i]PUBG[/i] fun.
I had fun.

I come back to this game at the very beginning of 2026 as my friend had just got a PC for Christmas and wanted to play so I joined in. review:
Used to play a lonnng time ago and quit due to hackers, a friend got me into PUBG Mobile and really liked it so came to try out the PC variant again.

I haven't seen anything crazy that makes me think people are hacking which is good and the game feels rather realistic except for the damage which is nice.

Everything seems fairly good, but the loot distribution is just absolute garbage and for a loot & shoot that's a nail in the coffin that earns it a Not Recommended.

The first thing I noticed in my 10 games was that no matter where you land, you basically immediately have dozens of sniper rifles and DMRs just laying absolutely everywhere. Like expect 3 KAR98s, an SLR, and a Dragunov all in the same rural loot BUILDING let alone area.

What this means is that you generally will NEVER use any weapon other than a Sniper/DMR. Its pointless to pick up almost any other gun except maybe SMGs JUST in case someone gets close, 90% of battles will be fought from greater than 100m as everyone just snipes at each other. And if you run with anything but a sniper rifle yourself you are just putting yourself at a disadvantage because you will NOT be able to compete against a sniper rifle for damage per shot.

It's really not very exciting gameplay and wasnt what I was looking for. I wanted more AR based fights

Sniper rifles and DMRs should be crate only or ultra rare so people actually can have some AR fights.

Speaking of, it takes SO many hits to kill compared to the mobile version and bullet velocity is so slow that people just sidestep bullets, games forgetting that a 300m shot should be damn near instantaneous.

Teams are just as bad here as in mobile, I selected My language only and only 3 teammates actually talked and ran with me in my 15 games.

In mobile, sniper rifles are pretty rare finds and most people end the game with just scoped ARs.

review:
PUBG – A game that works best when you bring the whole village
PUBG is a game I’ve played since release, and I’ve never played it the way the developers probably imagined. I’ve never been a solo guy, never a duo guy. PUBG is, in practice, a minimum three‑man game — and four is obviously best. Anything less feels like going to war with a water pistol and a smile. It’s simply not the same without a stable crew you know well.

The best thing about PUBG is the teamwork and the group dynamics. The laughter, the stupid mistakes, the idiotic situations, and the moments that are only funny when you’re playing with people you know. PUBG is basically a social simulator disguised as a battle royale. It’s not the game that makes it fun — it’s the people you play with.

Realism? No, we don’t do that here
Let’s be honest: PUBG is not realistic. Not even close. The vehicles behave like they’re made of rubber and low self‑esteem. Clothing has zero effect. The physics are… creative. But it doesn’t matter. The gunplay feels good, and it has improved over time. First‑person helps if you come from games like Battlefield, where the weapon feel is so good you almost want to send flowers to the developers.

Either way, PUBG is far better than CS, which gives me allergies, and miles better than Arma Reforger, which is absolutely terrible in that department.

The maps are great, and after many years with only one map, it’s actually refreshing to get some variety. Each map has its own challenges, and it keeps the game feeling fresh.

Tactics: Think ahead or die hungry
PUBG is tactically fantastic. If you don’t think ahead, you’re not getting any chicken dinner. The sound design is also excellent — with good headphones you hear enemies clearly, even though it’s usually just your teammate sneaking around and scaring the life out of you.

The pace used to be higher, with less loot and more desperation. I stopped playing for years, but came back because a friend wanted to try it again. I don’t regret it. The game is better now than it used to be, even if some people will swear the opposite. Opinions are like… well, you know.

Irritations: Almost gone
PUBG used to have growing pains, but now it mostly runs the way it should. I rarely bug, almost never crash, and the game feels stable. I think I’ve crashed once in six months.

I haven’t — as far as I know — encountered cheaters in a long time. It used to be worse, which is strange considering the game is now free. Free games usually attract unruly gamers from various countries who should probably spend more time on manners and education. It’s a bit like how certain countries’ behaviour in the world seems to rub off on their gamers online.

Balancing isn’t really a problem. Either you’re a noob, or you understand what’s going on. Eventually you get better than others, and then you play ranked.

Movement and controls: Still a bit stiff, but improved
PUBG has always had a slightly stiff feel, a bit like Arma, but it has improved over time. It probably comes from the Arma/DayZ mod heritage, but that’s just my theory. Either way, it’s much better now than before.

Teamwork: Alpha and omega (and minimum three players)
I always play with people I know. PUBG is, in practice, a minimum three‑man game, and four is obviously best. Playing with randoms only happens if we forget to turn off “fill squad,” and that usually reminds us why we don’t do that.

Communication is everything, spotting too. Pay attention to your colour, use it when you call things out, and stick with your group. That’s how you survive, and that’s how PUBG becomes the game it’s actually meant to be.

Conclusion
PUBG is a game that’s at its best when you play it with people you know. It’s tactical, fun, frustrating, social, and full of moments that only work when you have a regular crew to share them with.

review:
This used to be one of my favorite games. I played it years ago. I would sometimes tune into e-sports matches from time-to-time for this game when it first started getting big. I was good too. I would chicken dinner at least every 7 games. Sometimes, a few games in a row. Obviously, I'm not that good now and I haven't played in a long time.
I really want this game to be good again. I want to play this game again, but there were a series of steps which led to me posting a negative review now.

1. When they added new maps, but no one wanted to play those maps, so they took away the ability to choose which map to play on. Man, I just want to play on Erengal. That's the only map I want. 

2. I switched to Linux. I went back and forth between Linux and Windows. And I never really got the feeling to put effort into being good again after I couldn't choose which map I wanted to play (reason 1). So, I just never bothered to switch over to Windows just to play one game. This game is not Linux compatible. I assume it's due to the anticheat. 

3. I did switch back to Windows on my main gaming PC for a couple of months this year and decided to give PUBG another shot, but I found out that I have to deactivate a windows 11 security setting (Kernel Protection) to EVEN FRICKIN' PLAY THE GAME. Dude, I'm not making my PC less secure just to play one game. 

TLDR; To get me to play this game again, it would need to:
1. Allow choosing which map you play on
2. Gain Linux Support
3. Not use Kernel Level Anticheat or require me to make my system less secure just to play it.

review:
I remember playing this for one or two matches when it came out; it was a fun and interesting atmosphere at the time (2017). It seems as if PubG, however, has not only created a long-lasting Battle Royale trend in First-Person Shooter gaming, it has pushed gaming more-so into gambling and non-emergent gameplay. 

Many newer gamers now think First-Person Shooter games are about waiting for people to come into your line of sight. This problem of waiting for your opponent to come to you goes beyond 'camping'. It actually speaks to how the common-sense military tactics (flanking, moving as a group, having an end goal, using the right equipment) you would see in First-Person Shooters has been diluted and forgotten. 

In addition, I find that modern players in First-Person Shooters (and in gaming at large) are more interested in spectating their own participation in Battle Royale games. It's like a self-reflexive voyeurism where they are so obsessed with the goal of 'winning' a match, or 'winning' a firefight that they seek to avoid any and all mistakes. It's like an athlete who is too scared to play an opponent for fear they might lose...and that is exactly how most firefights in PubG play out: one player, or group, waits for the other players to make a move. This isn't tension, it's merely a lack of strategic options within the game design.

Simply put, the Battle Royale genre is not designed for player interaction; more-so player participation. It's the difference between building something yourself and having someone else build things for you. 

If you've been around video-games for a decent amount of time, you can intuitively sense that Fortnite, Minecraft and Roblox are not 'building block games' but more-so digital service platforms where players use artistically-devoid assets to 'create their own fun'. There is a space for throwback pixel-drawn, simplistically colored games, so long as they are actually inspired by the past. These new 'creative' platforms (which draw in children in droves, more-so than gaming did in the past) are inspired directly by LEGO products. To end this tangent briefly: LEGO was once about building creative sculptures or toys that children could call their own, that is not the case any longer. The goal of LEGO is artificial scarcity and highly-inflated consumer prices. Most LEGO products are now incredibly expensive and usually rare. Static 'sets' that adults collect and never interact with have become the norm; the most incredible LEGO creations are the ones you go to see at museums...not the simple ones children made at home for themselves and their family members.

Collecting, negative interactions, scarcity and waiting...all these actions are elements that PubG emphasized in its First-Person Shooter design. You collect guns, you counter-intuitively avoid most combat engagements, the player count of the match is forcibly whittled down, and you are usually waiting for others to drop out or become vulnerable. 

This combination of gameplay elements forms the 'gambling' aspect mentioned earlier: how other players move around the map and whether these players interact with others (or your own self) is always somewhat random; random, that is, until the playerbase starts to realise that the win condition of PubG (and all Battle Royale games) is merely to stop playing all-together and observe how the emergent behaviour of each player shapes the match. 

Just like Lego sets, you collect...observe others and meticulously avoid 'interacting' with the match. 

This overall aesthetic of Battle Royale shooting may always be pleasing to anti-social types: you never really have to shoot your gun in player-to-player engagements, you can just wait for others to show their backs and sides to you. The Battle Royale aesthetic is also thrilling to play once or twice in the short-term when you have players on a 'level' playing field where they don't know the map, when there is a large enough playerbase that is still learning the game's mechanics, or when players actually choose to 'hunt' knowing full well they could be the hunted. 

The problem, however, is that the design of PubG -- and all subsequent Battle Royal games --is that most players will constantly take up statue-esque positions away from other players, engaging in too-scared-to-move behaviour patterns. The need for in-game gratification will also cause players to cheat, leave the player-base all-together or become intensely attached to the extremely-slim possibility that they will be the last player standing (usually from no active gameplay of their own). 

Nothing about Battle Royal games encourages the player to eliminate threats with their guns.

It is almost the inverse: you are encouraged to let lesser 'experienced' players eliminate the threats for you. 

There was some room for improvement in this genre when it was bubbling, but I think that time has passed. Earlier influences like Arma 2's Day Z mod, Escape from Tarkov and even the much-earlier released STALKER had various horror-like elements which created real tension and dread. The win condition in all three of those titles is survival at the player's own pace; both the environment and other players can ruin your session. The so-called 'PvE' and 'PvP' experiences were never seperated: they were one and the same.

...On a side note, this lack of separation between PvE and PvP is what really let the Survival game genre boom in the 2010s. All these trends were happening around the same time and its worth exploring why there's a relation between Battle Royale and Survival games...but this review is about PubG!...

The win condition in PubG is quite different from these survival-focused First-Person Shooters: PubG curates survival at the expense of other player's misfortune. The huge caveat of PubG is that the most successful player is the one who avoids real confrontation, observes the match and waits for their accolades. 

It is like hitting a jackpot on the slot machine.

PubG is a PvP game where the second 'P' is slowly effaced from what's supposed to be a multi-player match. This fundamental design aesthetic of the Battle Royale genre to 'remove other players' also removes the chaos, the hand-eye reflexes and general 'fun' atmosphere of First-Person Shooters. There is more to be said about the inclusion of 'operators' with 'special abilities' and pre-designed, cosmetic characters in Battle Royale games like Apex and CoD's Warzone...but this review is already long enough. 

I'd recommend playing PubG at least once, but there's no real substance to this game after one or two matches.

review:
Let's be clear: PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds is not just a game. It's a 100-person therapy session where the couch is a military-grade jeep and the therapist is a stranger named xX_Shotgun_Sniper_Xx who just ended your 20-minute looting spree with a well-aimed pan.

I have a love-hate relationship with PUBG, and by "hate" I mean the kind of pure, unadulterated rage that makes me immediately queue up for another match. This game is a beautiful, janky, heart-pounding masterpiece of chaos.

The PUBG Experience, in Three Acts:

Act I: The Serene Looterella.
You drop onto the island. The sun is shining. Birds are... probably dead, let's be real. But for you, it's a peaceful shopping spree. You're in a secluded compound, humming to yourself as you pick up a nice backpack, a solid vest, and enough energy drinks to power a small festival. You feel like a post-apocalyptic Marie Kondo, organizing your inventory until everything sparks joy. "This sparkly helmet brings me joy. This extended quickdraw mag? Immense joy." This is the calm, the zen, the beautiful lie.

Act II: The Sudden, Deafening Symphony of "WHAT WAS THAT?!"
A sound. A single, distant crack. Your heart instantly becomes a drum solo. You are no longer a person; you are a pair of eyeballs glued to a screen, sweating pure adrenaline. You crawl through a field, believing yourself to be a shadow, a ghost, one with the grass. In reality, you are a very obvious man in a bright yellow shirt commando-crawling through a meadow. Someone from 500 meters away, using a scope you didn't even know existed, politely informs you of this fact. Your screen goes black. The panic was real. The failure is absolute.

Act III: The Unlikely Hero (or the Glorious Pan-ic).
But sometimes... sometimes you are the one who gets the drop. You stumble into a house, and a guy is there, looting a body. You both freeze. In a shared moment of pure, unscripted comedy, you both empty your entire magazines into the wall next to each other. In the ensuing panic, you switch to your trusty frying pan and, with a triumphant BONK, you emerge victorious. You didn't win the match, but you won that moment. And you will tell the story of "The Pan of Destiny" for weeks.

The Verdict:

PUBG is janky. The cars sometimes have a personal grudge against physics. You will get stuck on a tiny pebble. You will be killed by the dreaded "Blue Zone of I Forgot to Pay Attention."

But you know what? I wouldn't have it any other way. It's this beautiful, unpolished chaos that creates the most unforgettable gaming stories. It's the game where a cross-country road trip with three randoms in a UAZ turns into a lifelong (in-game) friendship, only for it all to end in a fiery explosion because Steve saw an airdrop and "just had to go for it."

It's tense, it's ridiculous, and that moment when you're in the final top 10, heart pounding in your ears, is a feeling few other games can replicate.

Final Score: 99/100. (Docked one point for that time I punched a fence for a solid minute instead of vaulting it.)

See you on the battlegrounds. Try not to shoot me. Unless you have a pan. Then all bets are off.

review:
123 hours of my life spent hiding in bushes, crawling through fields like a confused moose, and being one-shot by someone I never saw. And honestly? I'd do it all again, eh.

PUBG is the granddaddy of battle royale - the original chicken dinner before everyone and their nan jumped on the genre. Sure, it's got more jank than a rusty pickup truck in Sudbury, but there's something about the gunplay that just hits different. When you actually win a firefight, it feels earned.

The maps are massive and the variety of strategies keeps things fresh. You can be a hot-drop hero or a loot goblin who spends 20 minutes finding the perfect loadout only to die to the first person you see. Both are valid life choices, apparently.

I've spent more time in the loading screen than I care to admit, and the optimization has been... let's say "inconsistent" over the years. But when the game runs smooth, it runs real smooth.

The squad experience is where this game truly shines. Nothing brings friends together quite like collectively screaming about vehicles, debating whether that's an enemy or a tree, and then getting wiped by a single grenade.

Winner winner chicken dinner, bud.

After 1200+ hours, I can say PUBG is one of the most thrilling and realistic battle royale games out there. The gunplay, recoil, and overall tension in every match are unmatched — when it works, it’s pure adrenaline.

Still, despite the issues, I keep coming back.

review:
Generally a solid game, but the devs are holding it back big time, they should:

1. Focus less on this AI driven company garbage with AI team mates and focus more on improving the game for your actual human players. I don't mind having bots around to help newer players, but these bots shouldn't be the sole focus and AI team mates shouldn't even take up development resources.
2. Improve Anti Cheat
3. Make better balancing changes. You're nerfing DMRs yet MP5 is still allowed to laser beam you from mid range. I dont even need to mention Panzerfaust. C4 was also nerfed to complete uselessness.
4. Optimize the game more, FPS are pretty low and crashes are pretty annoying, especially in ranked
5. Give a better way of obtaining skins. A lot of skins aren't even obtainable anymore and if they are, you have to pray to get them out of a case with a 0.2% chance or lower. Also why do you have to unlock the same gun skin twice in Hideout to be able to craft it? Also the daily rewards are terrible, you get a handful of credits for surviving 240 minutes
6. Remove Rondo. There are periods where they spam us with Rondo for weeks but no region (not even Asia) likes it. 
7. Stop trying to make Esports TPP, it makes it boring to watch. Noone wants to watch pro players camp behind a wall until they see a free kill
8. Just generally listen to the community more and stop making weird changes. This whole AI driven company thing isn't something to proudly announce. Players want high quality, hand crafted content and not AI generated slop. Meanwhile Krafton can't even give us proper personalized offers in "Your Shop", which is something that AI could've actually been used for.

I'll still keep playing this game. I've been playing for roughly 9 months now but sadly I've only seen the devs make changes that were received mostly negative by the community during this time. The game is generally good and free to play so just try it out if you want to, but I wish the devs would actually use the resources and money they gained from it to make ongoing improvements to it, not experiment around with things that nobody asked for.

19年花98买的。。。算是为童年补个票，玩这个比较多一点。

Once again, the devs don't really care about the community. They keep adding updates that nobody asked for. Aimpunch, the DMR nerf, and tons of unimaginable things that aren't even enjoyable anymore.

Look, I want PUBG to rise again. It's a game that's simple but complex. But nerfing everything just because new players can't keep up is diabolically killing this game. Hundreds of hours of playing, ruined by one update.

If they want to create an easy way for new players to keep up, they should develop better matchmaking. For example, I played on another account that was only Lv. 1, and after just five matches, I was suddenly matched against Level 500, God-tier Master Rank players. How is that possible?

FPS games are difficult. I don't care if someone can't hold their recoil, if you can't, don't play an FPS in the first place. Everyone stayed with PUBG because it's rewarding and they enjoyed the complexity. If the devs really don't care about the community, then this is the downfall of a game that I truly enjoy :)

review: Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege stands as one of the most sophisticated tactical shooters ever created, blending intense strategy with beautifully crafted environments and deeply rewarding gameplay. Its maps are more than simple battlegrounds; they are intricate spaces filled with verticality, destruction, and carefully designed angles that make every round feel dynamic and unpredictable. The gunplay is sharp and deliberate, rewarding precision, patience, and composure rather than reckless aggression, while the game’s vast array of utility — from drones and breaching tools to traps and surveillance gadgets — creates endless strategic possibilities where information can be just as powerful as firepower. Although Siege possesses a notoriously steep learning curve, that challenge is precisely what makes it so compelling, as every mistake becomes a lesson and every improvement feels genuinely earned. Few multiplayer games capture tension, teamwork, and tactical depth with such elegance, making Siege an experience that remains both intellectually engaging and endlessly exhilarating.

review:
With Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege now being free-to-play, it’s obviously hard not to recommend a game where you lose nothing by trying it. However, for anyone trying to get into it for anything more than casual quick play or some chill matches with friends, it can feel like a waste of time. The fact that the game is made by Ubisoft should already tell you enough. 
More often than not, you’ll encounter some kind of bug or glitch that makes the game far more annoying than it already is. Not to mention the server issues that can kick you from matches and cost you rank points.

The lobbies are also quite often filled with toxic people with zero mental stability, but you should already be used to that if you’ve played any competitive shooter before.

To give the game some credit, the guns feel amazing, and it’s genuinely rewarding when you finally learn how to control a high-recoil weapon with an ACOG. Another strong point of Siege is the huge variety of operators it has gained throughout its 10+ year lifespan. While it takes a while for new players to learn all of them, I’m confident most players will eventually find a couple of operators on both attack and defense that suit their playstyle really well.

To sum it up, I wouldn’t recommend Siege for highly competitive players. But hey, if you’re just looking for a random game to mess around in with the boys, then go right ahead and give it a try, but with the amount of problems the game has, I still have to put this up as a negative review.

review:
Classic Ubisoft problem of having a genius idea for a game, something that would be easy money for almost any other developer, and steadily making it worse and worse to the point where the game is so fundamentally unrewarding that the community will inevitably become toxic. 

The balancing is horrific, with some characters being more oriented towards gunplay and kills while others are gadget oriented, and a final third class of operators with both. Characters like Aruni and Deimos simply should not exist when you have others like Glaz and Jaeger who, in comparison, are terrible. The game also launches incorrectly about half the time, which is likely a result of ubisoft connect being a useless and obsolete service. Matchmaking is another horrific choice, as it seems that every match comes down to a competition between one or two people with 12 kills and a virtually useless team that struggles to reach a positive kd. You will inevitably fall on either side of this equation, with neither being fun or at all rewarding. The "hidden mmr" system seems like a terrible idea, as you will reach the skill level of a higher tier yet be stuck playing against others who have only picked up the game again midway through the season or players two or sometimes three ranks above you. The only reason this game is still played is its unique concept, as the combination of hero shooter with (semi) realistic fps is something you can't really find elsewhere. This does not prevent Ubisoft from trying, to the best of their ability, to introduce nonsensical, unbalanced, stupid characters with the most awful writing you will find outside of a fanfiction page. With the addition of Snake, there are now four (five if you count Grim) characters whose abilities center around being able to track you around the map, through walls and whatever else there might be. You might wonder: How does Ubisoft balance something like that? They don't. Deimos has two of the best primaries in the game, however when he tracks he then has to take out his secondary revolver with full penetration and a damage score higher than Glaz, who was the original sniper character in the game. This secondary weapon both does more damage and has significantly less recoil than Glaz's primary, as while Deimos must be firing a high caliber, he apparently has the super strength to rapid fire a revolver with no movement. Of course, this is just one example of the horribly designed operators in the game, of which there are a few who utterly decimate the rest of the roster. 

I cannot recommend against this game enough, as while it is fun, it incentivizes playstyles that are against the core concept of the game. Would anyone under "siege" simply step outside of the fortified building to shoot at their attackers? No, that's stupid. Yet it is undoubtedly the best (and depending on the map, the only) way to win defense. The repercussion for doing so is a timer which at the end will reveal that you are outside. So what? The attackers have probably heard the barricade break either way, and even if they weren't aware, the icon showing the defender blends in fairly well with those of teammates under quick review. With the number of offensive trackers in the game, the attacker's parallel to this is to simply shoot defenders through walls, with the only indication that they even know you're there often being simply a message that 'whoever' has just pinged you. Often no counter, no response other than to sprint away, which then reveals your location again via the noise. 

Siege has the innate ability to be fun, but just like For Honor, and Assassin's Creed, and Ghost Recon, and anything else Ubisoft touches, they will inevitably ruin the experience through terrible writing and implementations that are tone-deaf and unbelievably stupid. For example, shield operators, when paired together, are overpowered and outright impossible for some operators to counter. What is the response? Not an in-depth reworking to shields, but instead a future operator whose gadget will target shields. Yippee. In half a season, a single ban will make them worthless and teams can shield rush again. What was the response to this idea? Applause from a bunch of idiots. The gameplay loop of siege is so irritating that it fosters what can only be described as outright hatred between players. You will often see your own teammates harass you throughout the match for anything from failing to clutch a 1v3 to reinforcing the wrong wall. You will be blamed for throwing the game despite being first or second on the team. This is what happens when normal people hoping to unwind are met with constant losses to pixel peaks and obvious scripting. 

I would like to reiterate that the writing is both bad and unnecessary, and whoever thought that the swat team game needed romance between characters should have been fired whenever they first opened their mouth. I would mute operator voicelines, but I then run into the problem of people not screaming when shot or on fire, because I guess the dialogue is just that important. I can't wait to see if Frost and Fenrir get married, I hope they add their heckin sweet babirino as an operator to the game.

Game sucks, Ubisoft sucks, I hope so dearly that they are bankrupted, those who work there never find jobs in the games industry again, and some other company can pick up the IPs.

review:
Rainbow Six Siege is not installed on my computer. It has infested it.

I don’t launch the game anymore. It summons me. I’ll be doing something normal; eating, sleeping, experiencing brief moments of peace, and suddenly I feel it. A pull. A whisper. “Just one match.” It’s never one match. It’s never been one match. Time does not function inside Siege. I have aged.

The operators are not real people. They are archetypes of suffering. Every Ash main moves like they’ve seen the code of the universe and rejected it. Every Doc on my team develops a rare condition where they physically cannot heal anyone except themselves, usually after the round has already been lost. And there is always one player who moves with the confidence of a prophet and the awareness of a Roomba.

I load into a match and immediately feel like I’ve entered a legally questionable experiment where four strangers are assigned to test how quickly I lose trust in humanity. By round two, I no longer recognize myself. By round three, I’m apologizing to inanimate objects.

Gunfights are less about skill and more about who the universe decides to favor in that exact millisecond. I’ve been eliminated by people I never saw, never heard, and I’m not entirely convinced were real. Just a killcam of a man emerging from drywall like he pays rent there.

My teammates communicate exclusively through sighs, silence, or acts of betrayal. The only time they use their microphones is to say something that fundamentally alters my understanding of human intelligence. Meanwhile, the enemy team moves like a synchronized organism. I am not fighting five people. I am fighting a single, hateful entity that has decided I need to be humbled.

At some point, you stop trying to win. You start trying to understand. Why did that happen? Why am I here? Why did my teammate reinforce me into a room like I’m being contained?

And then, against all logic, you clutch. A perfect, impossible moment. A clean entry. A flawless angle. Everything aligns. You feel powerful. Enlightened. Chosen.  Time slows down. You win the round and finally achieve inner peace.

The very next round you are immediately removed from existence by a man who was never on your screen.

I don’t think this is a game. I think this is a long-form psychological study and I am both the subject and the control group.

9/10. I feel different as a person.

review: Where should i start..? its a super nice game with very nice gunplay, a lot of tactical play and a challenging operator system. as you start to play you can just play the worst game mode in the game called quick play - if you progress to level 15, you can play unranked; there you will meet some blatant cheaters already and very unbalanced teams in general. most of the time you get one player who gets 80% of all kills to win the match; its just luck if you get the good player on your team or not. if you progress further to level 50, you can play ranked - and in ranked the BS starts. in my first 10 ranked games, half of my games i played against platinum, emerald, or diamond players - such a frustrating joke. i played only against prefire gods who knew every corner on the map and it took all the fun away for me. then there is the reputation system, which punishes teamkills even if the teamkill is accidental. in 60 hrs i killed 4 teammates (3 of these kills are to my own steam friends) and even with over 300 positive units my reputation is cooked for 100 matches - what a joke. you get even double punishment because not only do you get your reputation cooked, but you will also get redirected fire for the entire match; it feels so disproportionate. why not remove the opportunity to shoot allies and redirect it for everyone every time? long story short, 5/10.

This game is a love-hate relationship.
Every time i play this god forsaken game i want to sit on top of an atomic bomb and light the fuse. However, i still load the game up every day.
I believe ubisoft have created this game as a social experiment to see how addicted one can become to a game even though a ranked match induces much more trauma than the events of the holocaust.

The average ranked teammate lacks basic human functions such as:
- Object permanence
- Critical thinking
- Sense of direction

To anyone looking to buy this game - once you get to 50 hours theres no going back.
You will be addicted and your life will be ruined.

Side effects of playing this game:
- Broken hardware
- No social life
- No future
- 79.5% decrease in exam grades
- depression

But if you win a game and go positive the dopamine levels go through the roof and act as a drug stronger than hard crack cocaine.
Good luck to all trying to quit this game, a task harder than quitting smoking

review:
Hi. Hello. Yeehaw. And hot diggity dog!

I'm sure you can already tell by how excited I am to share my opinion on playing R6. You know, there is this one saying in life that goes: "It can't get any worse, or can it?" - As for somebody that never played R6, this phrase perfectly epitomises my entire experience. I'll get straight to the point.

How incompetent as gaming dev, do you have to be, to put a lvl 30 unranked, in a champions lobby, playing freaking quick match? I am only 50 hours into R6 and I've yet to play ranked, because I am softlocked by lvl 1000 ranked anxiety riddled parasocial degenerates, who refuse to play different gamemodes, literally preventing me from winning games and hitting lvl 50.

Holy bonkers, is he above me? Under me? Or inside of me? Where is what coming from? The audio in this game greatly enables my schizophrenia. I feel like a clueless buffoon most of the time, trying to pre-fire angles, to pretend like I know what I'm doing, when I'm clearly lost.

Callouts? What are those? Ah, my fault! "He's at that window!", "The one in the corridor!", "The one on third floor!", "The one facing the piano!" Is this why no one ever talks? Including me? Compare this to CS2 callouts: "Yo, they're coming B!", "One is mid!", "Two out A!" Hear the difference?

Blitz shield, Ying clusters, Ela mines, Zofia KS7, Echo drone, Fenrir Dreadmine, Twitch Drone. Yeah, if I'm not wrong, there are approximately 10 ways, if not more, to blind/stun your opponents. Gotta make sure I don't get a seizure at this point. After loading up R6 a couple of times now, I realised why they give you an epilepsy warning at the start of the game.

I may or may not have picked the absolute worst time to try out R6. The knowledge gap between a beginner and a "Shaiiko wannabe" is as wide as the distance between earth and proxima centauri b. Cannot imagine a newbie without any fps experience downloading Rainbow 6 Siege today, playing till lvl 50 and going: "Yeah, that went well."

I'm close to being immune to toxicity, but having my teammate blow my brains out after having accidentally destroyed his drone by a friction of my frag grenade that was used to break down the front entrance of a building, made me think of atleast 11 possible slurs. Not saying I used any, since I do respect the reputation system, which does benefit me with some of that GOLD GOLD GOLD action.

I did enjoy playing the 1v1 gamemode, since there is no one to blame but yourself. Makes you have less of an ego, when you do get outclassed. Hope valve steals that idea and puts it into cs2. Also recently found out that getting killed through walls in this game is a really common occurrence. 

To this day, I feel like most of the "veterans" themselves cannot even comprehend some of the things that do happen in R6. Usually, watching esports helps me understand new video games a lot better. There is a major coming up, in Salt Lake City. Can't wait for that. It will probably be my first and last tournament I watch from R6.

Rainbow Six Siege is basically Pokémon.

When it started, it was simple. You had like eight monster types. You counter a reinforced wall with the guy carrying thermite. You counter bullets with the guy holding a big shield. Easy.

Now? Now we are in the era of OVERLORD MAX DARK STEEL POISON TYPE. You are sitting there screaming because someone just perfectly countered your FLYING DIABETIC WATER BUG TYPE setup... and you have absolutely no idea how they knew.

It is literally a game with homework attached. You can't just log in and shoot; you have to study….

But here is the absolute worst part. When you forget to do that homework... it isn't the teacher who gets mad at you. It is the entire class. And they do not just get a little annoyed. The community reacts like your one missed assignment means the whole class now has to do wind sprints... uphill... in the pouring rain... until you either magically finish the homework right then and there or just leave the school because why would you even be here disgracing us without doing the homework.

In example: You put up a barricade in the wrong doorway or pick an operator that doesn't perfectly fit the current meta... and suddenly four strangers are screaming in your headset like you just committed an international war crime. They start giving testimony to the lobby like they are on trial at the Hague; building a legal case for why you specifically deserve to go to federal prison for picking the wrong character.

There is no casual mode anymore. You either log in with a PhD in Siege... or you are the kid making everyone else run laps.

I’ve never played LoL so my opinion on toxic communities could be one-upped…but we will save that for the debate of “whats worse: a flat tire on the freeway or strep throat?”

- Just a Paul Review

review:
This game is basically what happens if a SWAT simulator, a chess match, and a toxic group chat had a baby… and then gave that baby a gun and anxiety.

At its core, Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege is NOT a shooter—it’s a stress simulator where drywall is your worst enemy. You don’t walk through doors, you question their entire existence. Walls? Optional. Floors? Temporary. Your sanity? Gone by round 3.

🧠 Gameplay (aka “overthinking: the game”)

You don’t just aim and shoot—you:

drone like you’re in the FBI
argue with your team about which wall to reinforce
die instantly because someone made a tiny hole in a wall 2 minutes ago

It rewards big brain plays over raw aim, which is cool… until you get one-tapped by a dude named “xX_DoorCamper_Xx” who hasn’t moved since 2017. The game is praised for this tactical depth and teamwork focus, but it can also feel brutally punishing

📚 Learning curve (aka “welcome to hell”)

New players:

don’t know the maps
don’t know operators
don’t know why they died

Veterans:

still don’t know what just happened

Even Reddit sums it up perfectly:

“The game sucks but you’ll love it”

And honestly? That’s the most accurate review ever written.

💸 Monetization (aka “Ubisoft wants your lunch money”)
You bought the game? Cool.
Now grind 25 hours for one operator… or pay up.

People have been side-eyeing the grind and microtransactions for YEARS, with complaints about slow progression and constant monetization pressure

☠️ Community (aka “psychological warfare”)

You will experience:

teammates yelling slurs over voice chat
getting team-killed because you picked the “wrong” operator
ranked games that feel like hostage negotiations

Also… cheating and toxicity? Yeah, still a thing, and players regularly complain about it, especially at higher ranks

🔥 The good (because somehow there is some)
When it clicks? It’s one of the most intense FPS experiences ever
Winning with a coordinated team feels like you just completed a heist
Every round feels like life or death because… it kinda is (no respawns)
🧾 Final verdict

Rainbow Six Siege is:

40% tactical genius
30% anxiety
20% getting yelled at
10% wondering why you still play

It’s the kind of game you uninstall… then reinstall… then complain about… then queue again.

Rating:
“Why am I still playing this?” / 10

review:
Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege
Rating: Recommended

If you’re looking for a relaxing tactical shooter to play after work while you listen to a podcast, keep walking. Rainbow Six Siege isn't just a game; it’s a 15-minute panic attack that you voluntarily sign up for. It is the only game where the sound of a single Velcro strap ripping or a faint "thud" through a ceiling can make your heart rate jump to 120 BPM.

The Experience
The magic (and the misery) of Siege lies in the silence. * The Setup: You’ve spent the prep phase reinforcing walls, hiding gadgets, and checking cams. Then, the round starts, and for 45 seconds, there is nothing but the ambient hum of the map.

The Tension: You’re holding an angle through a hole the size of a coin. You know they're coming. You hear a floorboard creak. Your palms are sweating. You’re holding your breath in real life because you’re convinced the enemy can hear you breathe through your headset.

The Chaos: One explosion. That’s all it takes. The wall behind you vanishes, flashbangs turn your screen white, and suddenly you’re in a 1v3 situation with 20 seconds on the clock.

The 1vX Clutch
There is no "high" in gaming quite like being the last person alive on your team. Every teammate is watching your POV. The pressure is suffocating. When you finally pull off that flick shot or outsmart the last attacker with a well-placed C4, the adrenaline dump is so intense you’ll need to stare at a wall for five minutes after the match just to recalibrate.

The Verdict
Siege is a toxic, beautiful, frustrating, and brilliant mess. It has a learning curve that looks like a vertical cliff face, and the community will judge your every move. But for those moments where your heart is pounding against your ribs and you manage to survive by the skin of your teeth?

There is absolutely nothing else like it.

Pros:

Deepest tactical gameplay on the market.

Destruction physics that actually matter.

Unmatched tension.

Cons:

Might actually shorten your lifespan due to stress.

The learning curve is brutal.

You will hear "Cope" or "L" at least once an hour.

10/10 — I hate this game. I’m queuing for another match right now.

review:
Ultimate crème brûlée
Ingredients:
2 cartons double cream
1 large (284ml) plus 1 small (142ml)
100ml whole milk
1 vanilla pod
5 egg yolks
50g golden caster sugar
plus extra for the topping

step 1
Heat the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4. Sit four 175ml ramekins in a deep roasting tin at least 7.5cm deep (or a large deep cake tin), one that will enable a baking tray to sit well above the ramekins when laid across the top of the tin.

step 2
Pour the large and small cartons of double cream into a medium pan with the milk.

step 3
Lay the vanilla pod on a board and slice lengthways through the middle with a sharp knife to split it in two. Use the tip of the knife to scrape out all the tiny seeds into the cream mixture. Drop the vanilla pod in as well, and set aside.

step 4
Put the egg yolks and sugar in a mixing bowl and whisk for 1 min with an electric hand whisk until paler in colour and a bit fluffy.

step 5
Put the pan with the cream on a medium heat and bring almost to the boil. As soon as you see bubbles appear round the edge, take the pan off the heat.

step 6
Pour the hot cream into the beaten egg yolks, stirring with a wire whisk as you do so, and scraping out the seeds from the pan.

step 7
Set a fine sieve over a large wide jug or bowl and pour the hot mixture through to strain it, encouraging any stray vanilla seeds through at the end.

step 8
Using a big spoon, scoop off all the pale foam that is sitting on the top of the liquid (this will be several spoonfuls) and discard. Give the mixture a stir.

step 9
Pour in enough hot water (from the tap) into the roasting tin to come about 1.5cm up the sides of the ramekins. Pour the hot cream into the ramekins so you fill them up right to the top – it’s easier to spoon in the last little bit.

step 10
Put them in the oven and lay a baking sheet over the top of the tin so it sits well above the ramekins and completely covers them, but not the whole tin, leaving a small gap at one side to allow air to circulate.

step 11
Bake for 30-35 mins until the mixture is softly set. To check, gently sway the roasting tin and if the crème brûlées are ready, they will wobble a bit like a jelly in the middle. Don’t let them get too firm.

step 12
Lift the ramekins out of the roasting tin with oven gloves and set them on a wire rack to cool for a couple of minutes only, then put in the fridge to cool completely. This can be done overnight without affecting the texture.

step 13
When ready to serve, wipe round the top edge of the dishes, sprinkle 1½ tsp of caster sugar over each ramekin and spread it out with the back of a spoon to completely cover.

step 14
Spray with a little water using a fine spray (the sort you buy in a craft shop) to just dampen the sugar – then use a blow torch to caramelise it. Hold the flame just above the sugar and keep moving it round and round until caramelised. Serve when the brûlée is firm, or within an hour or two.

Nutrients:

fat
64g
saturates
38g
carbs
20g
sugars
20g
fibre
0g
protein
6g
salt
0.12g

review:
Probably one of the hardest games to get into if you are new.

That being said, Mooda cheated on his older account, got banned due to signing in into his old account and Ubisoft easily detected the same PC via same login and banned his new account. L Mooda, tho I believe he stopped cheating after he got caught long ago on his old account.

Now about the game:

It's entirely an AIM duel, and allot of it has to do with Map knowledge and the type of agent you play & your opponent/s play.

In most basic terms, If you have really good aim and map knowledge, you may get to Champion.
If you have really good aim, map knowledge, and know all your defender/attack agents and how they work and how to play agains't them or how to play with them, you may just go Pro if you care enough.

Other than that, The game feels fun to play, I haven't played the game enough yet to know just how many cheaters there are, tho I do see smurfs. And also the game has it's problems with False Bans, which kinda sucks, since many streamers/pros that played the game got falsely banned and then the ban get's revoked. Should that be a thing in any game? No. So that sucks.

If you wanna try out the game, expect a hard learning curve, you will die allot, you might hit your table for losing to something so stupid (a trap, knife) etc; But eventually if you play enough and learn the maps, aim better, learn the agents, you will get better and you will have "fun".

review:
This game is broken, just like it made my mental health. I used to play a bunch on console, so I've spent well over 1,000 hours in this game. Constant bugs; rampant cheats; developers that care more about money than player base. This game had ALL the recipes for failure, and yet it's still alive. That's because from a gameplay standpoint, it really is one of a kind. When you start playing and learn a little bit about the game, it feels magical and it feels very good to hit your peak rank and keep climbing; until you realize that ranked 2.0 is a thing and your rank means but naught.
Back to the mental health thing, this game is SO bad for you. It's more addictive than heroin, and I would argue it's much more harmful. I've had plenty of fights with friends over a damn VIDEO GAME, and it makes me mad enough to rub off on the people around me. Playing is fun, and still somehow feels like torture.
Anyways I digress, and I'm sad to say it, but after 4 years, I officially quit Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege :(.

review:
The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt — A World That Refuses to Let You Go

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | 30+ Hours In — And I'm Just Getting Started
Thirty hours. That's how long I've sunk into The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, and if the achievement counter is anything to go by (13 out of 78 — yes, I checked), I've barely scratched the surface. That alone tells you everything about the sheer scale of this game.
From the moment you step into the boots of Geralt of Rivia, the world doesn't just open up — it breathes. The wind tears through golden wheat fields, NPCs argue in taverns without waiting for you to listen, and every notice board leads you down a rabbit hole you didn't see coming.
The quests here aren't filler. Even the so-called "side" content carries emotional weight that most games never achieve in their main storylines. I've lost track of how many times I paused just to absorb the writing.
Combat clicks satisfyingly once you stop button-mashing and start reading your enemies. Signs, oils, timing — it rewards patience.
The verdict? Thirty hours feels like a prologue. With 65 achievements still locked and entire regions left unexplored, this game isn't something you finish — it's something you live in for a while.
Recommended without hesitation.

review:
I went into The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt knowing basically nothing about the books or the lore and somehow came out feeling like I could write a 50 page essay on it (not that I will, don’t worry). This game has officially become both my new and old favorite open-world action RPG at the same time, which doesn’t make sense, but neither does half the stuff Geralt deals with, so it fits.

First off, how does a game that’s over 10 years old still look this good? The world is ridiculously detailed, my pc is average but still it was capable of giving me high quality experience on 1440p high/very high (80fps in game). Also it’s one of those rare games where exploring doesn’t feel like a chore but more like, “oh cool, what weird cursed wierd ass thing am I about to find now?”

The combat feels great. It’s not the absolute best ever made, but it’s satisfying enough that you actually want to fight things instead of running past them. Plus, you get to spin around with swords like a professional monster blender, which never gets old.

And then there’s Gwent. Honestly, I came here to hunt monsters and ended up addicted to a card game (I even played it irl). At some point I was less concerned about saving the world and more focused on building the ultimate Gwent deck.

The story and lore was incredible. What I really liked is that the game doesn’t just dump everything on you, it kind of makes you work for it. The lore is like a puzzle that slowly comes together while you’re playing. You’ll see events happening, meet characters, and only later realize how important they actually were. Like with Uma. at first you’re just like “what is this little gremlin?” and later it hits you: oh. OH. That’s actually huge. If you know, you know.

What really ties all of this together is the dialogue. This game basically runs on conversations, and somehow they never feel boring. Every choice you make in dialogue can actually matter. whether it’s for the lore, side quests, relationships, or even the ending. Sometimes you think you’re just picking a random line, and 10 hours later the game hits you with the consequences (foreshadowing). Characters like Keira Metz are a perfect example. one conversation can completely change her fate depending on what you say. It makes you actually care about what you pick instead of just skipping through lines like in other games.

Even the side quests are heavily driven by dialogue, and that’s what makes them so good. They’re not just “go here, kill that” they feel like real stories with real outcomes and the fact that these choices can affect the ending of the entire game is kind of insane for a 2015 game that is still on top in 2026. You’re not just playing the story. you’re kinda shaping it, sometimes without even realizing it.

Even without knowing anything beforehand, the game does an amazing job of teaching you about its world naturally. By the end, I felt connected to the characters and understood what was going on without ever needing to Google stuff every five minutes.

Geralt himself was an absolute legend. Although I’m not sure if it’s just me, but the man runs a little… funny. Still, he somehow has enough charisma (or “aura” in my term) to rizz up basically everyone he meets (and sometimes have "fun" (; with them), which is impressive considering his main hobbies are fighting monsters and being emotionally unavailable.

Overall, The Witcher 3 is one of those games that completely pulls you in and doesn’t let go. It’s funny, dark, weird, emotional, and occasionally makes you question your life and why you’re playing cards in the middle of a war, but that’s part of the charm.

100/10, would ignore the main quest again to play Gwent. WHATEVER YOU DO, DO NOT TAKE CIRI TO EMHYR JUST TRUST ME FOR ONCE!!!!11!!1!!1.

[h1]The Witcher 3 Review - 65 Hours Later[/h1]

[i]"Glad I played it 10 years later, because if I played it earlier, my other games would be boring."[/i]

[b]What impressed me the most:[/b]
[list]
[*]Side quests that are better written than some full games
[*]Choices that actually feel meaningful instead of fake "illusion of choice" nonsense
[*]World-building that feels alive, dirty, tragic, and believable
[*]Characters that stay in your head long after the quest ends
[*]Soundtrack that quietly manipulates your soul like a medieval emotional support wizard
[/list]

Combat has flaws, movement can feel clunky at times, and Roach occasionally operates using advanced quantum physics, but somehow none of that ruined the experience.

This game didn’t feel like "content."
It felt like an adventure.

[b]Score:[/b] 9.5/10

review:
The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt

In my opinion

Criteria	        Score	Note
Story	        10/10	Even the smallest side quests feel like main story content.
Atmosphere	10/10	A living, breathing world that feels truly lived-in.
Gameplay	8.5/10	Combat can feel a bit repetitive after 30+ hours.
Music	        10/10	The soundtrack is a masterpiece of Slavic folk-metal.
Value/Content	10/10	DLCs HoS & BaW are better than most full games.
OVERALL SCORE: 9.7 / 10

Wind's howling
The Witcher 3 Wild Hunt isn't just an RPG  it’s a benchmark for storytelling in the gaming industry. Geralt of Rivia’s journey takes you through political intrigue, monster hunting and deeply emotional family drama all while making you feel the weight of every gray decision you make.

The Pros
Incredible Depth: Even a simple notice board contract can turn into a 2-hour epic with a moral twist you didn't see coming.
Memorable Characters: From Yennefer and my dear Triss to the Bloody Baron, every character has depth, flaws, and a soul.
Gwent: You will likely spend 20 hours ignoring the apocalypse just to collect rare cards and play a "round of Gwent."
Art Direction: Even years later, watching the sunset over the fields of Velen or the peaks of Skellige is breathtaking

The Cons
Inventory management and looting can feel a bit cluttered.
Roach still has a mind of her own and occasionally defies the laws of physics.

review:
The core gameplay is simple but rich. You and your opponent play over up to three rounds, each trying to have the higher total strength at the end of a round. First to win two rounds takes the match. You build a deck of up to ten cards plus a leader, then take turns playing cards from your hand onto one of three rows: melee, ranged, or siege. The strategic layer comes from special cards, row effects, and clever synergies that force you to think several turns ahead.
There are five distinct factions, each with its own personality and optimal playstyle. Northern Realms rewards tight combos and morale boosts. Nilfgaard excels at control, spying, and disrupting the opponent. Scoia'tael is flexible and punishes overcommitment to single rows. Monsters thrive on weather effects and resilient units that keep coming back. Skellige brings aggressive discard and resurrection mechanics that can swing games dramatically.
Weather cards can blanket entire rows and cripple your opponent. Scorch destroys the strongest units on the board. Spies let you play to the enemy side while drawing extra cards. Decoys, horns, tight bonds, and leader abilities all create constant meaningful decisions. Every match feels different depending on the decks and how the draws go.
Deck building is satisfying and rewarding. You can chase pure power stacks, weather control, spy disruption, or resurrection combos. Finding and adding strong cards feels great, and every faction has multiple viable archetypes. The balance is solid overall, with interesting counters between factions.
The presentation is fantastic. Every card has beautiful artwork, and the audio feedback when playing cards is crisp and immersive.

The game also apparently features a bonus RPG on the side, where you have to save some girl from this anorexic ghost elf or something. IDK, I haven't gotten there yet

review:
phenomenal. even for an 11-year-old game, it is still an incredible experience. this is my first exposure to the Witcher IP and it's left an unforgettable mark on me. the characters are very compelling, with dialogue and interactions that are enjoyable, funny, and memorable.  the story is very well-written, the action is thrilling, the romance and comedy is very fun and enjoyable, and the game does a good job of carrying you through the story as a standalone title, as in that even with no prior knowledge to the lore of the previous games or the books, you can easily follow the story with minimal confusion. exposition is also very well done, in no sequence did i ever feel like a conversation happened just for the sake of telling the player this and that and it informed the players of the happenings in the world in a natural way, one particular sequence i liked a lot was the conversation with ambassador Var Attre in Yennefer's room after the audience with Emhyr, it was a very fluid way of telling newcomers to the franchise of where the politics of the continent sat and it did so through a conversation that would make sense to exist in the dialogue.

while i do have some bones to pick with the gameplay (ahem, horse handling), they're very minor setbacks to an otherwise timeless classic. although feeling slightly dated, the combat is still very satisfying and intuitive, and difficulty is easily customisable, you can make it as hard or easy as you want the game to be. 

anybody with any sort of preference over a video game will enjoy it regardless. whether you prefer action packed gameplay or slow, deliberate, storytelling, the game allows any and all kinds of players to experience the game how they see fit. and with it now being 11-years-old and frequently going on sale, the cheap price point for such an amazing piece of art is a blessing. overall it's a deserved GotY and a timeless classic, massive replayability and heaps of quality side content. its a one size fits all kind of game, a jack of all trades, but arguably even a master of some.

review:
Three playthroughs and Roach still can't figure out how roofs work.

I first saw The Witcher 3 on a streamer's channel back in 2016. I'd never even heard of the series before that. Something about it caught my eye, so I decided to start from the very beginning - The Witcher 1. Played through the first game, then the second, and eventually made my way to the third. And what a journey it was.

I played through the game twice before I even bought it on Steam. Eventually I decided to buy it here, and I've now finished it a third time. Honestly, I'm already thinking about a fourth playthrough. The atmosphere in this game is just something else - it pulls you in and doesn't let go.

Every game in the trilogy has incredible soundtracks, but the first and third games really stand out. "River of Life" from the first game is beautiful, "A Quiet Corner" from the second sets the perfect mood, and the third game has some of my all-time favorites - "Kaer Morhen" and "A Story You Won't Believe," which always plays during Gwent matches and certain quests. Those tracks alone make you want to just stop and soak it all in.

Speaking of Gwent - it became one of my favorite games on its own. I'd sit there playing round after round.

The DLCs deserve a special mention. Blood and Wine is something else entirely. After spending so much time in the cold, harsh North, stepping into Toussaint feels like a breath of fresh air. The colors, the vineyards, the whole vibe - it's stunning. I keep coming back to it again and again. Hearts of Stone is great too - packed with interesting quests.

If you haven't played this yet, do yourself a favor and give it a shot. Start from the first game if you can. It's worth every minute.

review:
Man what a gem from the recent past. I played this years ago in my console days and it is still up there in quality. 

 I want to talk about two things, combat and narrative. 

 If you don't engage in the magic and alchemy systems you'll probably think that combat is just bad. This is not Dark  Souls or Devil May Cry, it's about planning, using the right tools for situation, etc.
 Don't go in with the wrong expectations. 

 The narrative is where the game really shines. It's presented well, engaging and actually full of difficult choices. 
 Oftentimes it's about choosing the lesser evil. Do you trust the trapped spirit to save those children? If it does, what else might happen?
 Sometimes you wouldn't be able to predict the potential outcome at all, but not in a bs kinda way. You can only act in good faith and hope for the best, just like irl. 

 One time I couldn't bear the consequences and actually reloaded the game. Guess what, the outcome of the other choice was even worse for me, I was "right" all along. It's that kind of game. 

 Choices made early in the playthrough can have consequences way later. Sometimes it's just an additional scene for worldbuilding, other times it has direct impact on your quest flow. 

 Considering how cheap it is on sale I'd recommend it to everyone even remotely interested.
 P.S. don't worry about the dlcs, the game is massive as is.

review:
Amazing breathe of content, even if repetitive at times, the story alone and the amount of character/depth put into characters throughout all the years made this game an absolutely worthwhile experience. I myself still haven't finished it to its fullest but from what I've experienced thus far does motivate me to come back to play more.

The artist depth/work put into this if you have a decent enough system to run it on higher settings you'll love what you see, the eye candy is all there. It really does look good just its also very much demanding. 

If DX12 works for you and you have a decent enough system go for it, otherwise DX11 is still very much an option for the game if you looking in the game properties on steam.

I originally ran the with DX12 Medium/High with Upscaling with a Titan XP 50-62 fps most times in the high 50's and under certain situations did it drop to low 50's.

Now i'm still tinkering with settings but I got a mostly stable experience although I am using DLSS. VRAM heavy this game is 24GB vram is a must for running max* settings with ray tracing in this.
